chunks.text:
The red vehicle (A) will turn backwards in a horseshoe shape, so the white vehicle and the gray
vehicle (B + C) have right of way. The white vehicle enters the intersection first - it is the first to be
allowed to pass. 152
Closed Back Rotation:
When entering a closed horseshoe curve, give way to all vehicles approaching the turn and move
to lane one when the road is clear of vehicles. Do not use parking lanes or sidewalks for horseshoe
turns. Open Back Rotation:
When entering an open horseshoe curve, give way to all vehicles approaching the curve and move
to the second lane when the road is clear of vehicles to allow vehicles coming from the junction to
enter. 153
Protected reverse rotation:
General rules of conduct at intersections:
•
Drivers should approach the intersection cautiously and reduce the speed in a way that
allows them to stop before reaching the crossing if other road users have priority to cross. •
If drivers cannot cross an intersection, they must stop before reaching the crossing, even if
they have right-of-way or with a green light. If there is danger of the driver getting stuck at
the intersection (due to slow or stopped traffic), he must avoid entering the intersection from
the start. 154
Importance of priority rules:
The priority rules are intended to make clear when drivers must give way to other road users. These rules play a vital role in ensuring safety on the roads of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. Compliance with these rules can contribute not only to ensuring the safety of traffic, but also to
reducing traffic accidents and minimizing their tragic consequences. It is, therefore, important for all drivers to be aware of these rules in order to fully comply with them
whenever and wherever necessary without any hesitation.

chunks.text:
3. Railway crossings
158
Railway vehicles have priority over other vehicles.
Drivers must immediately evacuate the railway tracks when the railway vehicle approaches.
Securing railway crossings and advance warning.
•
Barriers.
•
Guard - drivers of vehicles must abide by the
location of the barriers or guard.
•
Traffic lights: Red or yellow - indicates that the
train is approaching. Vehicle drivers must. Do not
cross the railway line.
•
Bell signals - an audible warning of an approaching
train. Vehicle drivers should not cross the railway
line.
The railway lane may only be crossed when the sign indicates that it is safe and the driver must make
sure before that there is no train approaching.
If traffic stops or slows down and the prospect of stopping at a railway lane becomes dangerous, the
driver must not enter the railway crossing.
159
Stop at a railway crossing:
Vehicle drivers must stop before the X signal.
•
When the train approaches.
•
When there is a red or yellow light signal.
•
When the gates are moving or when they are closed.
•
When a guard signals to stop.
•
If the traffic and crosswalks do not allow the crossing.
At railway crossings or railway tracks it is prohibited:
•
Stop on rails.
•
Parking of vehicles on the railways.
•
Driving along the rails.
Behave when crossing.
Wait until the gates are fully opened and the red light is
off before entering the railway crossing.
160
Driving speed/driving one by one
161
162

Barriers.
•
Guard - drivers of vehicles must abide by the
location of the barriers or guard.
•
Traffic lights: Red or yellow - indicates that the
train is approaching. Vehicle drivers must. Do not
cross the railway line.
•
Bell signals - an audible warning of an approaching
train. Vehicle drivers should not cross the railway
line.
The railway lane may only be crossed when the sign indicates that it is safe and the driver must make
sure before that there is no train approaching.
If traffic stops or slows down and the prospect of stopping at a railway lane becomes dangerous, the
driver must not enter the railway crossing.
159
Stop at a railway crossing:
Vehicle drivers must stop before the X signal.
•
When the train approaches.
•
When there is a red or yellow light signal.
•
When the gates are moving or when they are closed.
•
When a guard signals to stop.
•
If the traffic and crosswalks do not allow the crossing.
At railway crossings or railway tracks it is prohibited:
•
Stop on rails.
•
Parking of vehicles on the railways.
•
Driving along the rails.
Behave when crossing.
Wait until the gates are fully opened and the red light is
off before entering the railway crossing.
160
Driving speed/driving one by one
161
162

chunks.text:
1. General Speed Limiters:
Small vehicles:
If there is no indication of another speed limiter, drivers of small vehicles must not exceed the next
speed limit.
•
Sub-roads
according to the speed specified for the road.
•
The highways
according to the speed specified for the road.
163
Large vehicles.
If there is no indication of another speed limiter, drivers of large vehicles must not exceed the next
speed limit.
•
City roads within cities
50 km/h maximum.
•
Outside the city limits/open borders
depending on the speed of the road.
164
Traffic lights can also indicate other speed limits.
There is also a traffic light indicating the end of the speed limit:
Remember
if there is no other speed limiter, you must
stick to the speed limiter after this sign.
Remember
Don’t feel compelled to drive fast, as these
signs just show you the limit!
165
Situations in which the speed must be reduced below the minimum speed
•
At night.
•
When strong sunlight appears.
166
Situations in which the speed must be reduced below the minimum speed
•
During fog
•
During rain
•
During sandstorms as well as according to the road conditions
167
An exception:
Emergency vehicles can exceed the speed limit, but this should not endanger the safety of others.
Remember
Always be aware of what is happening
around you while driving so that you can
allow emergency vehicles to pass easily.
168
Traffic lights can also indicate the minimum speed limit.
Minimum speed End of minimum speed
•
These signals indicate to the driver not to drive at a speed slower than the minimum speed.
•
(Illegible): The driver can drive by a car if required by a situation or safety measures.
169

chunks.text:
2. Criteria for choosing driving speed
The driver must always adjust his speed according to the general conditions and road conditions,
regardless of the maximum permissible speed. He must ensure his safety and the safety of those with him in the vehicle and the safety of other road
users at all times. He must also adapt the vehicle’s speed to be able to stop in time in the event of a stop sign or barrier. 170
You should reduce your driving speed:
•
When approaching intersections or any kind of crossings
•
Before the curves
•
When crossing bridges
•
When crossing railway crossings
•
When driving through tunnels
•
When the level of vision is low
•
On slippery roads (the presence of water, sand, snow, ...)
171
How fast do you think you can go in these specific situations? After this signal? In this curve? In this case? On this road? 172
Remember
there is no single correct answer to any of the cases presented above, as
the images merely provide a catalyst for discussion in order to illustrate the
following:
You must evaluate each case individually. If you feel unsure about one of these situations, it is best to drive a little
slower. For your notes:
173
To drive at a safe speed, you must consider the following:
•
traffic movement
•
The possibility of obstacles on the road or other road users
•
Road conditions
•
The condition of your vehicle must be known. Remember
Be careful not to endanger people or
animals, and always make sure that you can
stop in time!

chunks.text:
174
Other examples where it is important to reduce speed:
•
If the road is not a highway
•
In the winding places of the road
•
During busy traffic
•
When approaching bumps
•
When approaching or passing a group of pedestrians
•
When approaching or passing animals, especially when the animals show signs of anxiety
and tension,
It is prohibited to race vehicles on public roads inside or outside the city limits! 175
Speed kills! Remember
the driver was clearly driving his vehicle very
fast. 176
Drifting is an illegal form of road racing
Drifting is a form of racing in which vehicles are driven at extremely high speeds of about 160 to 260
km per hour on wide highways. At the same time, the vehicle swayed to the left and right, and in this
case, drivers often drive their vehicles close to traffic, barriers, and dangerously close to onlookers
watching from the sides of the road without any protection. Be aware that many horrific traffic
accidents occur as a result of drifting. 177
A vivid example of how fast you drive
doesn’t have to be a race to put your life at risk. Driving at a speed of no more than 10 km per hour above the
permissible limit and not wearing seat belts is sufficient to
cause serious injury. Imagine:
You are traveling at a speed of 50 km/h and suddenly a truck
comes out of the intersection in front of you, you are afraid and
you apply the brakes hard and you can stop in time. Now imagine the following:
You are traveling at 60 km/h (10 km/h faster than in the first
case) and suddenly the truck comes out of the fork in front of
you again, you get scared again, and you hit the brakes hard
but this time you can’t stop in time. The truck will hit 40 km/h in the second example!

chunks.text:
3. Safety distance
Drivers who are driving behind other vehicles or preparing to pass other vehicles in front of them
must maintain a sufficient distance to avoid an accident.
181
Safety distance
If the driver of the front vehicle suddenly slows down or stops due to an emergency, the driver in the
rear vehicle must be able to respond in time to stop his vehicle safely.
Reasons for increasing the safety distance
•
Bad road conditions (e.g. wet or sandy road)
•
Driving behind vehicles on a one-lane road (e.g. motorcycles )
•
Driving a heavily loaded vehicle
182
The dangers of driving behind other vehicles without leaving a good distance
Remember
Driving without leaving a safe distance increases
the possibility of hitting vehicles in front of you.
Why? Because you don’t have enough time
to stop when the driver of the vehicle in front
applies the brakes
183

chunks.text:
4. Correct behavior
This driver is driving his vehicle without keeping a safe distance.
The minimum distance to drive behind another vehicle is two seconds!
184
The two-second rule (one thousand one thousand two) is two seconds
You should regularly check the following distance, especially when driving at higher speeds behind
other vehicles (e.g. on the highway or the open road).
Step 1:
Look for a reference point such as a traffic light or a tree, a mast close to the road or you can look for
a sign or shadow on the road.
Step 2:
Start after seconds when the back of the vehicle in front of you is at that reference point.
Step 3
Keep counting until the front of your vehicle reaches the reference point. You must count at least two
seconds; otherwise, the distance between you and the vehicle in front is not safe.
Step 1 Step 2 Step 3
185
Remember
you should leave a larger distance in case of
changing weather conditions, such as storms,
dust, rain, and poor road conditions.
186
Importance of Maintaining Large Relay Distances:
By applying the driving style and looking forward at the same time, you will find that you have more
time to respond to situations and emerging dangers. What is required is less pressure on the gas
pedal and a greater percentage of safety and security.

chunks.text:
5. Economic leadership
One of the key components of economic leadership is a forward-looking leadership style. This means taking your foot off the gas pedal and making the car decelerate without depressing the
brake pedal, e.g. a red traffic light so no fuel is used while approaching the traffic light and this also
reduces brake wear. The thing to avoid is to stop the car unnecessarily, as starting it again requires a lot of energy. But if
it must be stopped and the car has an automatic start/stop function, then you can stop to save fuel,
as this feature turns off the engine when the car is stopped and the engine restarts automatically as
soon as you take your foot off the brake pedal. If you drive the car without using the start/stop function, you can save fuel by switching off the
engine if the idle time is longer than 20 seconds. 187
Other things to keep in mind when trying to save fuel:
•
Speed: Speed between 70 to 90 km/h saves fuel, so you should avoid driving at any speed
exceeding 100 km/h when trying to save fuel
•
Acceleration: Avoid strong acceleration
•
Constant speed: Try to drive at a constant speed by using cruise control as much as possible
•
Excess weight shedding
•
Tire pressure: Check tire pressure regularly
•
Rear window heating: It uses about 0.7 liters of fuel per 100 km, and it should only be used
if the rear window is foggy, and it must be ensured that it has not been started by mistake. •
Air conditioner: Never set it to too low, in addition to operating the recirculation air button,
and always close the windows when the air conditioner is on.

chunks.section_title: Driving near vehicles and stationary objects
chunks.text:
1. Driving near vehicles and stationary objects
Driving near stationary objects such as parked vehicles, (groups) of people, or (herds of animals). 191
Do not pass:
•
If other road users are obstructed or endangered
•
In the event of causing damage to fixed objects
•
When people exit or enter railway vehicles on one side of the road
192
How to drive near fixed vehicles/obstacles:
Remember
Do you remember how to change course? This is similar to that! 193
How to drive near fixed vehicles/obstacles:
If there is an obstacle in your path:
Look: What is this? What happened? Evaluate: Is there a movement coming? Is there enough space for passage? Decide: Decide to either pass or stop. Act: As per your decision: Move (start) or stop
Driving near the obstacle:
Mirrors: Check your interior and left side mirrors to see vehicles behind you (may be about to
overtake or walk right behind you)
Signal: Give a left signal
Shoulder: Check the blind spot on the left side of your vehicle. Speed: Adapt the speed (speed up if the obstacle can be crossed quickly, or slow down if the obstacle
is narrow)
Move: Move to the left side and make sure there is enough sideways distance to pass the obstacle. Drive smoothly! 194
Remember
Remember that the driver whose road is
open on his right side has the right of way! 195
Driving near parked vehicles:
•
Be sure to move away from the side of parked vehicles
•
(at a speed of 50 km/h, you need a distance of about one meter)
•
On a two-way road, you may have to slow down or stop when there is traffic coming from the
other direction. •
Pay attention and be careful of vehicles that are about to move. •
The required lateral distance depends on the driving speed, the faster you are, the greater the
distance between you and parked vehicles.

•
At a speed of 50 km/h, one meter is a safe side distance, which is an approximation of the
space needed to open the vehicle door. 196

Overtake vehicles traveling in the same direction on the same road.

•
If the driver in front of you indicates that he intends to turn left
•
If the road is divided into more than two lanes heading in the same direction,
The driver on which the others pass ,
the driver on which the others pass:
•
It must not increase its speed
•
He must drive on the far right side of the road
•
He must indicate to the other driver that he can
overtake him
•

a group of vehicles

usually, other vehicles must be overtaken

chunks.text:
3. Overtaking prohibitions
•
At intersections and railway lanes
•
At pedestrian crossings
199
Overtaking is prohibited:
•
On bridges
•
On curved roads and on dangerous curves
•
On slippery roads
200
Overtaking is prohibited:
•
At the end of the highways
•
In the opposite direction to other vehicles
201
Overtaking is prohibited:
•
In cases where visibility on the roads is insufficient or weak
202
Overtaking is prohibited:
•
When there is a traffic light indicating “No overtaking”
•
When there are solid lines between the tracks
203
Overtaking is prohibited:
•
When you cannot drive (significantly) faster than the vehicle in front of you
•
If the speed limit is exceeded
•
If a vehicle in front of you begins to overtake another vehicle in front of it
•
If a vehicle behind you begins to overtake your vehicle,
Remember
as you can see, that there are many things that
must be taken into account when overtaking. So
remember not to try to overtake another vehicle if
you are in doubt.
204

chunks.text:
5. The issue of overtaking
Compare the issue of overtaking with the issue of passing over fixed objects.
What is the difference?
Why could overtaking be more dangerous?
206
Before overtaking
See (Look): Speed? Way ahead of you? Space to go back to the right lane?
Assess: Is overtaking allowed? Is it safe?
Decide: Decide if you want to overtake or stay behind within a safe distance.
Act: According to your decision: Either overtake (start MSSSM) or slow down
How to overtake a vehicle ahead:
Mirrors: Check the left and side mirrors to see the vehicles behind you (because they may start to
overtake you)
Signal: Give a signal to the left
Shoulder: Check the blind spot on the left side of the vehicles next to your vehicle
Speed: Accelerate (Exceed the speed as quickly as possible without exceeding the speed limit)
Move: Move to the left side. Make sure to leave enough lateral space for the vehicle you are overtaking
and then drive smoothly!
Return to the right path: Turn right when you can see the vehicle you have overtaken in the interior
mirror and ensure to drive smoothly!
207
Ensure that all the preparations and procedures are in place for safe overtaking – when in doubt,
alert the vehicle ahead by beeping your horn before starting to overtake.
Warning signs:
•
Use the horn
•
Use a flashing front light for your
For your notes:

Using the vehicle’s lights and signals

Dashboard lights
•
Clear view
Parking the vehicle
•
Pause

Stopping the vehicle and continuing to park for a short and limited period. Parking
•
Stop the vehicle and leave it parked. 209
Stopping
Parking in places where vehicles are allowed to be parked, the vehicle is parked either on the side of
the road or on the side of the sidewalk parallel to it, unless there is a sign indicating the possibility of
parking in another way. •
Parallel to the side of the road or on the side of the sidewalk parallel to it
•
In another way, if there is a sign indicating this. 210
Vehicle parking markers
Traffic lights
This signal indicates that parking is prohibited and the prohibition ends at the first turn after the
signal, and this area is painted yellow. •
This sign means: Waiting is allowed, no parking is allowed
•
This sign means: No standing or waiting
211
Vehicle parking limiters
•
On public streets, the parking area is painted yellow
•
If the lines are white, it means that there are no restrictions on stopping vehicles
212
Vehicle parking limiters
•
It is forbidden to stop the vehicle at pedestrian crossings and on the sidewalk and less than
one and a half meters from the crossing place of schoolchildren. 213
Vehicle parking limiters
•

distance of 20 meters or less from bridges, and on bridges

chunks.text:
7. Using vehicle headlights
Headlights (emitting white lights)
•
Position lights (vehicle stop lights)
•
Low beams (intersection lights)
•
High beams
•
Fog lights (only on some vehicles)
218
Taillights
•
Rear lights that emit red light
•
Fog tail lamp
•
The brake lights (stop lights)
Emit a white light
•
Reverse lights (only when the transmission is in the reverse (“R”) position
•
Number plate lights
219
Why is it important to use vehicle headlights? Because you want to see everything well at night and you also want others to see you, right? 220
Vehicle headlights usage rules
•
At night,
drivers must use the vehicle‘s headlights while driving at night, regardless of whether the
road is equipped with general lighting or not. •
During the day,
drivers must use the vehicle‘s headlights during the day in the event of thick fog or sandstorms
obscuring vision. 221
Position lights (vehicle parking) with taillights
222
Low beams (vehicle headlights) with taillights
Drivers must use low beams
•
When driving on highways
•
In cases where the use of high beams is prohibited, and the positioning lights do not provide
sufficient visibility, but the driver must be able to see clearly and far ahead. •
When the positioning lights are not sufficient to allow other road users to see the vehicles in
time and at a sufficient distance. •
If there are vision problems or fog is present and the vehicle is not equipped with fog lights. 223
High Beams with Taillights
Remember
that high beams will annoy others! So
use regular lights in case you have others
on the road!

It is forbidden to use high beams in cities
Within city limits,
•
drivers must use regular lights
on roads outside the city
•
In the event that there are other vehicles on the opposite side, the driver must turn off the
high beams at a sufficient distance to enable the driver of the oncoming vehicle to easily
follow his lane without any danger. •
If you are driving your vehicle less than 50 meters behind other vehicles
•
In all cases that may cause great inconvenience to other road users
•
In all cases that may cause great inconvenience to users of a waterway or railway parallel to
the road. For your notes:

driving directly behind you!

chunks.section_title: The red control lamp dashboard lights mean:
chunks.text:
8. The red control lamp dashboard lights mean:
You must stop immediately!
Have a look at your vehicle manual.
The yellow control lamp means:
There is something not right. Inspect the vehicle as soon as possible and don’t ignore it for too long!
Green control lamp means:
This tells you it’s all right.
230
Fuel Indicator
This is your fuel indicator, showing you how much fuel is still in your tank. You must fill the fuel tank
when the indicator is at a quarter of the tank in order to avoid running out of fuel while you are on
the road because this puts your life and the lives of others at risk.
231

chunks.text:
1.1. Summary of Chapters
Volume SHC 101 - General is divided into 6 chapters. A brief outline of these chapters is
given below:
Chapter 1. Introduction - This chapter provides an overview of the chapters and the
background that led to the need for the development of the Saudi Highway Code (SHC).
Chapter 2. SHC Scope, Application and Processes - This chapter provides the scope and
applicability. It describes the procedural requirement (engagement of technical SME’s,
stakeholders, legal SME’s, Quality Control Board, procedure for publishing and updating the
SHC) and the technical requirements. It also includes the highways project development
process (purpose and need, pre-engineering, planning and feasibility studies, input, tools and
technical procedure of the deliverables, and the approvals. The deviation and relaxation
procedure are also described.
Chapter 3. SHC Organization and Content - This chapter provides the general approach of
the SHC and presents in brief the content of the SHC volumes. The overlapping across SHC
volumes and different domains, the SHC website and the appendices are also described.
Chapter 4. Glossary of Terms - This chapter provides the glossary of terms that appear in all
SHC volumes.
Chapter 5. Acronyms, Abbreviations - This chapter provides the list of acronyms and
abbreviations that appear in all SHC volumes.
Chapter 6. Referenced Standards and Codes - This chapter provides the list of standards
and codes that are referenced in all SHC volumes.

chunks.text:
1.2. Background
Over the past decades, the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia has witnessed a great development in
the highway network between cities. The areas of Saudi cities have also grown significantly,
which has led to an increase in the lengths of highway networks and streets within cities and
their requirements. Moreover, the constantly increasing land use growth has led to the
generation of additional travel demand. The transportation system in KSA consists of a
multimodal network of highways that serve different road user groups / transport modes
(passenger cars, trucks, transit, bicyclists, pedestrians, etc.). This newly developing
transportation system is integrated in the KSA 2030 vision goals and strategy, and as such
needs to be supported by a unified national highway code that will be in in alignment with the
modern technological developments in areas of planning, design, construction, operation, and
maintenance (e.g., performance-based design, operation and maintenance, the use of
Intelligent Transportation Systems, utilization of the Geographic Information System "GIS",
etc.). However, the responsibilities for planning, designing, constructing, maintaining, and
managing highways are distributed among several entities.

maintenance (e.g., performance-based design, operation and maintenance, the use of
Intelligent Transportation Systems, utilization of the Geographic Information System "GIS",
etc.). However, the responsibilities for planning, designing, constructing, maintaining, and
managing highways are distributed among several entities.

chunks.text:
Highways between cities fall under
the responsibility of the Ministry of Transport and Logistic Services (now under the newly
established Roads General Authority, RGA), while highways and streets within cities fall under
the responsibilities of city Amanas/municipalities (Ministry of Municipal and Rural Affairs and
2
SHC 101
Housing, MoMRAH), and some of them fall within the responsibilities of other bodies, such as
cities development authorities (such as the Royal Commission for Jubail and Yanbu, the Saudi
Authority for Industrial Cities and Technology Zones "MODON", the Royal Commission for
Riyadh City, and others), and the Ministries of Defense and Interior (military and security cities
and border highways). The operation of traffic control devices within cities (such as traffic
signals) is, currently, the responsibility of the traffic departments (General Directorate of Traffic,
Ministry of Interior). In view of the absence of a unified national highway code in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia,
which covers the aspects of planning, design, construction, operation, maintenance and
management, each of the above authorities has endeavored – in order to fulfil its
responsibilities – to cover this deficiency by either developing its own standards and
specifications or adopting some of the various international ones. As a result, there is a
discrepancy in highway projects in terms of levels of requirements for planning, design,
construction, operation and maintenance, especially in terms of the extent to which they
provide safety requirements for highway users (vehicles, pedestrians, bicycles).

chunks.text:
There is no doubt that the multiplicity of authorities responsible for highways in the Kingdom
of Saudi Arabia, with different design standards and quality requirements, has a negative
impact on highways performance and users’ safety. The existence of clear engineering
standards for highway development at all levels is extremely important, as it ensures the
quality of all highways and the safety of its users. It also sets the foundations for other related
projects, regarding the framework of the project management process consisting of five basic
steps: 1) project initiating, 2) planning, 3) executing, 4) monitoring and control and 5) project
closeout. The development of a highway code harmonized to the local conditions is of
paramount importance. The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia has made great strides with regard to
establishing systematic approach and setting unified standards in various other fields (such as
the Saudi Building Code, SBC), and the need has become urgent to develop a high standard
highway code, in order to raise the level of safety and quality on highways in the Kingdom of
Saudi Arabia. In light of the above, the Ministry of Transport and Logistic Services (MoTLS) of the Kingdom
of Saudi Arabia, in close coordination and cooperation with other public and private
stakeholders, developed the Saudi Highway Code. The objective of the SHC development project is to update the general standards and technical
guides for the planning, design, construction, operation and maintenance of highways, bridges
and tunnels in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, in order to ultimately create a safe and reliable
network that meets the current and future needs of society, industry, and the economy.

Covers all phases of the life cycle of highways and related infrastructure (i.e., planning,

Serves as a unified technical reference to all relevant stakeholders. •
Incorporates all necessary constraints from other technical disciplines. •

system and to help make the Kingdom a key strategic international logistics and regional node,
promoting sustainable economic development and competitiveness in accordance with the
Saudi Vision 2030. 3

chunks.section_title: WP-IV: Develop official website for the Saudi Highway Code Volumes.
chunks.unit_or_chapter: 4. WP-IV: Develop official website for the Saudi Highway Code Volumes.
chunks.text:
4. WP-IV: Develop official website for the Saudi Highway Code Volumes.
WP-I established a solid base for the project by building an inventory and classification of
existing standards and manuals, pertaining to highways, bridges and tunnels across the
Kingdom. Evaluation of the level and condition of the existing standards, guidelines and
manuals and benchmarking against the accepted global best practices highlighted the
potential areas for improvement.
The objective of WP-II was to determine the international standards that should be used as
references to update and improve the existing KSA standards and guidelines, and following
that, to have these sets of standards and guidelines approved by the appropriate authorities
before proceeding with the development of the new code. The main source of standards and
guidelines that were used in the development of the new code was the American Association
of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO).
WP-III then entailed the actual drafting of the Saudi Highway Code, under the following
Thematic Categories (TC):
•
TC-A: Planning and preliminary studies.
•
TC-B: Design of highways, bridges and tunnels.
•
TC-C: Construction of highways, bridges and tunnels.
•
TC-D: Highways, bridges and tunnels maintenance and management systems.
•
TC-E: Traffic engineering and road safety.
•
TC-F: Environmental aspects of highways.
•
TC-G: Autonomous vehicles requirements.
WP-IV delivered an official website for the Saudi Highway Code volumes, capable of handling
advanced, interactive and electronic services for the guidelines and standards documents to
be produced, while providing the appropriate electronic data security protection to the highest
standards.
4
SHC 101

chunks.text:
2.1. Scope and Applicability
For a vast country such as the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, characterized by a diverse topography
that varies from long stretches of deserts and sand dunes to high mountain ranges coupled
with a climate of extremely high temperatures, the need to develop and maintain a highway
network capable of serving the current and future needs of society, industry and economy
becomes even more important and essential. More specifically, given the dominance of the
highway transport sector in the country, the challenge is to ensure a safe, reliable, and
sustainable highway network for its users that promotes the suitable use of resources and
recognizes the importance of minimizing its impact on the environment. To achieve the latter,
amongst others, highways and related infrastructure of various types together with their
functional and engineering classifications require updated technical manuals and general
standards set up within a wider framework of a Highway Code for the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The term “highway” is preferred to the term “road” since the latter refers to a way or route on
land between two places that are paved or unpaved, whereas the first term includes the entire
right of way or strip limit extending beyond the road to include roadside furniture, footways,
cycle ways, amenity strips, utility strips and landscaping/edge treatment. The development of a Highway Code in the KSA aims to set, through guidelines, regulatory
requirements, instructions and technical standards and specifications, highway and bridges
planning, design, construction, operation and maintenance process.

chunks.text:
The country needs
harmonization and standardization of highway development elements that will enhance
highway quality, ensure safety needs are met and safeguard the Saudi economy, while also
protecting cultural and environmental resources. In addition, establishing uniform standards through the creation of a Saudi Highway Code to
be applied throughout the entirety of the Kingdom will alleviate inconsistencies among the
existing various standards, guidelines and manuals adopted by the governmental authorities
currently responsible for overseeing and regulating the highway sector, while at the same time
setting the basis and the frame to deal with any possible arising dispute. Moreover, relevant stakeholders will be able to access the information necessary to plan,
design, construct, operate, and maintain highways, bridges and tunnels, of all their different
classes, in the Kingdom. The Saudi Highway Code will define the collective requirements for
every aspect of the country’s highway, bridge and tunnel infrastructure, will be the principal
guiding document for engineers, technicians, and professionals, enabling these to perform
their work in a safe and secure manner and to the required professional level and standard. The Saudi Highway Code will be available in both English and Arabic in electronic format, and
Arabic version prevails regarding to the contracts drafting, their documents and annexes, in
accordance with Article 55 of the the government procurement and competition system issued
by Royal Decree No. (M/128) dated 11/13/1440 AH, where there is any discrepancy in the
translation the English version of the Code shall prevail. 5
SHC 101
2.1.1.

chunks.text:
Procedural Requirement
To ensure that the published Saudi Highway Code is widely accepted by all entities and the
potential practitioners, its development was taken through a series of both consultative and
quality procedures. 2.1.1.1. Engagement of technical SMEs
Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) played a critical role in the development of the SHC, they
provided specialized technical insight, gap filling and the regional acceptance of the inclusion
of international benchmarked codes. The close integration of the inputs of academic and professional experts (i.e., SMEs) in both
the identification of the relevant literature and the review and validation of project deliverables
played an important role in the SHC's development. The development of each SHC Volume
was monitored, evaluated, and reviewed by the respective SME. In general, the SMEs’ expertise integrated with the technical team knowledge ensured that the
SHC Volumes meet the needs of the standards and best practices. To achieve this, SMEs:

chunks.text:
4. Contributed to knowledge transfer. 2.1.1.2. Engagement of key stakeholders
A list of the key stakeholders (entities and authorities) was produced and assessed based on
their technical and procedural needs, enabling a series of targeted workshops to be planned. These needs and requirements related to the design or specification aspects that can
collectively be considered. Each focus-group of stakeholders and SMEs was engaged in the targeted workshops and
participant’s interaction was coordinated and facilitated during the workshop activities. The intention of the initial workshops was for the Technical Development Team to draw on the
wider local knowledge of the stakeholders and SMEs and to fully consider their viewpoint on
key issues. The Technical Team subsequently presented the early findings of their work seeking
verification, and total buy-in of the work and the process in moving forward. A further consultative phase was undertaken when the delivery of the SHC Volumes were in
their draft versions, this involved the circulation of the Draft Volumes to entities, stakeholders
and interested parties for comments prior to finalization of the publications. 2.1.1.3. Engagement of legal SMEs (SHC Governance)
It is mandatory to establish a coordinating mechanism across the government entities
concerned with the activation and implementation of the Code and its requirements. This is a
6
SHC 101
particularly challenging and politically sensitive task, particularly when introducing new
concepts, roles and amendments to the current institutional framework. As part of this project, the existing roles and responsibilities of all relevant competent
government authorities and entities were reviewed by the Legal Experts.

The aim of the legal
review was to identify the role of each entity in the planning, design, construction, operation
and management/maintenance of highway infrastructure within the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. This assisted the legal team in advising and proposing any changes to the existing roles and
responsibilities. This may also involve re-alignment, redistribution and, in some cases, even
transfer of responsibilities. However, the legal team’s viewpoint was that ideally, any new
governance framework would need to be as simple as possible. To be effective, it was also
viewed that it is generally advisable for any new governance framework to be built on the
existing system to minimize disturbance and incorporate where possible current elements
which are working. Any new system should seek to accommodate the existing governance
infrastructure as far as possible. The risk of introducing a major overhaul is that it will not get
high level approval or cross-government support. This activity required extensive consultation with the relevant entities so that any governance
and institutional arrangements put forward in the Code will have cross-government support. As a principle, the project sought to establish a lead agency that will be the custodian of the
Highway Code. The selected lead agency must be a government body whose role will be to
lead the development, implementation and enforcement of the Highway Code. This body must
be accepted and fully supported by the rest of government; this will ensure that it receives the
necessary funding and capacity, and proper coordination mechanisms are agreed between
ministries, committees, and authorities involving duties of exchange of information and
cooperation.

chunks.text:
A number of stakeholders are expected to support the strategic initiatives of the project, since
they will encourage the applicability of the project’s proposals to assist in their success. This is
a facet of so-called “participatory planning”, the most common form of which is consultation
with all concerned authorities. The development of meaningful consultation with key stakeholders through workshops and
one-to-one meetings will minimize risks and encourage early “buy in”. The aim will be to
develop an understanding of key entities roles and responsibilities (as these are not always
transparent); to understand (e.g., from contractors and consultants) any difficulties or issues
that they have with the existing system; to share information and provide justification for the
proposed introduction of a new Code; and to take the opportunity to discuss any changes to
the governance framework and existing processes. Consultation was undertaken to enable the Technical and Legal Teams to better understand
any concerns and potentially refute, convince or address criticisms. Furthermore, it will enable
and reassure stakeholders that they understand the issues stemming from the existing system,
and in some cases, it may assist in identifying and solving problems. 2.1.1.4. Quality Control Board (QCB)
Additional experts were assigned to the quality control assessment of the deliverables. These Additional Experts groupings consisted of the following positions:
7
SHC 101

chunks.section_title: Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category. 2.1.1.5. Procedure for publishing and updating the SHC. Upon the approval of the contents of SHC, the responsible authority (RGA) has the power to
decide for SHC Volumes’ publications. All the Volumes will be available only in softcopy and
will be available from the SHC's official website. If one of the stakeholders raise an issue for modification/amendment in any of the SHC
Volumes, this issue can be discussed by the members (board of directors) of the responsible
authority (RGA) in order to decide if there is really a need for update in SHC. In case there are
minor amendments, then either an addendum/erratum or a list of modifications can be
published online in SHC website. If the changes are considered to be major, then the authority
has to follow the procedures for the government procurement and competition system of KSA
Government. 2.1.2. Technical Requirements
The Saudi Highway Code (SHC) can be defined to comprise a group of guidelines, regulatory
requirements, instructions and technical standards and specifications for the control of various
aspects of the highways and related infrastructure- from planning, design, and construction to
operation and maintenance - with a view to collectively obtaining a long-term impact on the
development of the highway network and the quality and efficiency of its infrastructure and
services. The Saudi Highway Code must be able to adapt to local and regional conditions, but
also to be technologically innovative and allow future challenges that highway transport
infrastructures may face to be met.

chunks.section_title: Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
The Saudi Highway Code has been written in a concise manner that will clearly state what shall
be done, following drafting rules that have been developed from internationally leading
research on excellence in standards development, to organize and streamline its adoption and
implementation, and avoid any misunderstanding or misinterpretation at the National level. Mandatory requirements in this code are indicated by the terms “must”, “shall” and “will”. Advisory guidance is indicated by the terms “may”, “should” and “would”. The Designer and
Contractor must comply with mandatory requirements. If in any particular case, it is not
possible to comply, they must agree to conform to the "Deviation & Relaxation" procedure
stated in Section 2.3. Some volumes of the code contain advice, explanation, and illustrative
examples, which are commended to the code user for further consideration. Finally, the most important aspect in this effort is that the Saudi Highway Code is adapted to
the National and local conditions and best practices accounting for traffic, behavioral, cultural,
and environmental factors. 8
SHC 101

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
2.2. Highway Projects Development and Design
2.2.1. Project Purpose and Need
The purpose and need statement inform priorities on what will, and what will not, be
undertaken in the project. The designer should refer to this purpose and need statement in
determining the scope of the project under development and assessing whether any changes
suggested by others are applicable to the project purpose and need. The purpose and need
statement for a project should be built around an assessment of past performance for all
transportation modes of the highways within the project limits and a forecast of future
performance if no project is undertaken. The purpose and need statement should address the
project context and how each transportation mode should be accommodated. The purpose and need statement should also indicate what aspects of performance should be
improved and, in some cases, may also set targets for how much performance should be
improved. The performance-based approach to establishing the purpose and need for and the
objectives of the project enables the designer to focus on addressing the needs of a project
without needlessly exceeding them. By limiting a project’s scope to focus only on documented
performance improvement needs, more resources are available to be spent on other needs
throughout the national highways system. The scope of projects, based on their purpose and need, may range from simple projects on
existing roadways to new construction projects that create a new highway and complex
reconstruction projects that may change the character of an existing one. The first step in effective Scope Management is the identification, definition and
documentation of the project objectives as well as selecting the best approach to achieving
the project objectives.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
The project work is then identified and defined to a common framework,
the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). The definition of project scope and associated planning documents are then reviewed for
formal work authorization before commencement of work. Following authorization of work,
baseline changes are controlled in a structured manner through a control system. Authorization is necessary before work is initiated on the change, thus providing a good audit
trail of baseline changes. Utilization of a WBS assures that all work is identified and defined within a common framework. It provides the means to subdivide the project into manageable segments and to describe the
scope of work within each segment. The documentation and approval of the scope baseline provides a basis for making future
decisions, accomplishing verification measures, and evaluating potential scope changes. Formal authorization of the commencement of work is to be made available through an audit
trail from project initiation to completion of all authorized work. Documented work
authorization provides a means for effective “internal” coordination, communication and
obtaining required approvals. Scope Reporting entails the timely determination, recording and accumulation of project
progress/status data and transformation of this data into structured information necessary to
judge project performance. This is to be established through the preparation of progress
reports. As the project progresses, project process/status information is recorded,
accumulated, and transformed into structured information necessary to judge project
9
SHC 101
performance, the data is analyzed to determine the current status of the project, significant
problem areas, developing trends and to forecast future status.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
The progress made is tracked against the baseline schedule and any changes to the schedule
requires authorization. 2.2.2. Pre-Engineering, Planning, Design and Implementation
The lifecycle of a highway project runs through various stages, starting as a simple concept,
possibly represented by a rough indication of a route, and finally reaching the operation of
the highway, with vehicles transporting passengers and goods running on it. The concept might arise from a national or regional masterplan or plan, a local plan, city plan
or an ad hoc decision, stemming from sudden or unexpected changes in the traffic and travel
pattern, in the geopolitical scene or even natural disasters. The stages highway projects go through can be grouped as follows:
•
Planning and preliminary studies. These generally include identification of available
options, feasibility studies, demand assessment, economic appraisal, preliminary
environmental considerations. In these stages of a highway project development, the
SHC 201 (Planning Process), SHC 202 (Surveying and Mapping) and SHC 203
(Preliminary Studies) Volumes are applicable, and possibly SHC 601 (Traffic
Engineering) in certain relevant situations. •
Design studies. These generally range from concept design to detailed design, at a
level whereby the study can be used for a tender and the subsequent construction. The
studies should cover all aspects of the highway, bridges, tunnels, equipment and
facilities.

Design studies. These generally range from concept design to detailed design, at a
level whereby the study can be used for a tender and the subsequent construction. The
studies should cover all aspects of the highway, bridges, tunnels, equipment and
facilities.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
In these stages of the highway development project the SHC 301 (Highway
Geometric Design), SHC 302 (Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Hydrology and
Hydraulic Design), SHC 303 (Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Rest Areas, Truck
Inspection Stations, Parking & Garage Facilities), SHC 304 (Highway Facilities and
Utilities Design – Passive Safety Systems Design), SHC 305 (Highway Facilities and
Utilities Design – Work Zone Design), SHC 306 (Highway Facilities and Utilities Design
– Public Utilities, Highway and Street Lighting, Control and Monitoring Devices) and
SHC 307 (Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Landscape Planting, Outdoor
Advertising) Volumes are applicable. For the assessment of the environmental impacts
of the project, SHC 701 (Environmental Aspects of Highways) is applicable. In certain
cases, a Road Safety Audit might be required, in which case the relevant provisions of
SHC 603 (Road Safety) are applicable. In all cases topographical surveys and suitable
maps are required; SHC 202 (Surveying and Mapping) is therefore applicable. •
Construction works. The construction of a highway requires a tendering procedure,
which subsequently leads to the construction of the project. In these stages of the
highway project development the SHC 401 (Construction of Highways), SHC 402
(Construction of Bridges and Tunnels) and SHC 403 (Construction of Highway Facilities)
Volumes apply. In certain cases, a Road Safety Audit might be required, in which case
the relevant provisions of SHC 603 (Road Safety) are applicable. For the works in
progress SHC 305 (Work Zone Design) and SHC 602 (Manual on Uniform Traffic Control
Devices) are applicable. 10
SHC 101
•
Operation-Maintenance stage.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
Once constructed, the highway project needs suitable
operation and maintenance. SHC 501 (Pavement Maintenance Management Systems),
SHC 502 (Bridges and Tunnels Maintenance and Management Systems) and SHC 503
(Highway Facilities Maintenance and Management Systems) Volumes are applicable. For works in progress during maintenance SHC 305 (Highway Facilities and Utilities
Design – Work Zone Design) and SHC 602 (Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices)
Volumes are applicable. For issues related to managing the traffic SHC 601 (Traffic
Engineering) is applicable. If a Road Safety Audit is required, the relevant provisions of
SHC 603 (Road Safety) are applicable. If any environmental aspect has to be addressed,
the provisions of SHC 701 (Environmental Aspects of Highways) are applicable. Finally, for the eventual future developments in the transport sector, and the prospects of
autonomous vehicles, SHC 801 (Autonomous Vehicles Requirements) provisions are
applicable. 2.2.3. Design Development (Inputs, Technical Procedures, and Outputs)
For all the stages of a highway project, relevant provisions are to be found in the SHC volumes:
for planning, design, construction, operation and maintenance, environmental monitoring,
road safety auditing and inspection, traffic monitoring and impact assessment, … etc. Inputs generally include measurements, topographical surveys, reconnaissance reports from
site visits, laboratory test results, recommendations on minimum values, use of models and/or
nomographs. Outputs generally include reports, designs and drawings, recommendations, bills of quantities,
proposals for alternative or additional schemes and possibly other outputs on a case-by-case
basis.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
Technical procedures and processes are provided in the relevant SHC volumes for all of the
above. In all cases sound engineering judgement is recommended in conjunction with a clear
understanding of local conditions and eventual particularities. The Designer must ensure that all aspects of the design work together to provide an overall
design that meets all the objectives in a sustainable manner. It is the responsibility of the
highway designer to ensure that all the elements fit together. Whereas it is the responsibility
of the Project Manager to ensure that any issues raised are resolved satisfactorily. All outputs from the design process should be delivered in accordance with the client's
requirements. Where the design is prepared within the Competent Authority, the Designer
should follow the “Quality Management Standards and Processes” for preparing a complete
projects development and design package. Where the design is prepared by a Designer
outside the Competent Authority (a design consultant) the client’s requirements shall be
detailed in the contract. A Detailed Design should be suitable to provide sufficient detail to allow for tendering and
construction purposes and will amongst other outputs include:
•
Finalized design documentation, whether electronic or hard copy,
•
Appropriate inputs to the construction specifications,
•
Confirmation of compliance with applicable statutory and regulatory requirements. 11
SHC 101
Part of the Detailed Design process is to ensure that the project will be easy to build and
maintain. The process of checking for constructability and maintainability should be addressed
at all phases of design and must, by its nature, be an iterative one during each phase.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
For
maintainability, design decisions are linked to whether the future infrastructure can be
maintained “Economically” and “Safely”. The Project Manager (for Design) must provide all the documentation relating to the project
in both hard and soft copy, either at the Tender stage or once the contract has been awarded. The Construction Project Manager and the team should be briefed on the key project
requirements. At this stage, the Competent Authority will start to assemble their team for the
implementation phase of the project. This briefing, often called the “transition briefing” should
also cover all the assumptions made during the design stages, all key decisions that have been
taken, and the risks that have been identified and managed during the design. The Implementation Phase involves the production, creation, and construction of the project
deliverables - new product or service. During this phase, the approved project plan is executed,
and the project deliverables are developed and completed according to scope and project
objectives. Design alterations during the construction phase may be necessary for a number
of reasons including:
•
Alternatives put forward during the tender process and accepted,
•
Non-conformance during construction,
•
Accepted scope changes due to requests and representations from other government
authorities,
•
Accepted changes whether initiated by the construction contractor or otherwise,
•
Previously unidentified inputs, for example, utilities, and
•
An error in the design. The design team should be made available to assist the Competent Authority in these matters. 2.2.4. Approval and Close Out Requirements
2.2.4.1.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
Project Management Plan (PMP)
The preparation of the project management plan helps to ensure successful project delivery
and the maintenance of public trust, support, and confidence throughout the life of the
project. The project management plan is a tool to help the Competent Authority maintain
focus toward effectively and efficiently delivering a quality product through construction
closeout. Any activities beyond construction closeout should not be addressed in the project
management plan. The purpose of the project management plan shall be to clearly define the
roles and responsibilities of the leadership and management teams and to document the
procedures and processes that shall be in effect to provide timely information to project
decision-makers in areas such as:
•
Identifying project requirements. •
Establishing communication protocols. •
Managing:
o
Scope
o
Quality
o
Schedule
12
SHC 101
o
Cost
o
Resources
o
Risks, and
o
Applicable local laws and regulations. Project management plans shall be prepared and submitted by the "Project Sponsor". For the
purposes of this guidance, the term "Project Sponsor" means the Competent Authority or
other entity, including any private entity that provides funding for the project and administers
any engineering consultancy, construction, or construction engineering/inspection activities
for the project. When a private entity is responsible for submitting the project management
plan, the plan should be coordinated with the Competent Authority. Approval of the project management plan as conforming to the project requirements will be
based upon a compliance review that evaluates the documented procedures and processes to
manage the project and the roles of the project management team.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
The project management plan should be updated as appropriate throughout the life of the
project. The purpose of the updates to the project management plan is to ensure that the
documented project procedures, processes, and roles are current. Project management plan
updates with significant changes to the project should be submitted to the Competent
Authority for review and approval. All other project management plan updates should be
submitted for recordkeeping purposes. 2.2.4.2. Project Governance
All projects are managed, from their inception to completion, by a Project Manager appointed
by the Competent Authority, who in most cases will be one of its staff. However, where an
external project manager is appointed, the requirements of the Competent Authority will also
apply. In some cases, the Competent Authority may retain the services of a firm specializing in the
supervision and management of design consultants, project management of contractors, or
site management of consultant supervision and design teams which will be appointed as
Consultant/Contractor Representative for the project. In such cases the Representative is
accountable for the delivery of the project objectives and deliverables under the direction of
the Competent Authority's Project Manager. In each of the project life cycles there are points between the stages where key project
decisions must be taken. These ‘gates’ logically link each stage of the project development to
create a linear flow process, but also provide a control point where a decision has to be made
whether to allow the project to move on to the next stage.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 3. Quality control experts per Thematic Category.
chunks.text:
At each ‘gate’ designated project leaders (the project steering committee, the project sponsor,
or other executive body assigned by the Competent Authority) determine whether the project
should proceed; require further development work; or be deferred or canceled. The project
leaders may invite experts on the topic or subject matter to advise them in their decision-
making. These review gates provide stakeholders with a transparent and disciplined approach to
project management. They also improve the focus on quality of project execution and more
13
SHC 101
effective product development. The results from each phase provide the key outcomes that
are used as the inputs to the next phase. All phase gate approvals from Concept Development to Project Delivery must be adhered to
for all highways projects and if required some of them may be combined together. However,
a project should not move from one stage of the life cycle to the next until all the requirements
have been met. 2.2.4.3. Project Close-Out Requirements
The Close-out phase formally marks the completion of the project and is performed after all
of the defined deliverables have been produced and all the project objectives have been met
and the client has accepted the product, service or result. The Close-out Phase involves the release of the final deliverables and hand-over to the client. It is the formal sign-off and acceptance of the project deliverable(s) and signals the completion
of the project work. The “Project Sponsor”, on behalf of the Competent Authority, approves
the final deliverable(s).

•
Financial and administrative closure of the project,

Sign-off and acceptance by sponsor and client,
•
Creation of project archives, documentation and lessons learned, and
•
Initiation of the operation and maintenance activities affected when the project is
handed over to the client. The key outcomes of the Project Close-out Phase include:
•
Project Deliverables completed and accepted,
•
Work Completion Form signed (for Construction and Maintenance projects only),
•
Project Completion Report Issued (for Construction and Maintenance projects only)
•
Final Completion Certificate Issued (for Construction and Maintenance projects only),
•
Contract Close-out (for Construction projects only),
•
Project Sponsor and Client sign-off,
•
Financial & Administrative Closure of the project,
•
Lessons Learnt Document prepared,
•
Project Documentation archived, and
•
Project Transferred to Client. At the end of the construction period and once the project has been signed off as substantially
complete, the project enters the maintenance period or construction defects liability stage
where the Contractor is responsible for the maintenance and for putting right any defects
which occur. The new highway will be open for use by the public during this period, but they

Road Safety Audits and Inspections
A “Road Safety Audit, RSA” can provide information that can be used by the highway designer
either as one of several initial inputs to the design process or as a part of the review process
for a design. For further guidance on the review aspect, refer to “Chapter 4, Road Safety Audits
and Inspections” of SHC 603 (Road Safety). The four audits are undertaken at certain stages of
the project:
•
Road Safety Audit 1 is carried out during Preliminary Design.
•
Road Safety Audit 2 is carried out during Detailed Design.
•
Road Safety Audit 3 is carried out during the Construction period.
•
Road Safety Audit 4 is carried out at the Opening of the project.
Road Safety Audits and Inspections shall be performed as a minimum according to the type
of project and the related stages of design and construction. The details of how to carry out
Road Safety Audits are set out in “Sec. 4.3., Stages and Types of Road Safety Audits” of SHC

2.3. Deviation and Relaxation
The provisions in the SHC volumes are all mandatory. Where alternative approaches are
proposed by the designer, contractor or maintenance-operation contractor, in the form of
deviations and relaxations from the mandatory provisions, these must be clearly justified and
subsequently approved. The reason for deviating-relaxing must be clearly presented,
indicating the consequences of non-deviation-relaxation and/or the grounds on which the
mandatory provisions cannot apply. These requests for deviation-relaxation must be signed
by accredited/certified personnel (engineers, planners, surveyors).

6. It can be justified that a requirement of a Standard is inappropriate in a particular
situation.
Deviation and relaxation applications shall be provided for approval on a location-specific
basis and relate to the particular circumstances identified in each case. A similar
deviation/relaxation approval may be quoted as a precedent to support a new application, but
each case will be considered on its eligibility.
The approval of the request for such deviations by the Competent Authorities should also be
signed by an accredited/certified professional. Competent Authorities include the RGA, but
also other authorities or agencies and ministries (MoTLS, MOMRAH, Amanah, Royal
Commissions, the NRSC, the NCEC, MODON, EXPRO, … etc.). The reasons for accepting the
deviations-relaxations should be clearly stated. In the case where these deviations-relaxations
are rejected, there should be adequate justification.
More details can be found in SHC 301 (Highway Geometric Design).

3.1. The General Approach
The implementation of the work packages culminated in the Saudi Highway Code, structured
around the Thematic and Sub-thematic Categories. An introduction to the SHC giving an
overview of the code is also included. The SHC therefore consists of 25 Volumes. These are
outlined below. TC A: Planning and Preliminary Studies
•
SHC 201 – Planning Process. •
SHC 202 – Surveying and Mapping. •
SHC 203 – Preliminary Studies. TC B: Design of Highways, Bridges and Tunnels
•
SHC 301 – Highway Geometric Design. •
SHC 302 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Hydrology and Hydraulic Design. •
SHC 303 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Rest Areas, Truck Inspection
Stations, Parking and Garage Facilities. •
SHC 304 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Passive Safety Systems Design. •
SHC 305 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Work Zone Design. •
SHC 306 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Public Utilities, Highway and
Street Lighting, Control and Monitoring Devices. •
SHC 307 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Landscape Planting, Outdoor
Advertising. •
SHC 308 – Pavement Design. •
SHC 309 – Material Specifications and Standardized Testing. •
SHC 310 – Bridges and Tunnels Design. TC C: Construction of Highways, Bridges and Tunnels
•
SHC 401 – Construction of Highways. •
SHC 402 – Construction of Bridges and Tunnels. •
SHC 403 – Construction of Highway Facilities. TC D: Highways, Bridges and Tunnels Maintenance and Management Systems
•
SHC 501 – Pavement Maintenance Management Systems. •
SHC 502 – Bridges and Tunnels Maintenance and Management Systems. •
SHC 503 – Highway Facilities Maintenance and Management Systems. TC E: Traffic Engineering and Road Safety
•
SHC 601 – Traffic Engineering.

chunks.section_title: The Content of the SHC Volumes
chunks.text:
3.2. The Content of the SHC Volumes
3.2.1. SHC 201 – Planning Process
SHC 201 provides requirements and considerations for fundamental aspects related to the
planning process for highways and streets. An overview of the highway planning process is
provided (Chapter 2), covering key requirements and concepts of metropolitan, regional and
nationwide highway planning. Furthermore, requirements for the planning of the highway
network in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia are specified, focusing on combining the goals of
spatial highway planning for the accessibility of places in the Kingdom and deriving the
functional hierarchy of the highway network (Chapter 3) to achieve a uniform spatial planning
approach and a coordinated highway network development. Guiding principles and major
considerations in the context of traffic in urban areas of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia are also
provided (Chapter 4), aiming to create a comprehensive approach in balancing the movement
of people and goods in urban streets, while at the same time preserving their safe, accessible
and economically sustainable character. Furthermore, general planning guidelines and
considerations regarding the on-road public transport network and facilities throughout the
Kingdom of Saudi Arabia are specified (Chapter 5), along with planning requirements for
pedestrian and bicycle facilities along streets and highways, inside the public right-of-way
(Chapter 6). Additionally, fundamental highway capacity guidance and context for
transportation planners and traffic engineers is provided (Chapter 7) aiming to ensure a high
degree of consistency in transportation planning, design and traffic analysis. 3.2.2.

approach and a coordinated highway network development. Guiding principles and major
considerations in the context of traffic in urban areas of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia are also
provided (Chapter 4), aiming to create a comprehensive approach in balancing the movement
of people and goods in urban streets, while at the same time preserving their safe, accessible
and economically sustainable character. Furthermore, general planning guidelines and
considerations regarding the on-road public transport network and facilities throughout the
Kingdom of Saudi Arabia are specified (Chapter 5), along with planning requirements for
pedestrian and bicycle facilities along streets and highways, inside the public right-of-way
(Chapter 6). Additionally, fundamental highway capacity guidance and context for
transportation planners and traffic engineers is provided (Chapter 7) aiming to ensure a high
degree of consistency in transportation planning, design and traffic analysis. 3.2.2.

chunks.section_title: The Content of the SHC Volumes
chunks.text:
SHC 202 – Surveying and Mapping
The purpose of this Volume is to provide guidelines for the personnel responsible for surveying
and mapping works. The Volume summarizes engineering knowledge and practices and
provides typical methods of surveying and mapping procedures. Chapter 2 (Basic Survey
Requirements) introduces Surveying and its basic methods, concepts, and principles as well as
the definition of survey datum. In chapter 3 (Survey Requirements for Road Projects) the
surveying procedures involved in highway design phases, construction, and maintenance, as
well as other basic concepts regarding the personnel involved in surveying are described. Chapter 4 (General Guidelines for Field Survey Work) describes planning and preparation
procedures related to survey fieldwork, as well as general directions for fieldwork applied in
all surveying methodologies. Chapter 5 (Preparing Maps) describes the form of the final maps
to be produced by surveying works. Chapter 6 (Data Delivery and Formats) provides
information on the available data formats used when submitting final survey data. Chapter 7
(Horizontal Control (TPS)) describes the procedures required for establishing horizontal
control in highway projects with the employment of Total Point Stations (TPS). Chapter 8
(Vertical Control (Leveling)) describes the procedures required for establishing vertical control
in highway projects with the employment of levels. Chapter 9 (Topographic Survey (TPS))
describes the procedures involved in ground surveying with Total Point Stations. Chapter 10
(GNSS Surveying) provides general information about GNSS surveys and techniques. Chapter

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 11. (GNSS Data Collection) describes the processes required for GNSS surveys in the field.
chunks.text:
11 (GNSS Data Collection) describes the processes required for GNSS surveys in the field. Chapter 12 (GNSS Data Processing) describes the procedures involved in GNSS data
processing and error adjustment. Chapter 13 (Aerial Surveying) describes the procedures of
aerial surveys, introducing the concept of photogrammetry and LiDAR aerial surveys with
manned and unmanned aerial vehicles. Chapter 14 (Mobile Mapping) describes mobile
19
SHC 101
mapping surveys along with the introduction to the procedures, data processing, and
equipment needed. Chapter 15 (Laser Scanning) describes the procedures required for using
Terrestrial Laser Scanning (TLS) in highway projects. Chapter 16 (Bathymetric Surveying)
describes the procedures employed for using bathymetric, echo-sounding surveys in highway
projects. Chapter 17 (Digital Terrain Modelling) provides guidelines for Digital Terrain Model
(DTM) data collection, creation, and quality control. Chapter 18 (Cadastral for Road Projects)
covers the tasks involved when creating cadastral maps for highway projects. Chapter 19
(Construction Surveys and AMG) sets the necessities for construction surveys and the
employment of Automatic Machine Guidance (AMG) in the construction of highway projects. Chapter 20 (Road Maintenance and BIM) covers the notion of BIM when employed in highway
projects. Finally, Chapter 21 (Exception Procedure) provides the Design Exception Procedure
required to override the content of this code. 3.2.3. SHC 203 – Preliminary Studies
SHC 203 is to be utilized for aspects related to the preliminary studies of highways.

chunks.text:
The Volume
sets the requirements for traffic forecasting and traffic impact studies (Chapter 2), by specifying
the preliminary level of traffic engineering analysis required to ensure consistency,
comprehensiveness and adequacy in the assessment and treatment of traffic impacts of new
developments or the expansion of existing developments, while considering the needs of all
highway users and the affected communities. Requirements and guidance regarding the
economic appraisal of highway projects (Chapter 3) is provided, using Benefit-Cost Analysis
(BCA) in order to assist evidence-based decision making on selecting the projects that deserve
implementation by KSA authorities, project managers and transport engineers. Additionally,
requirements for geotechnical investigations at the preliminary stage are provided (Chapter
4), including preliminary desktop study and site reconnaissance, project investigations, data
compilation, geotechnical analyses and interpretation, and writing of a geotechnical report. The framework requirements for conceptual and reconnaissance studies of highways are
specified (Chapter 5), starting from the problem definition and the selection of references and
design standards, then the preliminary engineering investigation, the alternatives
development and evaluation, the clarification of design features of the proposed schemes and
finally the required documentation of this study phase. Also, the framework for environmental
considerations at the preliminary study phase is defined (Chapter 6), focusing on the required
coordination between the environmental assessment of all potential impacts of the project
and the project's preliminary design stages.

considerations at the preliminary study phase is defined (Chapter 6), focusing on the required
coordination between the environmental assessment of all potential impacts of the project
and the project's preliminary design stages.

chunks.text:
Finally, a brief synopsis of hydrological aspects
that need to be considered at the preliminary studies phase is included (Chapter 7). The
Volume's appendices contain additional supplementary information, necessary theoretical
background and step by step guidance for analysis and procedures related to the
aforementioned topics. 3.2.4. SHC 301 – Highway Geometric Design
SHC 301 provides all necessary technical information regarding highway geometric design for
all highway categories in rural and urban areas, including intersections and interchanges. Its
content applies for the design of new highways and the reconstruction, and rehabilitation of
existing ones. The Volume defines the functional and contextual classification of highways and
describes the Rural and Urban areas, and context (Chapter 2), critical predesign elements in
20
SHC 101
Rural and Urban areas and general characteristics of major parameters, such as human factors,
speed, design vehicles, vulnerable highway users, (Chapter 3)general parameters for geometric
design regarding horizontal and vertical alignment, sight distance, design speed, cross-section
elements, typical cross-sections, including information for design and operation of special
infrastructure elements such as bridges and tunnels, climbing lanes, camel overpasses and
other associated facilities (Chapter 4).

chunks.text:
Also provided is all technical information needed for the
geometric design of:
•
a rural highway, such as design speed, cross-section specific elements, horizontal and
vertical design parameters, sight distances, superelevation and lane widening
parameters and other associated facilities (Chapter 5);
•
a residential street, a collector or an arterial in urban area, such as design speed, cross-
section specific elements, horizontal and vertical design parameters, sight distances,
superelevation and lane widening parameters and other associated facilities (Chapter
6);
•
a controlled access highway (freeways or expressways), such as design speed, cross-
section specific elements, horizontal and vertical design parameters, sight distances,
superelevation and lane widening parameters and other associated facilities (Chapter
7);
•
a low volume and access highway, such as design speed, cross-section specific
elements, horizontal and vertical design parameters, sight distances, superelevation
and lane widening parameters and other associated facilities (Chapter 8);
•
an industrial highway, such as design speed, cross-section specific elements, horizontal
and vertical design parameters, sight distances, superelevation and lane widening
parameters and other associated facilities (Chapter 9);
•
at-grade intersection, either “T” or Cross type, and roundabout. Parameters regarding
intersection design, such as design vehicles, design elements, sight distances and more
are defined, while consideration for bicycles, pedestrians and public transport mainly
in urban areas are described (Chapter 10);
•
interchanges. Parameters regarding interchange design, such as interchange types,
design elements for ramps, cross-section specific elements and more are defined
(Chapter 11).
