chunks.text:
Also contains information regarding the non-freeway rehabilitation design criteria (3R),
technical issues regarding reconstruction of all highway categories (4R) and finally mobility
corridor design criteria (5R). Finally, the Volume's appendices contain additional
supplementary information and necessary theoretical background related to highway
geometric design, to ensure a common understanding of the topics addressed within this
Volume. 3.2.5. SHC 302 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Hydrology and
Hydraulic Design
SHC 302 provides requirements and considerations related to Hydrology and Hydraulics in the
design, construction, maintenance and operation of highways. The Volume includes studies
on the necessary hydrological inputs for any hydraulic design or structure, which are described
in detail in Chapter 2 (Hydrology). In the next chapters, the most important hydraulic design
and structures are analyzed in detail. Specifically, Chapter 3 (Channel) described the design of
21
SHC 101
open channels as a type of natural or artificial waterways, where the water's surface is open to
the atmosphere and the motion is driven by the component of gravity acting in the direction
of motion. In Chapter 4 (Culverts), the necessary steps for the design of culverts are described,
aiming at the drainage of stormwater from the pavement and watercourses crossing the
highway system. The interception and concentration of overland flow and constriction of
natural waterways inevitably results in an increased erosion potential, and in order to protect
the culverts and adjacent areas, it is necessary to employ an energy dissipator, as described in
Chapter 5 (Energy Dissipators).

chunks.text:
In Chapters 6 (Storm Drainage Systems) and 7 (Storage
Facilities), the drainage system and storage policy, guidelines, design frequency, and roadway
geometrics are discussed. In Chapter 8 (Bridges and Low Water Crossings), guidelines and
instructions for the bridge design approach, design frequency, hydraulic analysis, bridge scour,
and deck drainage are provided. In Chapter 9 (Channel and Stream Bank Stabilization), the
necessary measures and precautions to ensure that the above hydraulic structures can
transport the intended flow, provide stable mechanisms and methods to withstand severe
erosion or scour and sediment control (analyzed in detail in the final chapter 9, Erosion and
Sediment Control). 3.2.6. SHC 303 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Rest Areas, Truck
Inspection Stations, Parking and Garage Facilities
SHC 303 is divided into three distinct parts, corresponding to the design of Rest Areas, Truck
Inspection Stations and Parking & Garage Facilities, respectively. With regard to Rest Areas,
guidelines and general requirements are provided for the design and operation of these
facilities in order to ensure a safe, secure and operationally efficient Rest Area network
regarding the freeway, divided expressway and rural highway system of the Kingdom of Saudi
Arabia. For Truck Inspection Stations, guidelines and general requirements for the design and
operation of these facilities are provided, regarding in particular main facility components,
layout, necessary equipment and operational procedures in order to contribute to the
enforcement of legal load limits in the KSA highway network.

chunks.text:
Finally, SHC 303 provides the
guidelines and general requirements for the design of Parking and Garage Facilities in order
to ensure the uniformity of parking design in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for on-street, off
street and parking building facilities. 3.2.7. SHC 304 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Passive Safety
Systems Design
SHC 304 provides guidelines for designers, contractors and authorities involved in the
selection, installation, and maintenance of vehicle restraint systems (VRS). The criteria related
to the VRS selection process in general constitute Guidelines which are binding; however,
exceptions in specific cases are allowed if they are properly supported. On the other hand,
aspects related to the installation, maintenance, controls, technical criteria, quality assurance
of VRS as well in-situ concrete barriers are considered as Specifications, meaning that their
content is obligatory in their implementation and use. Chapter 2 describes in brief the two VRS
approaches that prevail, namely, the US MASH and the European EN 1317. Interrelations
regarding basic requirements of performance and applicability aspects with core entities of
the Kingdom (National Standardization Committee, Saudi Standards Metrology and Quality
22
SHC 101
Organization (SASO) and the Roads General Authority (RGA) are also outlined. Chapter 3
describes the various types of vehicle restraint systems, their areas of implementation, the
surrounding area arrangements and additional structures that can be installed along with
vehicle restraint systems. Chapter 4 lists the criteria for installing vehicle restraint systems in
terms of hazardous locations, risk classification and critical vs decisive distances.

Chapter 5
outlines performance requirements of vehicle restraint systems per type. It also addresses
compliance characteristics between US MASH and EN 1317 roadside safety barriers. Chapter

chunks.section_title: describes the process for assessing requirements with regard to the installation of safety
chunks.unit_or_chapter: 6. describes the process for assessing requirements with regard to the installation of safety
chunks.text:
6 describes the process for assessing requirements with regard to the installation of safety
barriers in areas such as highway edge lines, bridges and retaining walls, median and lateral
separating islands and tunnels. Chapter 7 describes the required lengths for areas such as
highway edge lines and bridges. Chapter 8 describes the design of safety barriers’ interruption
areas. Chapter 9 describes the requirements for leading and trailing terminals of safety barriers. Chapter 10 lists the requirements of crash cushions. Chapter 11 outlines the criteria for
selecting the appropriate transitions between different safety barriers performance types. Chapter 12 outlines the requirements for installing temporary safety barriers. Chapter 13
describes the requirements for the protection of motorcyclists, in terms of additional structures
on steel restraint systems and the installation of safety barriers with declared additional
protection. Chapter 14 describes safety barriers’ installation details, in terms of installation
height and horizontal/vertical tolerances under various critical conditions. Chapter 15
describes construction procedures and requirements for certification and quality control for
the installation of in-situ concrete barriers. Chapter 16 describes aspects related to installation,
assembly and internal controls of VRS. Chapter 17 describes requirements related to
qualitative characteristics of various VRS types in terms of addressing concurrently desired
road safety levels, availability, quality of material and performance, factory support, repair and
replacement capabilities. Chapter 18 describes test conditions, performance classes and type
selection criteria for passive safety poles and posts.

work zone traffic control on highways, with guidelines. The Volume summarizes engineering
knowledge, practices and provides typical methods of typical traffic control applications. In Chapter 2 (General Work Area Requirements/Fundamentals) general principles on which the
Work Zone Design should be based are mentioned. Additionally, it deals with the categories
of the highway network, the criteria for determining the clear zone, and the ways of its
protection. In Chapter 3 (Signs) there are references to the signing equipment, the position
and their installation methods. In Chapter 4 (Work Zone Principles) the taper, the way of
separating - defining the work area from the traffic by using the appropriate devices, the
control of traffic in the work zone area, as well as the procedures for setting up, maintenance
and clearance of a work area and its devices are defined. In Chapter 5 (Markings) the
application of temporary marking in the Work Zone, the criteria for the design as well as the
types and methods needed for the improvement of traffic conditions are defined. In Chapter

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 6. (Lighting Devices) the necessary devices for improving safety and traffic conditions,
chunks.text:
6 (Lighting Devices) the necessary devices for improving safety and traffic conditions,
especially at night, are described. In Chapter 7 (Control of Traffic through Work Areas) the
23
SHC 101
methods for quick and safe detour of traffic through work zones, depending on the
construction phases are mentioned. Chapter 8 (Selection and Use of Traffic Control Devices)
looks into the appropriate choice of traffic control devices according to the category of works
and the work zone area that traffic passes through. Finally, in Chapter 9 (Typical Methods of
Traffic Control through Work Areas) the parts where temporary traffic control zone is included
and the rules or methods for the placement of the appropriate device are described. 3.2.9. SHC 306 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Public Utilities,
Highway and Street Lighting, Control and Monitoring Devices
SHC 306 provides an extensive update of the existing KSA guidelines and manuals related to
traffic control and monitoring devices based on more recent design manuals and Standards. New modern technologies shall be introduced for the chapter on Control Monitoring Devices
based on the most recent guides and manuals provided in recent existing USA and European
Standards. The most recent NTCIP standard shall be used as a major reference guide. In
general, all existing standards need to be thoroughly reviewed. As a significant amount of time
has elapsed since previous updates it is expected that many current KSA references shall either
be withdrawn, inactive, or superseded. An initial step in the process would be to identify these
first so that replacements and/or alternatives can be found. Beyond this, over the years it
appears that certain British standards and European norms have been introduced.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 6. (Lighting Devices) the necessary devices for improving safety and traffic conditions,
chunks.text:
3.2.10. SHC 307 – Highway Facilities and Utilities Design – Landscape Planting,
Outdoor Advertising
SHC 307 includes 2 issues. The first issue discusses landscape planting. The second issue
discusses outdoor advertising. More specifically, the first issue addresses the following:
•
Planting Design Guidelines. •
Landscape Design Considerations. •
Landscape Design Classification. •
Landscape Design Objectives and key principles. •
Site Evaluation and Assessment. •
Landscape Issues in the Highway Design Concept Stage. •
Landscape For Specific Highway Situations. 24
SHC 101
•
Planting and Protection of existing Vegetation. In the second issue Outdoor Advertising the scope of the present instructions is to establish a
framework concerning the demand for outdoor roadside advertising sign installation, which
will be applied throughout the entire country. These instructions intend to provide all the
necessary information concerning the control and management of outdoor advertising
without having to impose difficult conditions on the advertisers during the design, the
installation, the maintenance and the removal of the advertising signs. Chapter 2 (General Landscape Design Guidelines) gives detailed information regarding to
landscape design considerations, landscape design classification and key principles, site
evaluation and assessment, as well as landscape issues in the highway concept design stage. In Chapter 3 (Landscape for Specific Highway Situations) a brief description about planting for
specific highway situations - detailed landscape design guidelines are given.

chunks.text:
Chapter 4
(Irrigation System Guidelines) gives a brief and concise description about primary, secondary,
tertiary irrigation systems, irrigation control systems and the principles that should be
employed for the design of irrigation systems. In Chapter 5 (Planting & Protection of Existing
Vegetation) is provided summary information on planting design guidelines, establishment of
vegetation, soil and soil amendments, plant materials and procedures and construction,
maintenance and mitigation. Chapter 6 (Outdoor Advertising & Signage: General Design
Guidelines) refers to the general principles, the kind and the conditions on which Outdoor
Advertising should be based. It also refers to the cases that cause driver’s distraction and
solutions to avoid it. Chapter 7 (Placement of Advertising Signs) refers to the regulations for
the placement of advertising signs. Chapter 8 (Reduction of Outdoor Advertising Effects) deals
with the methods of reducing traffic consequences due to outdoor advertising and describes
the rules of outdoor advertising in order not to disturb the people or the environment where
they are placed. Finally, Chapter 9 (Special Cases of Advertising) refers to cases of outdoor
advertising such as landmark signs, off-premises CEVMS advertising devices etc. and it also
describes the special conditions for outdoor advertising in areas for industrial or commercial
uses.

chunks.text:
3.2.11. SHC 308 – Pavement Design
The scope of this Volume is to provide a pavement design engineer with sufficient information
and guidelines, so that the necessary input data can be developed, and proper engineering
principles applied to design new pavements or develop a properly engineered pavement
rehabilitation project. Chapter 2 provides the pavement design components, such as resilience
and sustainability. The three basic types of pavements are presented, flexible, rigid and
composite. Pavement design methods and their basic design principles are also included. Chapter 3 covers the materials used for pavements. It includes subgrade materials, unbound
aggregates, stabilized aggregates, recycled aggregates, asphalt materials, asphalt mix
materials and Portland cement concrete. Chapter 4 refers to the traffic analysis considered in
the frame of pavement design. It includes the factors that should be taken into consideration
in order to estimate the cumulative traffic Volume in terms of axial loads for the design period
of the pavement. These factors are: traffic Volumes, vehicle classification, vehicle class
distribution, percentage of heavy vehicles, annual growth rate, growth factor, load equivalency
factor. Chapter 5 provides the framework for the design of new flexible pavements. Three
methods are presented: the empirical, the mechanistic-empirical and the fixed pavement cross
25
SHC 101
section. The basic principles, the design criteria, the material properties and the structural
design is included for each design method. A framework for the choice of the design method
is also included. Chapter 6 provides the framework for the design of new rigid pavements.

chunks.section_title: SHC 309 – Material Specifications and Standardized Testing
chunks.unit_or_chapter: 6. (Lighting Devices) the necessary devices for improving safety and traffic conditions,
chunks.text:
3.2.12. SHC 309 – Material Specifications and Standardized Testing
This Volume is intended to be used as a guide through the test procedures, sampling
requirements, and test equipment as described in the standards that are mentioned in all SHC
Volumes. It includes the following chapters:
•
Chapter 2. Pavement Material Standards – It includes the standards referring to soils,
aggregates, asphalt, asphalt mix, cement, concrete, geosynthetics, sealants and fillers,
seal design and other.
•
Chapter 3. Other Material Standards – It includes the standards referring to steel, other
metals, plastics, pavement markings, guardrail, sewer, drains, pipes, pigments, tunnels
and bridges, construction, road safety and other.
•
Chapter 4. Road Safety Standards – Such as restrain systems, road signs and other
materials related to road safety.
•
Chapter 5. Electrical and Communication Standards - It includes the standards of
materials related to electrical and electronic systems, lighting systems, fiber optic,
networks, communications and safety/emergency.
•
Chapter 6. Evaluation/Monitoring Standards of pavements.
•
Chapter 7. Noise and Vibrations Standards, measurement and evaluation.
•
Chapter 8: Autonomous Vehicles Standards - Such as information systems for land
transport, traffic, and other.
•
Section 9: Surveying and Mapping Standards.
•
Section 10: Other Standards - Such as standards relating to coatings, ambient
temperature, management and other.

3.2.13. SHC 310 – Bridges and Tunnels Design
This Volume deals with the design for bridges and tunnels and its purpose is to document the
policy on bridge and tunnel design in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (KSA). It assists bridge
designers in applying provisions documented in the AASHTO LRFD Bridge Design
Specifications, 2020 9th Edition, with modifications that suits to KSA (provided as separate
Appendix D), which designers should adhere to unless directed otherwise by this manual.
Chapters 2 to 6 refer to the design of bridges. More specifically, Chapter 2 covers the general
design guidelines (location features, clearance, design objectives, hydrology and hydraulics,
roadway drainage). Limits on states and loads are covered by Chapter 3.

in Chapter 5. Chapter 6 deals with the design of other structures related to bridges and Chapter

chunks.text:
3.2.14. SHC 401 – Construction of Highways
This Volume defines material standards and construction specifications and establishes quality
acceptance criteria for items of work to construct new highways or rehabilitate existing
highways, and pavement structures. These Specifications shall be an integral part of Contract
documents for highway structures projects construction and will serve as the basis for review
and acceptance of all Contractor supplied materials and Contractor performed work. This volume is divided into 7 chapters. A brief outline of these chapters is given below:
•
Chapter 1. Introduction – This chapter provides general and project requirements
including specifications for acceptability of materials furnished and works performed
for measurement and payment. •
Chapter 2. Earthworks – This chapter provides the requirements for earthworks. It
covers work items for excavation, backfill, and subgrade construction of pavements. •
Chapter 3. Stabilization Works – This chapter covers the requirements for stabilization
works pertaining to slope protection and reinforcement, retaining walls, and material
specifications for supporting these works. •
Chapter 4. Subbase, Aggregate Base, and Base Courses – This chapter provides the
requirements for materials and construction of both virgin and recycled unbound
granular subbase/base courses and stabilized base courses including the incorporation
of geosynthetics. •
Chapter 5. Asphalt Pavement Courses – This chapter provides several requirements
related to materials and construction of the various asphalt courses, namely base,
binder, and surface courses. It covers specifications for a variety of asphalt mixtures,
virgin and recycled, including several asphalt mixture technologies for wearing courses. •
Chapter 6.

construct highway facilities. Chapter 2 provides the materials, labor, and the required
equipment to construct the various types of culverts, storm sewers, manholes, inlets and storm
drainage structures. Chapter 3 provides the materials, construction techniques, evaluation
techniques, acceptance criteria, measurement, and payment of highway related facilities such
as guardrails, impact attenuator systems, concrete curbs and gutters, sidewalks and paths,
fencing, pavement markings, noise barriers, barriers for sand dunes, cable barriers, concrete
barriers and safety barriers. Chapter 4 provides the materials, construction techniques,
evaluation techniques, acceptance criteria, measurement and payment related to lighting of
the highways (conduits and junctions, cables, light poles). Chapter 5 provides the materials,
construction techniques, evaluation techniques, acceptance criteria, measurement and
payment for the control and monitoring devices. Chapter 6 provides the construction
requirements of the landscaping, as well as aspects related to maintenance, the evaluation
techniques, the acceptance criteria and measurement and payment. Chapter 7 provides
construction related issues for utilities. Aspects referring to control and acceptance of materials
and work, measurement and payment are also discussed.

3.2.17. SHC 501 – Pavement Maintenance Management Systems
The main objective of Volume SHC 501 is to provide guidelines and specifications code related
to developing a Pavement Maintenance Management system (PMMS). The guidelines
considered all types of pavements: flexible, rigid, and unpaved roads. It distinguishes between
Urban and Rural roads. It took into consideration the latest development and technology
internationally and in the region. It also utilized the local manuals, equipment, and practices
of different highway agencies. The scope of this Volume does not include Highway Asset
Management and specification of pavement maintenance activities. Volume SHC 501 is divided into 8 chapters and three appendices. The Introduction chapter
outlined the scope, and reference standards, of this part of the Code. Chapter 2 is an overview
of pavement management system (PMS) and pavement maintenance management system
(PMMS). Explanation of PMMS elements, components, levels, and benefits. It includes urban
and rural PMMS. Network referencing systems were presented in Chapter 3. The general
parameters and extent of highway inventory data, highway classification for inventory
purposes, method of inventory data, and types of data required for PMMS were illustrated in
Chapter 4. Chapter 5 provides all the technical information needed for pavement condition
28
SHC 101
evaluation. It includes the functional and structural assessment types of pavement condition:
manual and automated inspection, pavement roughness, skid resistance, structural capacity,
ground penetration radar, and coring test. It also includes condition evaluation for flexible
pavement, rigid pavement, and unpaved roads in urban and rural areas.

3.2.18. SHC 502 – Bridges and Tunnels Maintenance and Management Systems
The scope of this Volume is to provide guidelines to serve as a resource for use in developing
specific policy and procedures for the inspection, evaluation, maintenance and management
of existing in-service highway bridges.
Chapter 2 includes information regarding the bridge maintenance management framework
and Chapter 3 discusses issues related to bridge condition assessment inspection. Details
about bridge elements inspection and evaluation are provided in Chapter 4, while Chapter 5
contains the methods for bridge repair and strengthening. Tunnel condition assessment
inspection (types, methods, procedures, planning, health and safety and equipment) is covered
in Chapter 6, whilst Chapter 7 is related to tunnel elements inspection and evaluation. Methods
of tunnel repair is discussed in Chapter 8, and Chapter 9 deals with concrete culvert
maintenance management.

3.2.19. SHC 503 – Highway Facilities Maintenance and Management Systems
This Volume is to be utilized for aspects related to highway facilities monitoring, maintenance,
and management. Chapter 2 provides an overview of the highway facilities management
process. It covers the components of Pavement Management Systems including, the elements,
key concepts, data analysis, and decision-making procedures. Chapter 3 provides an extensive
list of distresses for the various structures associated with highway facilities. Both images and
figures are provided to describe the distresses. Chapter 4 contains utilized monitoring
techniques as well as some emerging technologies that will likely be implemented in the near
future. Chapter 5 contains all the techniques to repair the distresses for highway facility
structures. Specifications are provided for both construction and quality assurance of these
maintenance techniques.

3.2.20. SHC 601 – Traffic Engineering
SHC 601 provides requirements related to traffic engineering aspects in the planning, design
and operation of highways. This code consists of an introduction and five chapters, as follows:
29
SHC 101
•
Chapter 2. Traffic Operations Studies - This chapter provides comprehensive guidance
with respect to the implementation of traffic operation studies and for the purpose of
traffic management and traffic control within KSA’s highway and road network. In
addition, this chapter provides guidelines for the establishment of a signal phasing and
timing program.
•
Chapter 3. Traffic Analysis & Modelling – This chapter contains guidance and general
requirements for the uniform application of traffic analysis tools on roadway corridors,
interchange, and intersection analyses.
•
Chapter 4. Electronic Signs in Traffic Management – This chapter provides guidelines
for the appropriate use of electronic signs for the implementation of traffic
management.
•
Chapter 5. Freight Transport Management – This chapter introduces a set of
management principles and guidelines aiming to improve freight movement both in
urban and rural areas.
•
Chapter 6. Smart Transport Networks Operations - provides guidance on traffic
operations of smart networks concerning road user services provided.

3.2.21. SHC 602 – Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices
This volume is to be utilized as a manual by designers, contractors, and authorities involved in
the selection, installation, and maintenance of traffic control devices. The process of traffic
control device selection consists of specifications, which are binding, recommended
guidelines, and some options, which may be considered exclusions in some cases, taking into
consideration the conditions prescribed in this manual. SHC 602 - Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices consists of 7 chapters. In the following
summary of these chapters. •
Chapter 1: Introduction: This chapter provides an overview of the chapters and the
scope of this volume of SHC and a full list of the specifications contained therein. •
Chapter 2: General Provisions: This chapter provides general provisions, including the
purpose and the requirements of traffic control devices and identifying the authorities
responsible for traffic control devices and their installation. •
Chapter 3: Signs. This chapter provides the standards, guidelines, and options, as well
as the clarifications that support in installation of the traffic signs on all types of
highways. •
Chapter 4: Markings: This chapter provides the standards, guidelines, and options, as
well as the clarifications that support installation of the markings on all types of
highways. •
Chapter 5: Signals: This chapter provides the standards, guidelines, and options, as well
as the clarifications that support installation of the traffic signals on all types of
highways. •
Chapter 6: Traffic Control for Special Zones: This chapter provides the standards,
guidelines, and options, as well as the clarifications that support all traffic control
devices for special zones on all types of highways.

•
Chapter 7: Traffic Control for Work Zones: This chapter provides the standards,
guidelines, and options, as well as the clarifications that support all control devices for
work zones on all types of highways. 30
SHC 101

3.2.22. SHC 603 – Road Safety
SHC 603 provides requirements related to road safety aspects in the design, construction,
maintenance and operation of highways. This volume is divided into 9 chapters. A brief outline of these chapters is given below:
•
Chapter 1. Introduction - This chapter provides an overview of the chapters, the scope
of the volume, and a full list of the standards referenced within the volume. •
Chapter 2. Data for Crash Analysis - This chapter defines the types and structure of
data that should be collected and used for the purposes of road safety analysis, namely
crash data (concerning each individual crash), roadway facility data, and traffic volume
data. •
Chapter 3. Road Safety Management - This chapter describes procedures and tools
that are used for monitoring and subsequently reducing crash frequency on existing
road networks. It includes Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs) appropriate for
monitoring road safety in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and guides the reader through
the processes of Network Screening, Road Safety Diagnosis, Countermeasure
Selection, Economic Appraisal of Safety Interventions, and Project Prioritization. •
Chapter 4. Road Safety Audits and Inspections - This chapter provides comprehensive
guidance and instructions on performing and managing Road Safety Audits of road
project design (at various stages) and Road Safety Inspections of existing roads. •
Chapter 5. Road Safety Analysis - This chapter describes analytical approaches
applicable to the investigation of potential road safety problems, with emphasis on the
Highway Safety Manual predictive method (AASHTO). •
Chapter 6.

3.2.23. SHC 701 – Environmental Aspects of Highways
SHC 701 is a suite of environmental codes comprising requirements, specifications, and advice
associated with the assessment, mitigation, reporting, and monitoring of the impacts of
highway Projects on natural and man-made resources. SHC 701 applies to all-purpose
highways and permeates the activities of the entire Project cycle, including conceptual,
planning, design, construction, operation, and maintenance. SHC 701 applies in conjunction
with KSA relevant legislation, where the latter prevails over the former. Chapter 2. Standardizes
a methodology for the assessment, mitigation, reporting, and monitoring of the environmental
31
SHC 101
impacts of highway Projects, in a manner aligned with the provisions of the relevant KSA
Regulation, including requirements, specifications, and advice. Chapters 3 to 11 apply the
methodology established in Chapter 2 for the assessment, mitigation, reporting, and
monitoring of the impacts of highway Projects on natural and man-made resources, by
comprising requirements, specifications, and advice in consistency with the KSA Regulations,
on matters addressed by the Regulations, and by providing supplementary guidance, on
matters not covered by them.

3.2.24. SHC 801 – Autonomous Vehicles Requirements
SHC 801 provides a holistic approach on the requirements of Autonomous Vehicles (Avs)
within the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (KSA). It gathers significant information on the full cycle
of operating Connected Automated Vehicles (CAVs) from a legal, technical, and piloting
perspective to be used holistically, given the dynamic changes this sector drastically
undergoes. It serves as guidelines for new initiatives on relevant infrastructure, operation, and
legal framework for stakeholders. It is intended to be used as a first step in a complex and
dynamically changed domain of AVs and as a reference for pilot demonstrations. Hence, it
may be used as reference based on AV/CAV analysis. To address the gap in knowledge, incomplete Laws and Decrees, and best practices in this
space, this Volume presents a dynamic character of the multi-level components in this field
through recent experience from global initiatives. Those initiatives have already become de
facto procedures followed by global and national Authorities, Regulatory Bodies,
Standardization Bodies, Research Institutes and Academia, Original Equipment Manufacturers
(OEMs), research project consortia, entrepreneur technical projects, and other relevant
Connected and Automated Driving (CAD) parties. SCH 801 - Autonomous Vehicles Requirements consists of 5 chapters. A brief outline of these
chapters is given below:
•
Chapter 1: Introduction – This chapter provides an overview of chapters, the scope of
the volume, and the applicable legal frameworks, and summarizes the problems of
insurance, liability, and privacy.

levels of automaton and supporting infrastructure for the classification of automated
driving. It also refers to the safety standards and presents the five subsections of the
operational infrastructure. •
Chapter 5: Guidelines for Launching Pilot Projects – This chapter presents the seven
pilot use cases and seven pilot examples. It provides a concise guide with required
steps to be followed by autonomous vehicles implementers.

chunks.text:
3.3. Overlapping Across SHC Volumes and Different
Domains
A list of overlapping elements across the SHC has been identified and listed. The overall
objective is to ensure no duplicity of the themes that will be covered and ensures there are no
gaps and missed topics that have not been analyzed. A list of the potential overlapping elements identified include, but are not limited to:
•
Planning:
o Highway classification. o Highway capacity and Level of Service (LoS). o Non-motorized users (pedestrians and bicyclists). o On-Road Public Transport. •
Surveying and mapping
•
Preliminary studies:
o Traffic (traffic impact studies). o Reconnaissance Studies and Investigations. o Economic analysis and consideration. •
Highway geometric design:
o Design Considerations (geometric features, predesign elements)
o Speed. •
Hydrology and hydraulic:
o Detailed studies. o Drainage. •
Rest areas and truck inspection stations:
o Detailed studies and design considerations. o Parking. •
Passive safety:
o Safety barriers and passive poles, roadside features. •
Work zones:
o Design considerations. o Traffic control and safety. •
Public utilities, lighting, control and monitoring devices:
o Public utilities design. o Lighting
33
SHC 101
o Traffic control and monitoring devices. •
Landscape and outdoor advertising:
•
Pavement design:
o Pavement materials and layers. o Pavement Structural design. •
Design of bridges and tunnels:
o Design considerations. o Traffic and safety. o Materials and foundation. •
Highways construction:
o Earthwork and stabilization. o Pavement construction. •
Bridges and tunnels construction. •
Utilities construction. •
Highways maintenance:
o Pavement assessment. o Inspection Plan.

chunks.text:
3.4. The SHC Website
WP IV addresses the development of the official website for the Saudi Highway Code
specification, capable of handling advanced, interactive, and electronic services for the
guidelines and specification documents to be produced, as outlined in the Scope of Work,
while providing the appropriate electronic data security protection to the highest standards in
accordance with the MoTLS Secure Coding Standard 2022.
The following components have been implemented:
•
A public section, introducing the final Saudi Highway Code volumes to the engineering
community in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and providing electronic services to acquire
original Code documents, either in the (original) English language or the translated
Arabic version.
•
Memberships, discounts, and communication with users.
Furthermore, the following features were already implemented:
•
Document Rights Management.
•
E-commerce Module (if required).
•
Payment (if required).
•
Codes Management.
•
Users Management.
•
Technical Inquiries.
•
Notes.
Figure 3-2, Figure 3-3 and Figure 3-4 present the SHC website introductory page, the SHC
Website Sign-in/Register screen and the SHC Website - SHC Volumes’ Presentation Screen,
respectively.

chunks.text:
4. Glossary of Terms
(3R) - 3R projects are those which address pavement needs and/or deficiencies and which
substantially follow the existing horizontal and vertical alignment, for the basic purposes of
resurfacing, restoration or rehabilitation. (4R) - 4R projects usually represent the highest type design since these are either new
highways or almost totally reconstructed highway sections. (5R) - 5R projects refer to mobility corridors that are intended to generate, or produce new,
very long-term transportation opportunities that may include multiple modes such as rail,
utilities, freight and passenger characteristics. These modes may occur within a single corridor
alignment. 0-10V Dimming - A unidirectional analog lighting control protocol. Abandoned or Abandonment - In the past, this term was used to denote utility facilities that
were deactivated or taken out-of-service. As the connotation of this term implied that there
was no further responsibility for the facility, this term is no longer used. The Utility is still
responsible for any utility facility that is deactivated or taken out-of-service unless otherwise
agreed to, in writing, by the MoTLS KSA and the Utility. Absorbed Gas Mat (AGM) - A type of battery that is designed for delivering powerful bursts
of starting amps and running electronics for a long time. Absorptive Noise Barrier - A noise barrier that has an absorptive lining. Acceleration Severity Index - Dimensionless parameter that assesses the impact against the
VRS. Acceptable Quality Level (AQL) - The level of Lot percent defective at/or below which the
work is considered to be satisfactory. Acceptance Plan - A prescribed method of sampling, measuring and testing together with
criteria for the acceptability of a lot of material or construction.

chunks.text:
Acceptance Program - All factors comprise the agency's determination of the degree of
compliance with contract requirements and value of a product. These factors include agency
or agency supervised sampling, testing, measuring, and inspection. These factors should also
include validated results of Contractor sampling and testing. Accepted Gap - Gap that a minor-street vehicle uses to move into the major street
Accepted Lag - Lag that a minor-street vehicle uses to move into the major street. Accepted Method of Analysis - A method of analysis that requires no further verification and
that has become a regular part of structural engineering practice. Access - Permission, liberty, or ability to enter, approach, or to make use of. Access (Mode) - A feeder mode to the principal mode of transportation; for example, walking
and park-and-ride. Access Control - The condition whereby the rights of owners or occupants of land abutting
the highway right-of-way, or other persons, to access, light, air, or view in connection with a
highway is controlled by the MoTLS KSA by law. 39
SHC 101
Access Point - An unsignalized intersection, driveway, or opening on either side of a highway. Accessibility - The percentage of the populace able to complete a selected trip within a
specified time. Accessible Parking - Parking facilities for the disabled or mobility-impaired individuals. Accessible Pedestrian Signal - A device that communicates information about pedestrian
signal timing in non-visual format such as audible tones, speech messages, and/or vibrating
surfaces. Accessible Pedestrian Signal Detector - A device designated to assist the pedestrian who
has visual or physical disabilities in activating the pedestrian phase.

chunks.text:
Accommodation - The installation of utility facilities along or across right-of-way with the
intent that they will occupy and jointly use the right-of-way. Accuracy - The degree of agreement between a measurement, calculation, or specification
and an accepted reference value or a standard. Acoustical Planning - Controlling future noise by planned measures, such as land-use
planning, systems Engineering for traffic, traffic planning, abatement by sound-insulation
measures, and noise control of sources. Action Plans - A Plan designed as part of EMP to detail the management of effects on any of
the environmental aspects throughout construction and operation/maintenance of a highway;
Action Plan may be in the form of (a) Control Plan to identify measures to reduce impacts to
the environmental resources and human health and safety; (b) Contingency Plan to detail
precautionary and reactive procedures and equipment necessary to address the effects of
contingency (e.g., spills of chemical, oil, or hazardous materials, severe weather conditions,
dust storm, etc.) and to remediate; (c) Construction Work Method Statements (or similar) for
activities identified as high-risk or above (pre-treatment). Active Earth Pressure - Lateral pressure resulting from the retention of the earth by a
structure or component that is tending to move away from the soil mass. Activity - Any industrial, commercial, or service-related facility, Project, business, or other
activities that are expected to have an Environmental Impact. Actuated Signal Control - A control method that allows a variable sequence and variable
duration of signal displays depending on vehicle and pedestrian traffic demands.

chunks.text:
All vehicle
movements (phases) are actuated in 'fully-actuated control' in contrast with 'semi-actuated
control' where only minor vehicle movements (e.g. side road traffic) are actuated. Actuation - A detection of a roadway user that is forwarded to the controller by a detector. Acute Impact - Impact that occurs as a result of a severe, usually transient, event. For road
runoff, acute pollution may be the result from a spillage of pollutants (most commonly) or
from routine runoff. Adaptive Signal Control - An advanced signal system that does not operate with time-of-
day plans. Adaptive Traffic Control System - These systems respond automatically to fluctuations in
traffic flow through the use of on-street detectors embedded in the road. Addendum - A written amendment or revision to the Contract Documents or plans issued to
40
SHC 101
Bidders subsequent to the date of advertisement and prior to the final date and time for
submission of Tenders indicated in the "Instructions to Tenderer."
Additional Plans - The Contract plans may be supplemented, from time to time, by the
Engineer, with additional plans to illustrate particular or special parts of the Work. These
additional plans shall be considered as having the same effect as the contract plans in the
control and execution of the Contract. These additional plans will not change the Work of the
Contract but will explain it. Adits - Small size tunnel excavated for investigation of the rock condition before design of the
tunnel support. It shall be open or close at the end. Generally, it is horizontal or inclined. Adjacent Electrical Service - Any section of roadway or any area with electrical service
location within one mile of the Project, such that electrical service can be delivered to the
project site.

chunks.text:
Adjustment - The required modification to an existing utility facility to eliminate a conflict
with a proposed highway/structure element on an active project where the utility facility will
generally be retained in the same location. Admixture (Additive) - A substance or agent used in a mixture, such as concrete, asphalt
mixes, or clay, to provide a specific characteristic and intensify the performance of the mixture. They can be pozzolith, anti-stripping agents, calcium chloride or sodium chloride, etc. Advanced Driver Assistance Systems - Systems designed to help the driver with the driving
task e.g., in-vehicle navigation systems and collision warning systems. Advanced Traffic Management - A variety of strategies to more efficiently manage traffic on
existing facilities, including managed lanes and ITS systems. Advanced Traffic Management System - Systems that seek to reduce traffic congestion (in
urban environments) by improving the efficiency of utilization of existing infrastructures. Advanced Transportation Management System (ATMS) - Systems of detection,
communication, and software technologies that are aimed to reduce traffic congestion. Advertisement for Bids or Notice to Contractors - A public announcement inviting bids for
work to be performed or materials to be furnished, as required by law. Advertising Sign - They are the signs used for guidance or self-announcement, whether by
writing, engraving, pasting, or through an electronic screen or other technologies, and they
are freestanding or connected to a facility or on a vehicle. Advisory Speed - A recommended speed for all vehicles operating on a section of highway
and based on the highway design, operating characteristics and conditions. Aeration - Introduction of air into a material.

Adjustment - The required modification to an existing utility facility to eliminate a conflict
with a proposed highway/structure element on an active project where the utility facility will
generally be retained in the same location. Admixture (Additive) - A substance or agent used in a mixture, such as concrete, asphalt
mixes, or clay, to provide a specific characteristic and intensify the performance of the mixture. They can be pozzolith, anti-stripping agents, calcium chloride or sodium chloride, etc. Advanced Driver Assistance Systems - Systems designed to help the driver with the driving
task e.g., in-vehicle navigation systems and collision warning systems. Advanced Traffic Management - A variety of strategies to more efficiently manage traffic on
existing facilities, including managed lanes and ITS systems. Advanced Traffic Management System - Systems that seek to reduce traffic congestion (in
urban environments) by improving the efficiency of utilization of existing infrastructures. Advanced Transportation Management System (ATMS) - Systems of detection,
communication, and software technologies that are aimed to reduce traffic congestion. Advertisement for Bids or Notice to Contractors - A public announcement inviting bids for
work to be performed or materials to be furnished, as required by law. Advertising Sign - They are the signs used for guidance or self-announcement, whether by
writing, engraving, pasting, or through an electronic screen or other technologies, and they
are freestanding or connected to a facility or on a vehicle. Advisory Speed - A recommended speed for all vehicles operating on a section of highway
and based on the highway design, operating characteristics and conditions. Aeration - Introduction of air into a material.

Agglomeration - Part of a territory, delimited and determined by the KSA as an urbanized
area, based on population and population density criteria. Aggregate - Granular material used as a construction material, meeting the requirements of
road and paving applications. Aggregate Gradation - The particle distribution of aggregate among standard sizes,
determined by sieve analysis and expressed in terms of cumulative percentages larger or
smaller than each of a series of sieve openings. 41
SHC 101
Aggregate Types - The aggregate types are: 1) Coarse Aggregate, 2) Fine Aggregate and 3)
Mineral Filler. Aggregate Working Load Limit - A calculation based on a summation of the working load
limits or restraining capacity of all devices used to secure an article on a vehicle. Air Entraining Admixture - A functional addition that will entrain air in mortar or concrete. Air Pollutants - Presence of one or more substances in certain quantities or characteristics,
over a period of time, that directly or indirectly lead to damaging the air quality. Air Quality - Air characteristics, assessed according to the standards and norms set by the
competent authority to protect the environment and human health. Aisle - A circulation route for vehicular traffic through a parking area, site or property, and
may connect to a through vehicle passage. Algorithm - A set of rules for solving a problem in a finite number of steps. In simulation, it is
a set of rules that describe a particular component of driver behavior (for example, car-
following, lane-changing, or gap-acceptance). Alignment Load (AL) - The nominal minimum load applied to an anchor during testing to
keep the testing equipment positioned correctly.

chunks.text:
Alligator Cracking - Interconnected or interlaced cracks in an asphalt pavement surface
resembling an alligator's skin or chicken wire. It is caused by heavy wheel loads and excessive
deflection of the surface over unstable subgrade or lower courses of the pavement. All-Lane-Running - The scheme under which the hard shoulder is removed and converted
into a running lane. All-way STOP-Controlled (AWSC) Intersection - An intersection with STOP signs on all
approaches. The driver’s decision to proceed is based on a consensus of right-of-way
governed by the traffic conditions of the other approaches and the rules of the road (e.g., the
driver on the right has the right-of-way if two vehicles arrive simultaneously). Almanac - It is included in the navigational message transmitted by GNSS satellites, contains
less accurate orbital information than ephemerides and is valid for a period of up to 90 days. Alternative Intersection - An intersection created by rerouting one or more movements
(often left turns) from their usual places to secondary junctions. Ambient Air - Atmospheric air where all life activities occur, not including indoor air. Ambient Noise - Ambient noise is the total background sound in a given situation at a given
time, usually composed of sound from many sources, near and far, but excluding site noise. Ambient noise plus site noise gives total noise. Ambient Water Quality - The properties of ambient water, which are evaluated based on the
standards and requirements set by the Ministry of Environment, Water and Agriculture. Ambient Water Quality Standards - The standards for Coastal Water, Surface Water and
Ground Water set out in Appendix (1) to the Executive Regulation for the Protection of
Aqueous Media for Pollution.

42
SHC 101
American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials - An organization of
highway engineers that develops standards and publishes specifications, test protocols and
guides for use by transportation agencies throughout the United States. American Society for Testing and Materials - A national organization that develops uniform
methods and standards for product quality. American Wire Gauge (AWG) - US standard used by MnDOT to measure wire conductor
sizes. Anchor Devices - The anchor head wedges or nuts that grip the prestressing steel. Anchor Head - The device by which the prestressing force is permanently transmitted from
the prestressing steel to the bearing plate. Anchor Point - Part of the structure, fitting or attachment on a vehicle or cargo to which a tie
down is attached. Anchorage - The combined system of anchor head, bearing plate, trumpet and anchorage
corrosion protection that is used to transmit the prestressing force from the prestressing steel
to the surface of the ground or the supported structure. Annoyance - The degree of community noise annoyance as determined by means of field
surveys. Annual Average Daily Traffic (AADT) - Annual average daily traffic is the total volume of
traffic observed on a point or segment of a highway in both directions over a period of one
year divided by the number of days in a year. Antenna Phase Center - The apparent source of radiation on a GNSS receiver. The position
of the antenna phase center is not necessarily the geometric center of the antenna. Anticipated Pedestrian Activity (Medium) - Areas where less numbers (10 to 100
pedestrians an hour) of pedestrians utilize the streets at night.

Typical are downtown office
areas, blocks with libraries, neighborhood shopping, parks, and area with transit lines. Anti-glare Screen - A screen designed to block blinding headlight glare from opposing traffic
lanes. Anti-Parking Bollard - Constructions designed with the function of limiting parking,
especially on sidewalk areas and paths, bollards are small objects made usually of high-impact
resistant materials such as concrete or metal. They come in a variety of shapes and sizes to fit
the aesthetic needs of an area. Apparent Earth Pressure - Lateral pressure distribution for anchored walls constructed from
the top down. Apparent Opening Size - A property which indicates the approximate largest particle that

right-turn movements from a given direction. 43
SHC 101
Approach Grade - The grade of an intersection approach, expressed as a percentage (with
positive values for upgrade and negative for downgrade). Approach Speed - The speed at which vehicles would pass unimpeded through the

facility or system, whether primary or secondary to its function. Aqueous Media - Water bodies and Water resources. Aquifer - Rock layers with porosity and permeability that enable water to infiltrate and be
stored.

chunks.text:
Architecture - The organizational structure of a system, identifying its components, their
interfaces, and a concept of execution among them. Area of Influence - The geographical area surrounding the site from which a proposed
development is likely to draw a high percentage of the total site traffic. Area of Influence (AOI) (Traffic Analysis) - The area within certain boundary limits
established to accurately capture the prevailing traffic operating characteristics for a given area
of interest. Arterial - A signalized street that primarily serves through traffic and that secondarily provides
access to abutting properties. Arterial Highway - A general term denoting a highway primarily used by through traffic,
usually on a continuous route or a highway designated as part of an arterial system. Arterial Management - Applies Country and local planning, capital, and regulatory and
management tools to enhance and/or preserve the transportation functions of the arterial
roadway through the use of surveillance devices, advanced signal algorithms, and
coordination. Arterial Rural - A rural arterial highway refers to roadways consisting of two lanes in the cross-
section, one for each direction of travel. Occasionally, passing lanes may be added to one or
two sides of the highway extending the cross-section to three or four lanes at those locations. In this entry, two-lane highways strictly refer to roadways in rural areas meeting the previous
definition and do not include urban and suburban streets. For higher traffic volumes multilane
rural highways could be designed with two or more traffic lanes per direction, to upgrade the
provided level of service.

A central median could be placed in between two directions, while
leveled intersections are usually applied in connections with other roads. The average running
speed for off-peak conditions is between 60 and 110 km/h. Article (Cargo) - A unit of cargo, other than a liquid or gaseous cargo, that includes articles
grouped together so that they can be handled as a single unit or unitized by wrapping,
strapping, banding or edge protection device(s). Artificial Intelligence (AI) - The science and engineering of making intelligent machines,
especially intelligent computer programs. As-Built Plans - Plans that show the state of the bridge at the end of construction; usually
prepared by the Contractor the resident Engineer. 44
SHC 101
Aspect (Environmental) - Any aspect taken into consideration in the EIA such as Geology and
Soils, Water quality, Biodiversity, Noise, Air quality, Landscape, Cultural heritage, etc. Aspect Ratio - Ratio of the length to the width of a rectangle. Asphalt - A dark brown to black cement-like residuum obtained from the distillation of
suitable crude oils, naturally occurring sources, or combinations thereof. Asphalt Binder - Also referred to as asphalt cement binder or asphalt cement, is produced
from the thick residue that remains after distillation of petroleum. It is the modified or non-
modified asphalt cement, liquid asphalt, and emulsified asphalt material used in producing
asphalt concrete in order to meet the specifications for paving purposes. Asphalt Cement - See Asphalt. Asphalt Concrete - Also referred to as asphalt or blacktop, is a composite mixture that consists
of aggregate and asphalt binder. It is the most commonly used surface material for the
construction of roads, highways, airports, parking lots, and other types of pavements.

chunks.text:
Asphalt Emulsion - A mixture of processed asphalt cement and water mixed with an
emulsifying agent producing a suspension of minute globules of asphalt material in water or
in an aqueous solution, or (2) a suspension of minute globules of water or of an aqueous
solution in a liquid asphalt material. Asphalt Overlay - A paving course applied to an existing asphalt surface to repair any created
cracking or potholing, while still not being damaged enough to need replacement. Asphalt Pavement - A structure consisting of one or more prepared courses of asphalt mix
atop one or more supporting courses of unbound, modified, or treated subgrade, subbase or
base materials. Asphalt Surface Treatment - An application of asphalt material followed by a layer of mineral
aggregate. Multiple applications of asphalt material and mineral aggregate may be used. ATR Site - The location of an automated traffic recorder (permanent station) used for
collecting traffic volume data. Audit Brief - The instructions to the Audit Team defining the scope and details of the road or
development project to be audited, including sufficient information for the audit to be
undertaken. Audit Feedback Form - The formal report that includes the response to the Audit Report
findings, by the Design Team and the Project Manager - also "RSA Feedback Form" or "Audit
Response Report". Audit Report - The formal report produced by the Audit Team describing the road safety
deficiencies identified by the team and the recommended solutions to these deficiencies - also
"RSA report". Audit Team - A team that works together on all aspects of the audit, independent of the
Design Team and approved for an audit by the relevant road authority on behalf of the Client.

chunks.text:
Audit Team Leader - A person with the appropriate training, skills and experience who is
approved for an audit by the relevant road authority on behalf of the Client as the Audit Team
Leader. The Audit Team Leader has overall responsibility for carrying out the audit, managing
the Audit Team and signing the report. 45
SHC 101
Audit Team Member - A person with the appropriate training, skills and experience who is
approved for an audit by the relevant road authority on behalf of the Client as an audit team
member, reporting to the Audit Team Leader. Audit Team Observer - A person with the appropriate training, skills and experience
accompanying the Audit Team to observe and gain experience of the audit procedure. Automated Driving - Driving with an Automated Vehicle that is capable of operating in clearly
defined automated mode(s) which can safely drive the vehicle in specified design domains
with limited or no human intervention. Automated Driving System - The hardware and software that are collectively capable of
performing the entire dynamic driving task on a sustained basis, regardless of whether it is
limited to a specific Operational Design Domain (ODD); this term is used specifically to
describe a level 3, 4, or 5 driving automation system. Automated Lane Keeping Systems - An autonomous driving system for low-speed (60 km/h
or less) application that uses sensing technology, to detect lane lines and maintain a
programmed distance between them or a set distance from one of them. Also referred to as
“traffic jam chauffeur”. Automated Parking System - Mechanical system designed to minimize the area and/or
volume required for parking cars.

chunks.text:
Automated Pavement Condition Survey - A survey used to provide optimal maintenance
and rehabilitation strategy by collecting pavement condition data, i.e., cracking, roughness,
rutting, texture, poor drainage, etc., and by using customized vehicles fitted with sensing
equipment that travel on the highway or near highway speeds. Automatic Passenger Counter - An automated system that counts the number of passengers
boarding and alighting a transit vehicle. The information may be used for later data analysis,
or for real-time activities, such as providing signal priority only to buses that are at least half
full. Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL) System - A system that determines the location of
vehicles carrying special electronic equipment that communicates a signal back to a central
control facility. AVLs are used for detecting irregularity in service and are often combined with
a computer-aided dispatch system. Automobile Leapfrog - The situation where two or more cars on a highway continually pass
each other as they vary their acceleration. Autonomous Vehicle - Also referred to as Driverless Vehicle or Self-driving Vehicle, is defined
as one that can operate itself and is capable of sensing its surrounding environment and
moving safely without any human intervention. It may include adaptive cruise control, active
steering (steer by wire), etc. Auxiliary Lane - An additional lane on a freeway to connect an on-ramp and an off-ramp. Avenue - A wide road with trees or tall buildings on both sides. Average Daily Traffic (ADT) - The total volume of traffic during a given time period (greater
than one day and less than a year) divided by the number of days.

chunks.text:
46
SHC 101
Average Day - A day representing traffic volumes normally and repeatedly found at a location,
typically a weekday when volumes are influenced by employment or a weekend day when
volumes are influenced by entertainment or recreation. Average Driver - A driver who behaves on the road responsibly and safely, perceiving and
reacting immediately to any change in traffic conditions. Average Travel Speed - The length of the highway segment divided by the average travel
time of all vehicles traversing the segment, including all stopped delay times. Award - The formal acceptance by the MoTLS KSA of a bid for highway construction work. A-weighting - A-weighting is an adjustment made to the sound pressure level measurement
to approximate the response of the human ear. Axle - A central supporting axis oriented transversely to the nominal direction of vehicle
motion that is fixed to the wheels, rotating with them, or fixed to the vehicle, with the wheels
rotating around the axle. Axle Correction Factor (ACF) - The factor developed to adjust axle counts into vehicle counts. ACF is developed from classification counts by dividing the total number of vehicles counted
by the total number of axles on these vehicles. Axle Loads - A design parameter in the engineering of roadways and railways that is defined
as the sum of the vertical static forces of the road surface in the contact area on the wheels of
any axle. Axle Unit - Single axle or tandem axle. Back Slope - In cuts, the slope from the bottom of the ditch to the top of cut. Backfill - Material used to replace or the act of replacing material removed during
construction; also, may denote material placed or the act of placing material adjacent to
structures.

SHC 101
Average Day - A day representing traffic volumes normally and repeatedly found at a location,
typically a weekday when volumes are influenced by employment or a weekend day when
volumes are influenced by entertainment or recreation. Average Driver - A driver who behaves on the road responsibly and safely, perceiving and
reacting immediately to any change in traffic conditions. Average Travel Speed - The length of the highway segment divided by the average travel
time of all vehicles traversing the segment, including all stopped delay times. Award - The formal acceptance by the MoTLS KSA of a bid for highway construction work. A-weighting - A-weighting is an adjustment made to the sound pressure level measurement
to approximate the response of the human ear. Axle - A central supporting axis oriented transversely to the nominal direction of vehicle
motion that is fixed to the wheels, rotating with them, or fixed to the vehicle, with the wheels
rotating around the axle. Axle Correction Factor (ACF) - The factor developed to adjust axle counts into vehicle counts. ACF is developed from classification counts by dividing the total number of vehicles counted
by the total number of axles on these vehicles. Axle Loads - A design parameter in the engineering of roadways and railways that is defined
as the sum of the vertical static forces of the road surface in the contact area on the wheels of
any axle. Axle Unit - Single axle or tandem axle. Back Slope - In cuts, the slope from the bottom of the ditch to the top of cut. Backfill - Material used to replace or the act of replacing material removed during
construction; also, may denote material placed or the act of placing material adjacent to
structures.

Backlight - The percent lamp lumens or the luminaire lumens distributed behind a luminaire
between zero degrees vertical (nadir) and 90 degrees vertical. Backplate - See Signal Backplate. Bandwidth - The maximum amount of green time for a designated coordinated movement
as it passes through a corridor at an assumed constant speed, typically measured in seconds. Banner - An advertisement graphic. Barrier - 1. Continuous road restraint system installed alongside the highway. 2. A reference
point in the sequence where two or more rings are interlocked. Barriers ensure that there will
be no concurrent selection and timing of conflicting movements in different rings. Rings along
with barriers fundamentally define how a controller organizes phases, so that compatible
phases can time together and conflicts do not occur. Base Capacity - The flow rate achievable under base conditions. Base capacity reflects ideal
conditions on a facility with no capacity-reducing effects. Base Case - The situation without the project in place. 47
SHC 101
Base Course - The layer of material immediately beneath the surface or intermediate course. It may be composed of crushed stone, crushed slag, crushed or uncrushed gravel and sand, or
of hot mix asphalt, typically with larger size aggregate. Base Free-Flow Speed - The potential free-flow speed based only on the highway’s horizontal
and vertical alignment, not including the impacts of lane widths, lateral clearances, median
type, and access points. Baseline - A GNSS baseline is established by two survey-quality GNSS receivers, with one at
each end of the line to be measured, occupying and collecting GNSS data on two points during
the same time - period.

implementation of the Project. Basic Freeway Segment - A length of freeway facility whose undersaturated operations are
unaffected by weaving, diverging, or merging. Basic Noise Level - The basic noise level (BNL) is a measure of source noise. Batch - It is a body of material in which the ingredients are uniformly blended together at one
time. Batch Plant - A manufacturing facility for producing asphalt paving mixtures in discrete
production batches. Each batch includes specific proportions, by weight, of aggregate, asphalt
binder, and other chosen additives. Each discrete batch is mixed and then discharged. Beacon - A highway traffic signal with one or more signal sections that operates in a flashing
mode. Bearing Plate - The steel plate that evenly distributes the ground anchor force to the
Structure. Bedding - Composition and shaping of soil or other suitable material to support a pipe,
conduit, casing, or utility tunnel. Benchmark - A point marked with a survey mark, serving as elevation reference, a vertical
control point. Betterment - The act or process of making or upgrading of a utility facility such that value,
capacity, performance, or convenience of maintenance to the utility system is better to that
which existed prior to construction of the transportation project. Bias - The ratio of mean to nominal value of a random variable. Bicycle - Vehicle with two wheels in tandem that is propelled by human power and is usually

chunks.text:
Bicycle Lane - A portion of a roadway that has been designated for preferential or exclusive
use by bicyclists by pavement markings and, if used, signs. Bidder and Tenderer - An individual, firm, partnership, corporation or any acceptable
combination thereof submitting a bid for the advertised work. 48
SHC 101
Big Data - The data that traditional data management systems cannot manage due to its size
and complexity. Bikeway - Any road, street, path, or way that is specifically designated as being open to bicycle
travel, regardless of whether such facilities are designed for the exclusive use of bicycles or are
to be shared with other transportation modes. Bill of Quantities - A document used in tendering in the construction industry that includes
a table in which materials, work items, their estimated quantities, units, individual and total
costs are itemized and is created after the design is complete. Binder Layer - The lower layer of the surface, consisting of a plant mix of graded aggregate
and bituminous material. Biodiversity - Variety and variability of plant, animal, or microscopic organisms; and the
variation between them. Bitumen - Also referred to as asphalt cement, is a black viscous material obtained naturally or
from petroleum refining that is characterized by waterproofing and adhesive properties. Bleeding (Asphalt) - A surface defect of an asphalt pavement that is described as the
appearance of a film of asphalt on the surface. It is caused by excessive asphalt binder in the
surface asphalt mix layer. Bleeding (Concrete) - The process where free water in the mix rises to the surface of fresh
concrete and forms a paste of cement. It occurs because of the settlement of coarse
aggregates that leads to the rising of free water.

chunks.text:
Blended Hydraulic Cement - A hydraulic cement consisting of two or more inorganic
constituents (at least one of which is not Portland cement or Portland cement clinker) which
separately or in combination contribute to the strength gaining properties of the cement
(made with or without other constituents, processing additions and functional additions, by
inter-grinding or other blending). Block Cracking - A type of cracking appearing in asphalt pavement surfaces that combines
longitudinal and transverse cracks and divides the pavement into approximately rectangular
pieces. It is typically caused by the shrinkage of the asphalt. Blowup - Localized upward slab movement and shattering of concrete at a joint or crack,
caused by compression stresses resulting from heat and water. Boarding Lost Time - Time spent waiting for passengers to travel from their waiting position
at the bus stop to the bus door. Bollard - A bollard is a sturdy, short, vertical post. Parking bollards are typically situated in the
center of a parking bay as a physical obstruction. They then fold either manually or
automatically to admit authorized users. These bollards are often used in smaller parking lots
such as visitor parking or corporate parking lots, as an alternative to boom gates. They can be
powered by a wide variety of means, with the most common being battery-powered or
electric-powered. Bond - A surety bond posted to ensure proper and complete construction and/or repair of a
facility and the affected rights-of-way pursuant to a permit. 49
SHC 101
Bond Length - The length of the ground anchor that is bonded to the ground and transmits
the tensile force to the soil or rock.

chunks.text:
Boom Barrier - A boom barrier, also known as a boom gate, is a bar, or pole pivoted to allow
the boom to block vehicular or pedestrian access through a controlled point. Boring - The operation by which holes are made or enlarged. Typically, with regards to
trenchless technology, bores are carved progressively ahead of the leading edge of the
advancing pipe as soil is conveyed back through the pipe. Boulder - A rock fragment, usually rounded by weathering or abrasion, which will be retained
on a seventy five millimeters sieve. Boulevard - A street or avenue, especially one that's very wide and lined with trees. Many
boulevards have a median strip of grass and sometimes trees. Boundary Conditions - Structural restraint characteristics regarding the support for and/or
the continuity between structural models. Breaklines - Linear strings compiled along the breaks or changes of grade in the terrain,
identifying terrain discontinuities such as ridges, toe of slopes, or any abrupt change in the
existing surface. Bridge - A structure including supports erected over a depression or an obstruction such as
water, highway, or railway; having a track or passageway for carrying traffic or other moving
loads; and having an opening measured along the center of the roadway of more than 6 m
between undercopings of abutments or spring lines of arches, or extreme ends of openings
for multiple boxes. It may also include multiple pipes, where the clear distance between
openings is less than half of the smaller contiguous opening. Bridge Agency - Bridge owner or bridge operator.

chunks.text:
Bridge Inspection Standards (BIS) - Federal regulations establishing requirements for
inspection procedures, frequency of inspections, a bridge inspection organization,
qualifications of personnel, inspection reports, and preparation and maintenance of bridge
inventory records. The NBIS apply to all structures defined as highway bridges located on or
over all public roads. Bridge Inventory (BI) - The aggregation of structure inventory and appraisal data collected
to fulfill the requirements of the National Bridge Inspection Standards. Bridge Length - The length of a bridge structure is the over-all length measured along the
line of survey stationing back-to-back of backwalls of abutments, if present; otherwise, end to
end of the bridge floor; but in no case less than the total clear opening of the structure. Bridge Management System (BMS) - A system designed to optimize the use of available
resources for the inspection, maintenance, rehabilitation, and replacement of bridges. Bridge Roadway Width - The clear width measured at right angles to the longitudinal
centerline of the bridge between the bottom of curbs or guard timber risers. BUG (Backlight, Uplight, Glare) Rating - The quantity of light within various luminaire beam
angles
50
SHC 101
Building Information Model - An Information model that represents structural elements
using 3D digital technology while accommodating them with a data source that contains
various related information about those elements. Bulkhead - A vertical barrier across a vehicle to prevent forward movement of cargo. Bumps and Sags - A small pavement surface that has sustained an upward displacement
caused due to the instability factor of pavement. Bundle - A group of articles of what has been unitized for securement as a single article.

chunks.text:
Bus - Self-propelled, rubber-tired road vehicle designed to carry a substantial number of
passengers and commonly operated on streets and highways. Bus Bulb - An extension of the sidewalk into the highway for passenger loading without the
buss pulling into the curb, gives priority to buses and eases re-entry into traffic. Bus Lane - Highway or street lane reserved primarily for buses during specified periods (may
be used by other traffic under certain circumstances, such as for making a right or left turn, or
by taxis, motorcycles, or carpools that meet the requirements in the traffic laws a specific
jurisdiction. Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) - Bus rapid transit is a public transportation strategy where buses
travel on exclusive right-of-way or receive preferential treatment at signalized intersections,
with the objective of reducing transit vehicle travel time and increasing reliability. Bypass / Flowby (Carry Over) - Occurs at an inlet on grade. It is the flow that is not captured
at an inlet on grade, bypasses, and is carried to the next inlet downgrade. Inlets on grade are
usually designed to allow a certain amount of flowby, unless located upstream of an area
where pedestrians are expected to use the street. Cab Shield - A vertical barrier placed directly behind the cab of a tractor to protect the cab in
the event cargo should shift forward. Cabinet - An all-weather enclosure that houses the intersection field equipment. Cable Barrier - A type of roadside or median safety traffic barrier. It consists of steel wire ropes
mounted on weak posts. Its primary purpose is to prevent a vehicle from leaving the traveled
way and striking a fixed object or terrain feature that is less forgiving than itself.

chunks.text:
Calculation of Road Traffic Noise (CRTN) - The technical memorandum that describes the
procedures for calculating noise from road traffic CRTN. Calendar Day - Every day shown on the calendar, beginning and ending at midnight. Calibrate - To determine settings of the plant which will provide correct proportions of the
components of plant-mixed materials. To compare with a standard or check the graduations
of a gauge or other measuring devices. Calibration - 1. The process of changing the inputs or configuration of a device or system to
improve the accuracy and precision of the estimate. 2. The process of adjusting data collection
technology to match a known ground-truth. 3. Changes in inputs to a traffic simulation model
to assure the outputs match field observations. 4. A process of adjusting the parameters in a
new standard to achieve approximately the same reliability as exists in a current standard or
specification or to achieve a target reliability index. 51
SHC 101
California Bearing Ratio - A measurement that is defined as the ratio of the load required to
force a calibrated piston into a soil specimen and the load required to penetrate a mass of
high-quality crushed stone with the same piston. It primarily expresses the support of a
crushed stone base or subgrade soil. Call - A request for service to an actuated controller. Some movements may have separate
calls for different user types. For example, a transit priority call is one where the normal phase
timing may be altered due to a call from a transit vehicle. More typical calls are vehicle calls
and pedestrian calls. Camber - A slight arch designed or built into a structure to compensate for the natural
deflection after loading.

chunks.text:
Cameras and Monitoring Devices - Devices such as CCTV cameras, speed cameras, red-light
cameras, and bus lane cameras can be used for many purposes: preventing dangerous
situations, measuring speed, collecting data for activities like vehicle insurance control, vehicle
tracking, traffic analysis, checking the flow of traffic and automatically identifying traffic
congestions, etc. Candela (see luminous intensity), (cd) - The unit of luminous intensity. Cap - A rigid structural element surmounting a pipe or other facility to provide protection and
distribute loads. Capability Maturity Model (CMM) - It is a development model. The term "maturity" relates
to the degree of formality and optimization of processes, from ad hoc practices to formally
defined steps, to managed result metrics, to active optimization of the processes. The model's
aim is to improve existing software development processes, but it can also be applied to other
processes. Capacity - A transportation facility’s ability to accommodate a moving stream of people or
vehicles in a given time period. Cape Seal - A surface treatment that combines the use of a chip seal and a slurry seal or micro-
surfacing and is used to prevent water damage and provide a waterproof surface with
improved skid resistance and ride quality. Car-Following - An algorithm used in microsimulation models to describe the behavior of
one driver following another vehicle. Cargo - All articles or material carried by a vehicle, including those used in operation of the
vehicle. Carriageway - That portion of a road or bridge devoted particularly to the use of vehicles,
inclusive of shoulders and auxiliary lanes.
