chunks.text:
Carrier - A pipe directly enclosing a transmitted substance such as liquid, gas, etc., or an
electric or communication cable, wire, or line. Casing - A larger protective pipe enclosing a carrier pipe, conduit, or duct and designed to
resist potential impacts and carry imposed loads. Cat’s Eye - A retroreflective safety device used in road marking and was the first of a range of
raised pavement markers. 52
SHC 101
Catch Basin - A receptacle at the entrance of a sewer designed to prevent obstructive material
from entering and blocking the sewer. Catch basins can serve various purposes, such as
catching, holding, and filtering runoff water or draining excess water off of surfaces or out of
gutter drain systems. Cationic Emulsified Asphalt (Cationic Emulsion) - A type of emulsified asphalt such that a
particular emulsifying agent establishes a predominance of positive charges on the
discontinuous phase. Cattle - Domesticated animals for breeding (camels, cows, sheep, and goats). CB Radio - Also called citizens band radio. A short-range radio voice communications system
used chiefly by private individuals in motor vehicles, homes, offices, and other locations where
wireless telephone service is unavailable. Cement - A mixture of clay and limestone, usually mixed with water and sand, gravel, etc.,
used for construction that sets, hardens, and adheres to other materials to bind them together. It is used to produce mortar and concrete. Cement Mixture - A mixture of clay and limestone, usually mixed with water and sand, gravel,
etc., used for construction that sets, hardens, and adheres to other materials to bind them
together. It is used to produce mortar and concrete.

from entering and blocking the sewer. Catch basins can serve various purposes, such as
catching, holding, and filtering runoff water or draining excess water off of surfaces or out of
gutter drain systems. Cationic Emulsified Asphalt (Cationic Emulsion) - A type of emulsified asphalt such that a
particular emulsifying agent establishes a predominance of positive charges on the
discontinuous phase. Cattle - Domesticated animals for breeding (camels, cows, sheep, and goats). CB Radio - Also called citizens band radio. A short-range radio voice communications system
used chiefly by private individuals in motor vehicles, homes, offices, and other locations where
wireless telephone service is unavailable. Cement - A mixture of clay and limestone, usually mixed with water and sand, gravel, etc.,
used for construction that sets, hardens, and adheres to other materials to bind them together. It is used to produce mortar and concrete. Cement Mixture - A mixture of clay and limestone, usually mixed with water and sand, gravel,
etc., used for construction that sets, hardens, and adheres to other materials to bind them
together. It is used to produce mortar and concrete.

traffic lanes that have opposite directions of travel on a roadway. These markings need not be
at the geometrical center of the pavement. Centerline - The defined and surveyed line shown on the plans from which the highway

grout cover is provided. Centrifugal Force - A lateral force resulting from a change in the direction of a vehicle’s
movement. Certificate of Guarantee - A signed statement by a person having legal authority to bind a
company or supplier to its product. Such certificate shall state that the material specifications
and test results are in compliance with the specified requirements of the pertinent AASHTO,
ASTM and/or other designations. Chain-link Fence - A type of woven fence usually made from galvanized or LLDPE-coated
steel wire. Change (Intergreen) Interval - The yellow change interval plus red clearance interval that
occurs between phases of a traffic signal in order to clear the intersection before conflicting
movements are released. Checker - A person performing a transit study while positioned either on-board a transit
vehicle or at a transit stop.

in the center of the intersection where traffic passes to the right of the island. Circular
intersections include roundabouts, rotaries, and traffic circles. Circulatory Roadway - The roadway within a circular intersection on which traffic travels in a

into components whose stiffness can be independently calculated. Equilibrium and
compatibility among the components is restored by determining the deformations at the
interfaces. Classical Force Method - A method of analysis in which the structure is subdivided into
statically determinate components. Compatibility among the components is restored by
determining the interface forces. Classification (Vehicles) - A grouping of data into bins that describe different vehicle
categories, such as passenger cars, trucks and buses.

chunks.text:
Clear Zone - The total roadside border area, starting at the edge of the traveled way, that is
available for an errant driver to stop or regain control of a vehicle. This area might consist of
a shoulder, a recoverable slope, and/or a non-recoverable, traversable slope with a clear run-
out area at its toe. Clearance - An unobstructed horizontal or vertical space. Clearance Line - The line within which no point of rock in completed excavation shall project. Where no clearance line is shown on the Drawings it shall be taken as the outer limit of the
minimum thickness of concrete shown. Clearance Lost Time - The latter part of the change period that is not typically used by drivers
to proceed through the intersection (i.e., they use this time to stop in advance of the stop line). Measured in seconds. Client - The organization responsible for the project and deemed to be the owner of the road. The Client depends on the type of project, e.g.:
•
for road projects: the respective road authority (e.g., MoTLS, MoMRAH, MODON, Royal
Commissions, etc.)
•
for municipal streets: the Municipalities (Amanah)
•
for private developments: the developer. Climber - Plant that requires support to maintain upward growth. 54
SHC 101
Closed-Box Section - A cross-section composed of two vertical or inclined webs which has at
least one completely enclosed cell. A closed-section member is effective in resisting applied
torsion by developing shear flow in the webs and flanges. Cloud Computing - Also referred to simply as Cloud.

A model for enabling on-demand
network access to a shared pool of configurable computing capabilities or resources (e.g.,

μm. Coastal Water - The coastal waters, including the territorial sea, the adjacent area, and the
special economic zone of the Kingdom. Coating - Protective material applied to or wrapped around a pipe, ductline, conduit, cable,
etc. Coefficient of Variation - The ratio of the standard deviation to the mean of a random
variable. Cold Central-Plant Recycling - The process in which asphalt recycling takes place at a central
location using a stationary cold mix plant. Cold In-Place Recycling - The on-site recycling process in which the existing asphalt
pavement is recycled using specialized train equipment (tanker trucks, cold planning machines,
crushing/screening or sizing units, mixers, pavers, and compaction rollers). The CIR process
uses 100 percent of the on-site generated RAP, and typically treats an asphalt pavement with
a depth of 75 to 100 mm. Collapse - A major change in the geometry of the bridge rendering it unfit for use. Collector - Roadways servicing traffic between major and local streets. These are streets
primarily used for traffic movements within residential, commercial, and industrial areas. Collector streets may be used for truck or bus movements and give direct service for abutting
properties.

chunks.text:
Collector Highway - A term denoting a highway that in rural areas connects small towns and
local highways to arterial highways, and in urban areas provides land access and traffic
circulation within residential, commercial, and business areas and connects local highways to
the arterial highways. Collector-Distributor Highway (C-D Highway) - A continuous highway without local access
provided parallel to a freeway mainline through one or more interchanges for the purpose of
removing weaving movements or closely spaced merges and diverges from the mainline. Column / Corner / Wall Guard - Material which guards the columns, walls, and vehicles from
the impact as a result of any potential collision and scrapes. Combination Inlet - A drainage inlet usually composed of a curb-opening inlet and a grate
inlet. Commercial Vehicle - Vehicle licensed to be used for the transportation of goods. 55
SHC 101
Committed Development - A development that has received full or initial planning
permission or is located in an adopted development plan. Communication Facility - The aggregate of equipment such as telephones, facsimile
equipment, conduits, cables, fiber optic cables, and other electronic equipment used for
various modes of transmission, such as light, digital data, audio signals, image and video
signals. Commute - Regular travel between home and a fixed location (e.g., work, school). Compacted Cement Concrete - It can be either Self-compacted concrete (SCC) or Roller-
compacted concrete (RCC). The SCC can be cast without compaction or vibration and the RCC
is placed with conventional or high-density asphalt paving equipment and then compacted
with rollers. Compaction - The active or mechanical consolidation of a mass by rolling, tamping or other
similar means.

sector, each according to its competence. Complete Interchange Lighting - A lighting system that provides relative uniform lighting

frontage road or crossroad intersections. Complete Streets - A transportation design approach that requires streets to be planned,
designed, operated and maintained to enable safe, convenient and comfortable travel and
access for users of all ages and abilities regardless of their mode of transportation. Complete
Streets allow for safe travel by those walking, cycling, driving automobiles, riding public
transportation, or delivering goods. Complex Bridges - Movable, suspension, cable stayed, and other bridges with unusual

software units, and manual operations. 2. Either a discrete element of the bridge or a
combination of elements requiring individual design consideration; A structural unit requiring
separate design consideration; synonymous with member. Compression Stress - The stress that causes deformation to a material or structure by pushing

can resist without fracture.

chunks.text:
Compressive Strength Test - A mechanical test made on a material specimen (cube, prism,
or cylinder) such as concrete to determine the maximum compressive load that can bear
before fracture. Concept of Operations (ConOps) - A foundation document that frames an overall proposed
system and sets the technical course for a project. 56
SHC 101
Concrete - A composite material that consists essentially of a binding medium within which
are embedded particles or fragments of aggregate; in hydraulic-cement concrete, the binder
is formed from a mixture of hydraulic cement and water. Concrete Barrier - Also referred to as Jersey Barrier, Jersey wall, or Jersey bump, is a protective
concrete barrier used in highway medians in order to prevent vehicle crossovers onto
oncoming traffic resulting in a likely head-on collision. Concrete Pavement Contraction Design - A type of concrete pavement which is also called
jointed concrete pavement (JCP) that uses contraction (transverse) joints spaced at regular
intervals to control cracking caused by temperature-induced contraction and expansion in the
concrete and does not use any reinforcing steel. The 1993 AASHTO Guide for the Design of
Pavement Structures is the only approved design method for CPCD projects. Concrete Thickness - The thickness of the concrete slab of the rigid pavement that is usually
between 150 mm (6 inches) (for light loading) and 300 mm (12 inches) (for heavy loads and
high traffic). Concurrent Flow HOV Lane - High occupancy vehicle (HOV) lane that operates in the same
direction as the adjacent mixed flow lanes, separated from the adjacent general purpose
freeway lanes by a standard lane stripe, painted buffer, or barrier.

chunks.text:
Concurrent Flow Preferential Lane - A preferential lane that is operated in the same direction
as the adjacent mixed flow lanes, separated from the adjacent general-purpose freeway lanes
by a standard lane stripe, painted buffer, or barrier. Concurrent Phases - Two or more phases in separate rings that are able to operate together
without conflicting movements. Condition Rating - The result of the assessment of the functional capability and the physical
condition of bridge components by considering the extent of deterioration and other defects. Conduit - An enclosed tubular casing, singularly or multiple, for the protection of wires, cables,
or lines, usually jacketed and often extended from utility access hole to utility access hole. Cone of Vision - The area of sight – or the angle of sight. Confidence Level - Describes the level of statistical confidence in an estimated parameter or
a test of significance. A 95 percent confidence level is common for most transportation
operations. Conflict - A conflict occurs when a utility facility requires relocation or adjustment to avoid
damage or disruption or to comply with the regulations and accommodation requirements to
accommodate construction, maintenance, operations, or other alterations the MoTLS KSA
undertakes. Conflict Study Data - The identification of potential conflict points at intersections, and the
associated numbers of vehicles exposed to the potential conflicts. Congested Flow - Traffic-flow condition caused by a downstream bottleneck.

chunks.text:
Connected Automated Driving - Driving that combines connectivity and automated
technologies such as advanced sensor technology, on-board and remote processing
capabilities, GPS and telecommunications systems, improving the corporation between traffic
participants and traffic efficiency. 57
SHC 101
Connected Automated Vehicle - A vehicle capable of communicating with other vehicles,
traffic signals, signs or other road infrastructure, and personal devices through wireless
technology in order to improve safety and traffic flow. Connected Vehicles - 1. A vehicle that uses devices and networks to communicate with other
vehicles and field devices. 2. Connected vehicles interact with each other (V2V), the roadside
(V2I) and beyond (V2X) via wireless communications. Consecutive Days - Two or more calendar days, one is following the other. Constant Warning Time Detection - A means of detecting rail traffic that provides relatively
uniform warning time for the approach of trains or light rail transit traffic that are not
accelerating or decelerating after being detected. Construction Joint - A joint made necessary by a prolonged interruption in the placing of
concrete. Construction Noise Assessment - An assessment that compares predicted noise levels from
construction tasks to ambient noise levels at nearby noise-sensitive receptors. Construction Vibration Assessment - An assessment of the magnitude of predicted vibration
from construction activities. Construction Works - Construction works include the erection, installation, alteration, repair,
maintenance, cleaning, painting, renewal, removal, excavation, dismantling, demolition, or
addition to any infrastructure, or any work in connection to any of these things.

chunks.text:
Construction
works occur on a site for a limited period of time only. Consumer Surplus - It reflects the total benefit to society resulting from highway
improvement. It is the sum of the individual consumer surplus measures for all persons that
will be affected by the project. Container Grown Plant - Plant that has been grown in a container for at least 1 growing
season. Containerized Plant - Plant that has been transferred at some stage of development into a
container, and whose roots are not established in a growing medium. Containment Level - Most unfavorable typical impact case that a VRS can successfully handle. Context Classification - Description of the land use and transportation context where a
highway is found. Highways are designed to match the characteristics and demands defined
by the appropriate Context Classification criteria. Context Sensitive Design - A collaborative, interdisciplinary approach that involves all
stakeholders to develop a transportation facility that fits its physical setting and preserves
scenic, aesthetic, historic, and environmental resources, while maintaining safety and mobility. Context Sensitive Design is an approach that considers the total context within which a
transportation improvement project will exist. Continuous Lighting - When uniform light levels are provided by luminaires spaced an equal
distance apart along a roadway. Continuous-Flow Intersection (CFI) - An intersection design that improves traffic flow and
reduces delays by allowing left turns and through movements of one or both approaches to
occur at the same time. Also referred to as crossover displaced left-turn (XDL or DLT).

concrete volume changes and load transfer. Contract and Contract Documents - The written agreement between the Ministry of

parties hereunder for the performance of the prescribed work. The Contract includes the
proposal, contract forms and contract bonds, specifications, supplemental specifications,
special provisions, general and detailed plans, and notice to proceed, also any change orders
and supplemental agreements that are required to complete the construction of the work in
an acceptable manner, including authorized extensions thereof, and such other documents as
by law or references are made a part thereof, all of which constitute one instrument. Contract Bid Price - The sum total of the products of the approximate quantities of the items

provided in the Contract Documents.

extensions. When a calendar date of completion is shown in the Tender, in lieu of the number

chunks.text:
59
SHC 101
Control Delay - Delay brought about by the presence of a traffic control device, including
delay associated with vehicles slowing in advance of an intersection, the time spent stopped
on an intersection approach, the time spent as vehicles move up in the queue, and the time
needed for vehicles to accelerate to their desired speed. Control Route - A route, the use of which is subject to traffic or movement restrictions which
may be supervised. Controller - The piece of hardware that determines how a traffic signal responds to calls based
on signal timing parameters. Controller Assembly - A complete electrical device mounted in a cabinet for controlling the
operation of a highway traffic signal. Cooperative ITS - Platform that can be applied to vehicles and roadside infrastructure to
enable direct two-way communication. Includes vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) and vehicle-to-
infrastructure (V2I) communications. Coordinated Phase(s) - Two or more phases in separate rings that are able to operate
together without conflicting movements. Coordinated-Actuated Control - A variation of semi-actuated control that uses the
controller’s force-off settings to constrain the non-coordinated phases associated with the
minor movements such that the coordinated phases are served at the appropriate time during
the signal cycle and progression for the major movements is maintained. Coordination - The ability to synchronize multiple intersections to enhance the operation of
one or more directional movements in a system
Cordon Count - An area count at major entry and exit points to a subarea performed by
defining a cordon line around the perimeter of the studied region.

chunks.text:
Corrective Maintenance - Also referred to as reactive maintenance, is defined as a
maintenance activity performed in response to the development of defects of pavement such
as friction loss, rutting, or cracking. Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) - The absolute temperature of a blackbody whose
chromaticity most nearly resembles that of the light source. Corridor - A set of parallel transportation facilities designed to move people between two
locations, for example, a freeway and an arterial street. Corrugation - A type of asphalt pavement deflection that involves transverse undulations at
regular intervals in the surface caused by a lack of stability. Cosine Error - A source of error in speed studies that occurs because the angle of incidence
of the beam to the travel direction of the target vehicle produces a reading on the unit that is
less than the actual speed. Count Expansion - The process of extrapolating a volume count performed over a short time
period (less than an hour) to obtain an estimate of hourly volumes. Country - The country or Districts of Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in which work herein specified
is to be performed. Coupler - The means by which the prestressing force can be transmitted from one partial-
length of prestressing tendon to another. 60
SHC 101
Course - A layer of the pavement structure separately compacted such as a wearing-surface
course, or base/subbase course. Course Texture - The local deviation of a course from a perfectly flat plane, defined by the
three characteristics of lay, coarse surface roughness, and waviness. Cover (Depth) - Vertical distance, from the top of pipe or pipe’s protective coating, casing,
duct, cable, etc. to some specified surface such as pavement, ditches, or shoulders.

chunks.text:
Cover Crop - Fast growing but short-lived exotic pasture grasses used to revegetate exposed
batters and to help minimize erosion and weed infestation. Coverage - In transit operations, the geographical area that a transit system is considered to
serve, normally based on acceptable walking distances (e.g., 0.4 km) from loading points. For
suburban rail transit that depends on automobile access (park-and-ride), coverage may extend
several kilometers. Coverage is usually computed for transit-supportive areas. Coverage Count - A short-term but continuous count of traffic performed over a period of 24
- 72 hours at a particular location. Coverage counts are multiplied by daily and seasonal
adjustment factors from control counts to develop AADT estimates at the coverage-count
location. Crack (or Cracking) - Fissure or discontinuity of the pavement surface caused by excess traffic
loading, thermal effects, or excess deformation. Crack Filler - A sealing asphalt material used to fill cracks and voids protecting from moisture
and water vapor. Crack Filling - A short-term pavement treatment is defined as the placement of materials such
as asphalt cement, asphalt emulsion, or fiber-modified asphalt into non-working or low-
movement cracks to prevent water infiltration into the underlying pavement layers. Crack Seal - A long-term pavement treatment defined as the placement of materials such as
Asphalt Rubber or silicone into working cracks to prevent water infiltration into the underlying
pavement layers. Cradle - A rigid structural element below and supporting a pipe carrier or casing.

chunks.text:
Crash - A set of events that result in death, injury or property damage due to the collision of
at least one motorized vehicle and may involve collision with another motorized vehicle, a
bicyclist, a pedestrian, or an object. The term, as used in this Volume, does not include
collisions between cyclists and pedestrians, or vehicles on rails. Crash Cushion - Road vehicle energy absorption device installed in front of one or more
hazardous locations to reduce the severity of impact. Crash Frequency - The number of crashes occurring at a particular site, facility, or network in
a one-year period. Crash frequency is calculated according to the following equation:
Crash Frequency =
Number of Crashes
Period in Years
Crash Hotspots - See "Hazardous Locations". Crash Modification Factor - A multiplicative factor, defined as the ratio of expected crash
frequency with improvement over that without improvement, that is used to compute the
expected number of crashes after implementing a given countermeasure at a specific site. 61
SHC 101
Crash Severity - The level of injury or property damage due to a crash. A crash may cause a
number of injuries of varying severity to several road users; the term crash severity refers to
the most severe injury caused by a crash. Critical Distance - The distance from the highway edge line where VRS protection may be
required, provided that within this distance there are areas requiring protection or lateral
obstacles. Critical Gap - The threshold gap time used to determine whether vehicles, pedestrians, or
bicyclists at a minor approach enter or cross the major traffic stream. In traffic flow theory, it
is defined as the gap time where minor-street traffic is equally likely to accept or reject a gap.

chunks.text:
In the context of traffic simulation models it is often used synonymously with the concept of
a minimum gap where the minor-street vehicle will always accept gaps greater than the critical
gap, and will always reject shorter ones. Cross-Section - A perpendicular intersection of the roadway at right angles to the centerline
of the roadway, including the elements of the road such as vertical and horizontal dimensions,
lanes, shoulders, elevations and slopes, pavement structure and layer thickness. Crosswalk - (a) That part of a roadway at an intersection included within the connections of
the lateral lines of the sidewalks on opposite sides of the highway measured from the curbs
or in the absence of curbs, from the edges of the traversable roadway, and in the absence of
a sidewalk on one side of the roadway, the part of a roadway included within the extension of
the lateral lines of the sidewalk at right angles to the center line; (b) any portion of a roadway
at an intersection or elsewhere distinctly indicated as a pedestrian crossing by pavement
marking lines on the surface, which might be supplemented by contrasting pavement texture,
style, or color. Crosswalk Lines - White pavement marking lines that identify a crosswalk. Crown - 1. The crown, sometimes known as soffit, is the top inside of a pipe. 2. Part of the tree
or shrub that is composed of branches. Cul-De-Sac Street - A local street open at one end only, and with special provision for turning
around. Cultivating - Preparing of ground for and growing of plants. Culvert - A structure constructed under roadways or railways and surrounded by soil or
ground in order to convey water, normally from one side of a road to the other, and move
rainwater runoff along, under, or away from highways.

chunks.text:
Cumulative Effects - Impacts that result from incremental changes caused by other present
or reasonably foreseeable actions together with the Project. A cumulative impact can arise as
the result of a) the combined impact of a number of different environmental factors-specific
impacts from a single Project on a single receptor/resource; and/or b) the combined impact
of a number of different Projects within the vicinity (in combination with the environmental
impact assessment Project) on a single receptor/resource. Curb - A structure with a vertical, horizontal, or sloping face placed along the edge of a
pavement or shoulder forming part of a gutter and strengthening or protecting the edge. Curb Extension - Curb extensions visually and physically narrow the highway, creating safer
and shorter crossings for pedestrians while increasing the available space for street furniture,
62
SHC 101
benches, plantings, and street trees. They may be implemented on downtown, neighborhood,
and residential streets, large and small. Curb extensions have multiple applications and may
be segmented into various sub-categories, ranging from traffic calming to bus bulbs and mid-
block crossings. Curb Opening - A drainage inlet consisting of an opening in the roadway curb. Curbside Transit Lane - See Transit Lane, Curbside. Curing - Action taken to maintain moisture and temperature conditions in a freshly placed
cementitious mixture to allow hydraulic cement hydration and (if applicable) pozzolanic
reactions to occur so that the potential properties of the mixture may develop. Curing Compound - A liquid that is sprayed or otherwise applied to newly placed concrete
which retards the loss of water during curing.

chunks.text:
Cutting - An area where soil or rock has been excavated along a route to allow the road to
pass at a lower level than the original ground level. Cycle - A complete sequence of signal indications. Contains at least 2 phases. Cycle Length - The duration of a complete sequence of phases in the absence of priority calls. In an actuated controller unit, a complete cycle is dependent on the presence of calls for all
non-priority phases. Some indications may be served more than once in a cycle. Cycle Path - A path or track (section of roadway) separated from motorized traffic that only
cyclists are allowed to use. Damper - A device that transfers and reduces forces between superstructure elements,
superstructure and substructure elements, or both, while permitting thermal movements. The
device provides damping by dissipating energy under seismic, braking, or other dynamic loads. Dark Mode - The lack of all signal indications at a signalized location. (The dark mode is most
commonly associated with power failures, ramp meters, hybrid beacons, beacons, and some
movable bridge signals). Data Analysis - The process of systematically inspecting, categorizing, manipulating,
summarizing, and modeling data discovering useful information, and supporting decision-
making. It is used by transportation professionals to access accurate data for every road in the
country, every day of the year. Data Interpretation - The process of reviewing data using diverse analytical methods which
will help in categorizing, manipulating, and summarizing the information in order to answer
critical questions and arrive at relevant conclusions. Data Lake - A data lake is a very large collection of raw and unfiltered data that has not been
altered from its original form before being stored.

chunks.text:
The term data lake is sometimes used
synonymously with Big Data store. Data Processing - The collection and manipulation of data in order to produce relevant
information. Data Quality - A measure of the condition of data based on factors including accuracy,
completeness, consistency, reliability, coverage, and timeliness that is used to examine how
well-suited the data is to serve its specific purpose. 63
SHC 101
Database Schema - A database schema is a type of data model used to organize data inside
a relational database. Date - Day, month and year reckoned according to the Hegira calendar and the applicable
date corresponding to the Gregorian calendar. Datum - A reference frame for precisely representing the positions and heights of locations
on the surface of the Earth along with a set of reference points of known positions. Daytime - Time period between 7:00 am and 8:00 pm. D-Cracking - It is caused by the concrete's inability to withstand environmental factors. It
usually appears as a pattern of cracks running parallel to a joint or linear crack. Decibel A (dB(A) or dBA) - The unit is used to measure sound pressure levels if the A-
weighting filter is used. Deciduous Tree - Tree that sheds its leaves at the end of the growing season. Decision Tree - A decision support tool that uses a tree-like model of decisions and their
possible consequences, including chance event outcomes, resource costs, and utility. It is one
way to display an algorithm that only contains conditional control statements. Decision Zone - An area in front of a stop bar where some drivers would choose to stop and
others would choose to proceed through the intersection, upon the change from a green to
yellow indication.

chunks.text:
Detection designs can be used to reduce the probability of drivers having to
make this type of decision on high-speed approaches. This area is also known as option zone,
indecision zone, and Type II dilemma zone. The term decision zone is used in this volume to
avoid confusion with dilemma zones that occur due to improper clearance. Decisive Distance - Distance between the highway edge line and the inner boundary of the
hazardous location. Deck - 1. A component, with or without wearing surface, directly supporting wheel loads. 2. The surface layer of concrete and reinforcing steel on a bridge. Deck System - A superstructure in which the deck is integral with its supporting components
or in which the effects or deformation of supporting components on the behavior of the deck
is significant. Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC) - A wireless (radio) communications
approach that enables short-range communications between vehicles and between vehicles
and the roadside for a variety of purposes. Deep Patch - A type of patch involving a 3 to 6-ft deep excavation that is backfilled with well-
compacted granular material and incorporates one or more layers of geosynthetic
reinforcement that is used for grading over cracks and areas of settlement on aggregate-
surfaced roads during regularly scheduled road maintenance, thus covering the area but not
correcting the problem. Deflection - The pavement surface’s vertical movement as a result of an applied (either static
or dynamic) load. Deflection Test - A non-destructive test method used to determine the structural capacity
and properties of existing pavement in order to evaluate its capability of bearing future traffic
loadings.
chunks.text_cleaned:
Detection designs can be used to reduce the probability of drivers having to
make this type of decision on high-speed approaches. This area is also known as option zone,
indecision zone, and Type II dilemma zone. The term decision zone is used in this volume to
avoid confusion with dilemma zones that occur due to improper clearance. Decisive Distance - Distance between the highway edge line and the inner boundary of the
hazardous location. Deck - 1. A component, with or without wearing surface, directly supporting wheel loads. 2. The surface layer of concrete and reinforcing steel on a bridge. Deck System - A superstructure in which the deck is integral with its supporting components
or in which the effects or deformation of supporting components on the behavior of the deck
is significant. Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC) - A wireless (radio) communications
approach that enables short-range communications between vehicles and between vehicles
and the roadside for a variety of purposes. Deep Patch - A type of patch involving a 3 to 6-ft deep excavation that is backfilled with well-
compacted granular material and incorporates one or more layers of geosynthetic
reinforcement that is used for grading over cracks and areas of settlement on aggregate-
surfaced roads during regularly scheduled road maintenance, thus covering the area but not
correcting the problem. Deflection - The pavement surface’s vertical movement as a result of an applied (either static
or dynamic) load. Deflection Test - A non-destructive test method used to determine the structural capacity
and properties of existing pavement in order to evaluate its capability of bearing future traffic
loadings.

SHC 101
Deformation - A change in structural geometry due to force effects, including axial
displacement, shear displacement, and/or rotations. Degradation (Environmental) - Severe damage to the environment caused by the depletion
of natural resources or the destruction of habitats or the extinction of wildlife species or the
pollution of environmental media and the deterioration of air, water, and soil quality. Degree-of-Freedom - One of a number of translations or rotations required to define the
movement of a node. The displaced shape of components and/or the entire structure may be
defined by a number of degrees-of-freedom. Delay - Additional travel time experienced by a driver, passenger, bicyclist, or pedestrian
beyond that required to travel at the desired speed. Delineator - A retroreflective device mounted on the roadway surface or at the side of the
roadway in a series to indicate the alignment of the roadway, especially at night or in adverse
weather. Demand - The number of vehicles forecasted to pass a point in a certain period of time. The
actual observed volume at that point can be less due to congestion metering upstream traffic
or a traffic control device metering the throughput at the study location. Also, Demand may
refer to the amount of traffic load that intends to use a facility. Demand Management - The active intervention in the market to influence the demand for
services and the assets generated and/or used in the supply of these services in order to best
match available resources to real needs and to ensure the services provided are delivered with
the best value for money. Demand Responsive Transit (DRT) - Forms of transportation services that are more flexible
and personalized than conventional fixed-route, fixed-schedule service.

67
SHC 101
Dilemma Zone - A condition that occurs when yellow change and red clearance times are too
short for a driver to either stop or clear the intersection before the beginning of a conflicting
phase. Also known as Type I dilemma zone. Diluted Emulsified Asphalt - An emulsion with additional water added to it. The most
common dilution rate is 1:1 (one part undiluted emulsion and one part additional water). Dimming Protocol - The control standards that vary the luminaire intensity. Direct Tie Down - A tie down that is intended to provide direct resistance to potential shift of
an article. Directional Design Hourly Volume (DDHV) - Directional design hourly volume is the volume
of traffic expected during the design hour (typically the peak hour) in one direction of travel. Directional Split - Percentage of the design volumes in either direction. Directional Trip Distribution - The distribution of estimated trips resulting from a proposed
development on the highway network of the study area, based on the expected directions to
go and return. Directive - An official written communication, having contractual status, from the Engineer to
the Contractor with respect to any or all phases of the Contract and Work including, but not
limited to, progress, approvals, rejections, procedures, methods, safety, etc. Disability Glare - Also referred to as veiling luminance, it is a luminance superimposed on the
retinal image that reduces its contrast. It is this veiling effect produced by bright sources or
areas in the visual field that results in decreased visual performance and visibility. Discomfort Glare - Glare that produces discomfort but does not necessarily diminish visual
performance.

chunks.section_title: A term that refers to a location that is encountered by traffic subsequent to an upstream
chunks.unit_or_chapter: 2. A term that refers to a location that is encountered by traffic subsequent to an upstream
chunks.text:
2. A term that refers to a location that is encountered by traffic subsequent to an upstream
location as it flows in an “upstream to downstream” direction. For example, “the downstream
end of a lane line separating the turn lane from a through lane on the approach to an
intersection” is the end of the lane line that is closest to the intersection. Drain Inlet - A side drainage pit that collects water from roads and parking lots and convey it
to an underground storm drain system. Dressing - Applying fresh soil, peat moss, compost, lime or fertilizer to soil or sward. Driver - Any person who drives a motor vehicle or other vehicle (including a cycle). Driver (LED Driver) - A device composed of a power source and light emitting diode (LED)
control circuitry designed to operate and dim an LED light source. Driver Workload - The number, complexity, and type of evaluations and decisions that a
driver of a motor vehicle must make to operate the vehicle safely in a specific physical and
traffic environment. Drivers’ Line of Sight - The length of road surface a driver can see and have an acceptable
reaction time. Driveway - A defined area used by vehicles travelling between a public highway and a
property adjacent or near to the highway. Drop Inlet - A box that is sized to match the storm drainage pipe and provides a base for the
grate frame. 69
SHC 101
Dropped Curb - A combined ramp and landing to accomplish a change in level at a curb. Dropped Lane - A through lane that becomes a mandatory turn lane on a conventional
roadway, or a through lane that becomes a mandatory exit lane on a freeway or expressway. The end of an acceleration lane and reductions in the number of through lanes that do not
involve a mandatory turn or exit are not considered dropped lanes.

chunks.text:
Drum Mix Plant - A manufacturing facility for producing asphalt paving mixtures that
continuously proportions the aggregate, heats and dries it in a rotating drum, adds any chosen
additives, and simultaneously mixes the material with a controlled amount of asphalt binder. Dry-Mix Mortar - Mortar in which most of the mixing water is added at the nozzle. Duct - An enclosed tubular casing for protecting wires, lines, or cables, often flexible or semi-
rigid. Ductility - Property of a component or connection that allows inelastic response. Dung - Animal dung, well-rotted. Dunnage - All loose materials used to support and protect cargo. Durability - Ability of a product to maintain its required performance over time, under the
influence of foreseeable actions. Dwell Time - Either: 1. The sum of passenger service time and boarding lost time, or 2. the
time a transit unit (vehicle or train) spends at a station or stop, measured as the interval
between its stopping and starting. Dynamic Deflection - Maximum lateral displacement of the front face of the vehicle restraint
system from its initial condition. Dynamic Exit Gate Operating Mode - A mode of operation where the exit gate operation is
based on the presence of vehicles within the minimum track clearance distance. Dynamic Load Allowance - An increase in the applied static force effects to account for the
dynamic interaction between the bridge and moving vehicles. Dynamic Message Sign (DMS) - An electronic sign deployed on roadways to inform travelers
of specific warnings including but not limited to congestion, special events, traffic incidents,
and other emergency alerts.

continuously proportions the aggregate, heats and dries it in a rotating drum, adds any chosen

chunks.text:
Dynamic Traffic Assignment - Dynamic Traffic Assignment is the process of allocating traffic
routes in a simulation model based on current traffic conditions. See Route. Early Return to Green - A term used to describe the servicing of a coordinated phase in
advance of its programmed begin time as a result of unused time from uncoordinated phases. Easement - A right, other than the acquisition of title, acquired to use or control property for
a designated purpose. Ecotourism - Eco-friendly tourist activities, preserving wildlife and natural and cultural
heritage. Edge Cracking (Corner Cracking) - The formation of crescent-shaped cracks or fairly
continuous cracks within 0.6 m (2 ft) of the pavement edge that is caused by poor subgrade,
poor compaction, shrinkage, poor drainage, or frost action of surrounding/underlying
material. 70
SHC 101
Edge Line Markings - White or yellow pavement marking lines that delineate the right or left
edge(s) of a traveled way. Effect - Term used to express the consequence of an impact (expressed as the 'significance of
effect'). Effective Green Time (G) - The time during which a combination of traffic movements is
considered to proceed effectively at the saturation flow rate. Measured in seconds. Electronic Toll Collection (ETC) - A system for automated collection of tolls from moving or
stopped vehicles through wireless technologies such as radio-frequency communication or
optical scanning.

chunks.text:
ETC systems are classified as one of the following: (1) systems that require
users to have registered toll accounts, with the use of equipment inside or on the exterior of
vehicles, such as a transponder or barcode decal, that communicates with or is detected by
roadside or overhead receiving equipment, or with the use of license plate optical scanning,
to automatically deduct the toll from the registered user account, or (2) systems that do not
require users to have registered toll accounts because vehicle license plates are optically
scanned and invoices for the toll amount are sent through postal mail to the address of the
vehicle owner. Electronic Toll Collection (ETC) Account-Only Lane - A non-attended toll lane that is
restricted to use only by vehicles with a registered toll payment account. Elements - 1. Part of factors. For example, protected species are part of biodiversity. 2. A part
of a component or member consisting of one material. Elevated Highway - A highway whose highway is on fill or structure above the level of the
adjacent ground. Elevation - Height above sea level or other datum. Embankment - Soil, soil aggregate, or broken rock which laid and compacted to raise the
grade line of a proposed highway above the original ground level of the area. Emergency - A situation resulting from a sudden unexpected event or incident which presents
a clear and imminent danger requiring immediate action to prevent or mitigate loss or damage
to life, health, property or essential public services. Emergency Maintenance - A type of highway maintenance including emergency treatments
performed to pavements in order to repair damages due to road accidents or natural causes. An example of emergency maintenance is filling a pothole or removing foreign object debris.

coordinated emergency response, including police, fire, emergency medical services,
hazardous
materials
(HazMat)
response
teams,
rescue
service
providers,
and
security/surveillance services that improve traveler security in public areas. Emergency-Vehicle Hybrid Beacon - A special type of hybrid beacon used to warn and
control traffic at an unsignalized location to assist authorized emergency vehicles in entering
or crossing a street or highway. Emergency-Vehicle Traffic Control Signal - A special traffic control signal that assigns the
right-of-way to an authorized emergency vehicle. Emissions - Release of gases or airborne particles from a specific source into the air. 71
SHC 101
Emulsified Asphalt - See Asphalt Emulsion. Encasement - A structural element surrounding a pipe carrier or casing. End-of-Roadway Marker - A device used to warn and alert road users of the end of a roadway
in other than temporary traffic control zones. Engineer - The officially authorized person by the Ministry, and works directly or through
officially authorized representatives, who are responsible for supervising the construction of
the works included in the contract and its supplements. The word (Engineer) means, wherever
indicated, the approved representative of the Ministry at the site. Engineering Judgment - The evaluation of available pertinent information, and the
application of appropriate principles, provisions, and practices as contained in this SHC and
other sources, for the purpose of deciding upon the applicability, design, operation, or
installation of a traffic control device.

chunks.text:
Engineering judgment shall be exercised by an engineer,
or by an individual working under the supervision of an engineer, through the application of
procedures and criteria established by the engineer. Documentation of engineering judgment
is not required. Engineering Practice - The comprehensive analysis and evaluation of available pertinent
information, and the application of appropriate principles, provisions, and practices as
contained in this SHC and other sources, for the purpose of deciding upon the applicability,
design, operation, or installation of a traffic control device. An engineering study shall be
performed by an engineer, or by an individual working under the supervision of an engineer,
through the application of procedures and criteria established by the engineer. An engineering
study shall be documented. Engineering Study - The comprehensive analysis and evaluation of available pertinent
information, and the application of appropriate principles, provisions, and practices, for the
purpose of deciding upon the applicability, design, operation, or installation of a traffic control
device. An engineering study shall be performed by an engineer, or by an individual working
under the supervision of an engineer, through the application of procedures and criteria
established by the engineer. An engineering study shall be documented. Entrance Gate - An automatic gate that can be lowered across the lanes approaching a grade
crossing to block road users from entering the grade crossing. Entry Radius - The minimum radius of curvature measured along the right curb at entry of a
roundabout. Smaller radii may decrease capacity, while larger radii may cause inadequate entry
deflection.

chunks.text:
Entry Width - The perpendicular distance from the right curb line of the entry to the
intersection of the left edge line and the inscribed circle of a roundabout. Environment Law - The KSA Environment Law issued by the Royal Decree No. (M/165), dated
19/11/1441 Hijri. Environmental Aspect - Any aspect taken into consideration in the EIA such as Geology and
Soils, Water quality, Biodiversity, Noise, Air quality, Landscape, Cultural heritage, etc. Environmental Assessment - A process by which information about environmental effects is
collected, assessed, and used to inform decision-making. 72
SHC 101
Environmental Characteristics - Characteristics of all that surrounds humans, animals, plants,
or any other living organism, such as water, air, land, soil, organisms, biodiversity, atmospheric
gases, water bodies and all the contents of these milieus such as inanimate objects, various
forms of energy, habitats, and natural processes, and their interaction with each other. Environmental Classification Form - A form submitted to the Center to determine the
category of the proposed Activity in terms of environmental impacts. Environmental Impact - Any negative or positive longer-term effect (result) of any activity or
incident that affects the environment. Environmental Impact Assessment Study - A study to identify, assess, and mitigate the
environmental impacts of the construction, operation, maintenance, upgrade, or removal of
any Activity on any Environmental Aspect and their inclusion in parallel with economic, social
and security considerations.

chunks.text:
Environmental Management Plan - A guidance document that consists of the set of
mitigation, monitoring, and institutional measures to be taken during implementation and
operation to eliminate adverse environmental and social impacts, offset them, or reduce them
to acceptable levels. Environmental Noise - Unwanted or harmful outdoor sound created by human activities,
including noise emitted by means of transport, road traffic, rail traffic, air traffic, and from sites
of industrial Activity. Environmental Permit for Construction - A document granted by the Center to a person to
allow the start of construction work after completing the classification and evaluation of the
Activity and the expected environmental impacts and specifying the environmental conditions
and requirements to be adhered to during the construction work. Environmental Permit for Operation - A document granted by the Center to a person to
operate the Activity. Environmental Rehabilitation - Any procedure conducted on an environmentally degraded
site or one that is affected by environmental damage or pollution, to restore its natural state
of environmental balance, in accordance with the standards set by the competent authority. Environmentally Sensitive Areas - Areas of environmental importance that, if degraded, lead
to negative environmental repercussions, including: protected areas, parks, forests, wetlands,
areas of importance to birds, mangroves, landscape sites, watershed areas, pools, and their
runoff, sea beaches, waterways, and aquifers or any area/areas that are identified or declared
by the state, the ministry or the national centers for the environment as being environmentally
sensitive areas.

chunks.text:
Environmentally Sound Management - Taking all practicable steps to ensure that waste,
including used and/or end-of-life products and wastes, is managed in a manner that will
protect human health and the environment against adverse effects which may result from such
waste. Ephemeris - It is included in the navigational message transmitted by GNSS satellites, is used
for real-time satellite coordinate computation, and contains information on week number,
satellite accuracy and health, age of data, satellite clock correction coefficients and orbital
parameters. 73
SHC 101
Equilibrium - A state where the sum of forces and moments of any point in space is 0.0. Equipment - All machinery and equipment, together with the necessary supplies for operation
and upkeep, maintenance, and protection, and also tools and apparatus necessary for the
proper construction and acceptable completion of the work. Equivalent Annual Cost - A uniform annual cost that is equivalent to all disbursements or
costs over the analysis period. The present value of equivalent annual cost equals the present
value of all such disbursements. Equivalent Beam - A single straight or curved beam resisting both flexural and torsional
effects. Equivalent Cross Slope - An imaginary straight cross slope having conveyance capacity equal
to that of the given compound cross slope. Equivalent Fluid - A notional substance whose density is such that it would exert the same
pressure as the soil it is seen to replace for computational purposes. Equivalent Standard Axle Load - A value that expresses the number of applications of the
standard axle that is equivalent to the damage impact of pavement to an axle of any mass.

chunks.text:
Equivalent Strip - An artificial linear element, isolated from a deck for the purpose of analysis,
in which extreme force effects calculated for a line of wheel loads, transverse or longitudinal,
will approximate those actually taking place in the deck. Erosion Control - Practices used to minimize soil loss and the discharge of turbid runoff in
accordance with Local, State and Federal regulations. Evacuation Response Curve - Evacuation Response Curve is a model that predicts the rate of
evacuation of people from an emergency area (e.g., hurricane) as a function of time until the
predicted event starts. Evaluation - An assessment of the performance of an existing bridge. Ex-Ante BCA - This type of BCA is performed to describe a project that is currently under
consideration, but has not begun. Ex ante is used to assist the decision making and appraise
the costs and benefits of a project. Exclusion Vehicle - Grandfather provisions which allow bridge agencies to retain higher limits
than weight limits if such limits were in effect when the applicable provisions were enacted. Exclusion vehicles are vehicles routinely permitted on highways under grandfather exclusions
to weight laws. Executive Regulations - The Regulations issued in execution of the Environment Law. Existing - The physical status as of the date of the invitation for bids of any structure, base,
surface, sub-grade, road, bridge, detour, or other unit affected by a particular project or
designated highway. Exit Box - The movement zone of the vehicle after the impact at the leading or trailing
terminal, determined during the impact test according to SASO EN 1317.

chunks.text:
Exit Gate - An automatic gate that can be lowered across the lanes departing from a grade
crossing to block road users from entering the grade crossing by driving in the opposing traffic
lanes. 74
SHC 101
Exit Gate Clearance Time - For Four-Quadrant Gate systems at grade crossings, the amount
of time provided to delay the descent of the exit gate arm(s) after entrance gate arm(s) begin
to descend. Exit Gate Operating Mode - For Four-Quadrant Gate systems at grade crossings, the mode
of control used to govern the operation of the exit gate arms. Exit Radius - The minimum radius of curvature measured along the right curb at the exit of a
roundabout. Exit radii should then be larger than entering radii. Exit Speed - Speed of the test vehicle after the impact with the test item, measured
perpendicular to the extended approach path at a point 12 m beyond the impact point. Exit Width - The perpendicular distance from the right curb line of the exit to the intersection
of the left edge line and the inscribed circle. Expansion Joint - A type of joint constructed in the transverse direction of concrete
pavements in order to allow the slab to expand without cracking due to developed
compressive stress. Expansion Ratio - The ratio between the maximum volume achieved during the foamed state
and the final volume of the binder once the foam has dispersed. Expansive Soil - Soil that has a potential for swelling or shrinkage under changing moisture
conditions. Expected Average Crash Frequency - The estimate of long-term average crash frequency of
a site, facility, or network under a given set of geometric design and traffic volumes in a given
time period (in years). Ex-Post BCA - This type of BCA is carried out after a project has been completed.

In this case:
i) for all the years for which information is available actual values, instead of forecasted, for
costs and benefits are used; ii) instead of discounting, past values are capitalized with a proper
backward discount rate. This type of project is therefore used primarily to assess the project,
so that the information gathered can be used in assessing future relevant projects. Express Lanes - A lane or set of lanes physically separated or barriered from the general-
purpose lane capacity provided within major roadway corridors. Express lane access is
managed by limiting the number of entrance and exit points to the facility. Express lanes may
be operated as reversible flow facilities or bi-directional facilities. These can include High-
Occupancy Toll lanes. Express Toll - Express Toll lane is a managed lane freeway facility that is open to toll-paying
vehicles only. Expressway - A high-speed divided arterial highway for through traffic with limited points of
access or exit and grade separations at major intersection. Extend - A detector parameter that extends a detector actuation by a programmable fixed
amount of time. It is typically used with detection designs that combine multiple setback
detectors for safe phase termination of high-speed intersection approaches or to provide lane-
by-lane detection. 75
SHC 101
Extension Of Effective Green E - The initial portion of the yellow change interval during which
a combination of traffic movements is considered to proceed effectively at the saturation flow
rate. Measured in seconds. Extra Work - Additional or new work not provided for in the Contract as awarded but
subsequently ordered by the Ministry for the satisfactory completion of a project within its
intended scope.

Extra Work Order - A change order concerning the performance of work or furnishing of
materials involving extra work. Such extra work shall be performed at agreed prices or on a
force account basis as provided elsewhere in this Code. Extreme - A maximum or a minimum. Extreme Event Limit States - Limit states relating to events such as earthquakes, vehicle and
vessel collision, with return periods in excess of the design life of the bridge. Façade Sound Level - Sound level that is determined 1 m in front of a window or door in a
façade. Facilities Management System (FMS) - The part of Highway Management System related to
highway facilities. Facility - 1. A contiguous set of individual intersections and roadway segments, e.g., rural two-
lane two-way roads, multilane highways, urban and suburban arterials. 2. (or Facilities) Any
part, item, material, or piece of equipment (including any laterals and/or appurtenances) used
by the Utility for the transmission and/or distribution of a utility product (e.g., water, sewer,
electricity, gas, telecommunications, etc.), or portion thereof that is in place and is part of the
Utility System. The term “Facility” or “Facilities” shall include but is not limited to, any and all
poles, wires, guys, anchors, buried cable, conduit, pedestals, pipelines, hydrants, valve boxes,
manholes, casings, river gages, and related fixtures. 3. All or any portion of buildings,
structures, vehicles, equipment, roads, walks, parking lots, or other real or personal property
or interest in such property. Factored Load - The nominal loads multiplied by the appropriate load factors specified for
the load combination under consideration. Factored Resistance - The nominal resistance multiplied by a resistance factor.

performance requirements. Falling Weight Deflectometer - A testing device used for estimation of the bearing capacity
of pavements in highways, local roads, airport pavements, etc., as well as to determine the
layers’ stiffness modulus. Fatigue - The progressive and localized structural damage of pavements caused by repetitive
heavy loadings and stress fluctuations that usually appear as alligator cracking. Fatigue Cracking - A distress type in asphalt pavement defined as a series of interconnected
cracks caused by fatigue failure of the surface due to repeated traffic loading. Faulting - A type of concrete pavement distress, usually observed in Jointed Plain Concrete
Pavements (JPCP), is defined as the difference in elevation across a transverse joint or crack

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 2. A term that refers to a location that is encountered by traffic subsequent to an upstream
chunks.text:
Flagger - A person who actively controls the flow of vehicular traffic into and/or through a
temporary traffic control zone using hand-signaling devices or an Automated Flagger
Assistance Device (AFAD). Flanking Inlets - Inlets placed upstream and on either side of an inlet at the low point in a sag
vertical curve. These inlets intercept debris as the slope decreases and act in relief of the inlet
at the low point. Flasher - A device used to turn highway traffic signal indications on and off at a repetitive rate
of approximately once per second. Flashing - An operation in which a light source, such as a traffic signal indication, is turned on
and off repetitively. Flashing Don’t Walk - An indication warning pedestrians that the walk indication has ended
and the don’t walk indication is underway. The pedestrian clearance interval may be longer
than the flashing don’t walk interval, as it can include the yellow change and red clearance
times. Flashing Mode - A mode of operation in which at least one traffic signal indication in each
vehicular signal face of a highway traffic signal is turned on and off repetitively. Flashing Warning Arrow (Chevron) Panel - A device consists of yellow light units arranged
in the shape of an arrow or chevron on a rectangular panel with a matte black background. The lights all flash simultaneously giving a flashing arrow or chevron effect. Flashing-Light Signals - A warning device consisting of two red signal indications arranged
horizontally that are activated to flash alternately when rail traffic is approaching or present at
a grade crossing.

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 2. A term that refers to a location that is encountered by traffic subsequent to an upstream
chunks.text:
Flexible Pavement - A pavement structure made of asphalt concrete that maintains intimate
contact with and distributes loads to the subgrade and depends upon aggregate interlock,
particle friction, and cohesion for stability. Flexural Strength - Maximum resistance of a specimen subjected to bending. Flow - The study of interactions between vehicles, drivers and infrastructure, with the aim of
understanding and developing an optimal road network with efficient movement of traffic and
minimal traffic congestion problems. Flow Rate - 1. Flow refers to a quantity of water that is flowing. 2. The equivalent hourly rate
at which vehicles or other highway users pass over a given point or section of a lane or highway
during a given time interval of less than 1 hour, usually 15 min. Flowable Fill - Controlled Low Strength Material A low strength, slurry-like fill material
primarily used in below grade applications such as utility trenches, where low strength and
78
SHC 101
ease of placement are required. It is typically placed using conventional ready-mix concrete
trucks. Fly Ash - A finely divided residue that results from the process of combustion of ground or
powdered coal and that is transported by flue gases. Flyover - A high level overpass, built above main overpass lanes, or a bridge built over an at-
grade intersection. Foamed Asphalt - A mixture of air, water and hot asphalt. Hot asphalt binder is exposed to a
small amount of water in a controlled manner, causing the asphalt to expand very rapidly into
a large volume of bubbles or foam. The viscosity of the binder is greatly reduced and its surface
area is greatly increased, enabling it to be readily dispersed throughout an aggregate or RAP
mixture.

setting asphalt emulsion diluted with water, usually without a covering aggregate, and used
to restore the surface, filling the cracks and preventing raveling. Follower - A vehicle following its leader at a headway of 2.5 seconds or less (TRB, 2022). Follower Density - The number of followers per kilometer per lane. Footcandle (fc) - A unit of illuminance. One footcandle is 10.7639 lux, which is the SI unit of

percentage for overhead and profit, where appropriate. Force Effect - A deformation, stress, or stress resultant (i.e., axial force, shear force, torsional,

times. Foundation - A supporting element that derives its resistance by transferring its load to the

chunks.unit_or_chapter: 100. mm at around 500 mm above ground level are considered frangible.
chunks.text:
100 mm at around 500 mm above ground level are considered frangible. Free Operation - See Uncoordinated Operation. Free Stressing (Unbonded) Length - The designed length of the tendon that is not bonded
to the surrounding ground or grout during stressing. Free-Field Sound Level - The sound level, which is measured or calculated in the open,
without any reflections from nearby surfaces except the ground. 79
SHC 101
Free-Flow Speed (FFS) - The average speed of vehicles on a roadway segment measured
under low-volume conditions and in the absence of traffic control devices. FFS can either be
estimated using analytical model or based on field measurement. Freeway - A fully access-controlled, divided highway with a minimum of two lanes (and
frequently more) in each direction. Freeway A (Primary Arterials) - Roadways with great visual complexity and high traffic
volumes. Usually, this type of roadway will be found in major metropolitan areas in or near the
central core and will operate at or near design capacity through some of the early morning or
evening hours of darkness. Freeway B (Secondary Arterials and Others) - All other divided roadways with full control of
access. Freeway Facility - An extended length of freeway composed of continuously connected basic
freeway, weaving, merge, and diverge segments. Freeway Management - Controls, guides, and warns traffic in order to improve the flow of
people and goods on limited-access facilities. Examples of freeway management include the
integration of surveillance information with freeway road geometry; vehicle control, such as
ramp metering; dynamic message signs (DMS).

Headboard - A vertical barrier across the front of the deck of a vehicle to prevent forward
movement of cargo. Headwall - A wall, at the end of a culvert to prevent earth from spilling into the channel. Headway - The time in seconds between the arrival of a common point on two successive
vehicles (e.g., front bumper) to reach the same point in space. Headway also describes the
time in minutes between successive transit vehicle arrivals. Herb - Any flowering plant except those developing persistent woody bases and stems above
ground. High Density Polyethylene (HDPE) - A thermoplastic polymer used for many applications. In
ITS design it is used for underground conduit systems. High Occupancy and Toll (HOT) (Facility) - Facility that is open to both HOV and drivers

carpools, vanpools and buses. 84
SHC 101
High Pedestrian Activity - Areas with significant numbers (over 100 pedestrians an hour) of
pedestrians expected to be on the sidewalks or crossing the streets during darkness. Examples
are downtown retail areas, near theaters, concert halls, and stadiums. High pedestrian activity
is not anticipated for any CDOT maintained areas, refer to ANSI/IES RP-8-18 for more
information. High Risk Sites - See "Hazardous Locations". High-Mast Lighting - Illumination of a large area by means of a group of luminaires that are

lowering devices, generally 20 m or higher. New high mast lights are no longer installed by
CDOT.

chunks.text:
High-Occupancy Toll (HOT) Lane - Managed, limited-access, highway lanes that provide free
access to HOVs, and make excess capacity available to other vehicles not meeting occupancy
requirements at a market price. High-Occupancy Vehicle (HOV) - A passenger vehicle carrying more than a specified
minimum number of passengers, such as an automobile carrying more than one or more than
two people. HOVs include carpools and vanpools, as well as buses. Highway - A general term for denoting a public way for purposes of vehicular travel, including
the entire area within the right-of-way. Highway Capacity Manual (HCM) - A Transportation Research Board (TRB: see later term)
document providing computational methodologies for estimating the operational
performance of traffic flow. Highway Edge Line - The lateral boundary of the traffic space which coincides with the paved
edge line. Highway Project - Any relatively independent component of a proposed highway
improvement. By this definition, independent links of a large improvement proposal can be
evaluated separately. For a given transportation improvement, all individual contracts or work
orders such as grading and draining, pavement, signs, and landscaping can be considered as
a single project. Where alternative construction improvements are being considered, separate
projects can be defined. However, highly interdependent sections of a highway improvement
(such as a bridge and its approach roads) ordinarily should be considered as one project for
economy study purposes. Highway Traffic Signal - A power-operated traffic control device by which traffic is warned
or directed to take some specific action.

Hot in Place Recycling - An in-place (on-site) method of hot asphalt recycling to reduce the
use of new materials involving heater scarification, mechanical heating of surface material,
remixing with recycling agent, asphalt binder, or new mix, and paving the recycled mix on the
pavement surface. Hot Mix Asphalt - A high-quality, temperature-controlled (generally between 270 and 350
°F) hot mixture of asphalt binder (cement) and well-graded, open-graded, or gap-graded
aggregate that can be compacted into a uniform dense mass. Hot Mix Asphalt Concrete - See Hot Mix Asphalt. Hot Mix Asphalt Membrane (Hot Rubberized Waterproofing System) - A hot-applied
rubberized asphalt waterproofing membrane that consists of a blend of asphalt and modified
rubber polymers containing mineral stabilizers that are used to provide effective
waterproofing for large surface areas, such as tunnels, parking decks, podium decks, etc. HOV Bypass Lane - Exclusive on-ramp lane for vehicles with a defined minimum number of
occupants (more than one), including buses, taxis, carpools, for specified time periods. Hov Lane - Any preferential lane designated for exclusive use by high occupancy vehicles for
all or part of a day, including a designated lane on a freeway, other highway, street, or
independent highway on a separate right of way. Human Machine Interface (HMI) - A user interface or dashboard that connects a person to
a machine, system, or device
Hybrid Beacon - A special type of beacon that is intentionally placed in a dark mode (no
indications displayed) between periods of operation and, when operated, displays both steady
and flashing traffic control signal indications.

chunks.text:
Hydrated Lime - A powder obtained by treating quicklime with water enough to satisfy its
chemical affinity for water under the conditions of its hydration. Hydraulic Cement - A cement that sets and hardens by chemical reaction with water and is
capable of doing so under water. Hydraulic Grade Line - The hydraulic grade line is the locus of elevations to which the water
would rise in successive piezometer tubes if the tubes were installed along a pipe run (pressure
head plus elevation head). 86
SHC 101
Hydraulics - The science concerned with the behavior and flow of liquids, especially in pipes
and channels. Hydrology - The science concerned with the occurrence, distribution, and circulation of water
on the earth, including precipitation, runoff, and groundwater. Illuminance (footcandle, fc) - The incident light falling on a surface such as roadway
pavement. The source of light may include roadway luminaires, automobile headlights and
other nearby lighting equipment. Total illuminance at a point is a combination of all light
sources that contribute. Units in footcandles (fc). Impact (Environmental) - Any negative or positive change that affects the environment as a
result of any Activity. Impact Attenuator System - A device that prevents an errant vehicle from impacting fixed
object hazards by gradually decelerating the vehicle to a safe stop or by redirecting the vehicle
away from the hazard. Impact Moling or Missiling - A pneumatically-powered cylinder, attached to an external air
supply, which is inserted in a pilot hole at proper depth and grade. The pneumatic hammering
action of the head of the tool propels the cylinder through the soil. A wire, cable, casing, or
carrier pipe is pulled back through the opening by the attached air supply line.

chunks.text:
Impact Score - A way to assign a weighting to different performance measures or questions
in a survey. Impact Severity - The theoretical parameter that determines the physical stress, the severity
of injuries or the fatality risk of vehicle occupants. Incident - A crash, broken-down vehicle, or other event on a transportation facility that
represents an unanticipated source of congestion. Incident Management - Manages unexpected incidents so that the impact on the
transportation network and traveler safety is minimized. Includes incident detection
capabilities through roadway surveillance devices and incident response through coordination
with freeway service patrols and emergency response agencies. Incremental Cost - The net change in SAR costs directly attributable to a given decision or
proposal compared with some other alternative, including the existing situation or “do-
nothing” alternative. This definition includes any resulting cost reductions (negative costs, or
benefits). The only costs that are relevant to a given proposal are incremental future costs, in
contrast to sunk costs of the past. Indirect Tie Down - A tie down whose tension is intended to increase the pressure of an
article or stack of articles on the deck of the vehicle. Inelastic - Any structural behavior in which the ratio of stress and strain is not constant, and
part of the deformation remains after load removal. Inflation Rate - The percentage change in the unit value of an element of costs or benefits. Generally expressed as an annual average rate.

check the surface temperature by detecting radiation (heat) coming from the surface and
87
SHC 101
displaying and recording the temperatures as colored images with a reference scale. It is used
in searching for defects or damages in the road or bridge pavement structure. Infrastructure - Fixed facilities, such as roadway or railroad tracks; permanent structures. Infrastructure to Vehicle (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure) - A communication model that allows
the wireless exchange of information about safety, mobility, or environment-related conditions
between vehicles and road infrastructure components such as overhead RFID readers and
cameras, traffic lights, lane markers, streetlights, road signs and parking meters. Inhibit Max - A basic signal timing parameter that removes maximum green as a phase
parameter during coordination and allows a phase to extend beyond its normal maximum
green time. Initial Illuminance (footcandle, fc) - The amount of horizontal illuminance on the pavement
area at the time the lighting system is installed, when light sources are new, and the luminaires
are clean; units in footcandles (fc). In-Lane Sidewalk Stop - Where transit vehicles run adjacent to the curb, passengers board
and alight directly from the sidewalk. Curbside stops are also often observed in dedicated
transit lanes, there pulling out of traffic is unnecessary. In-Place Mixing - A process of mixing soil in the site with a binding agent that is used in the
formation of concrete. Insertion Loss - A measure of the effectiveness of noise control devices such as silencers and
enclosures.

chunks.text:
Transportation service
provided between cities by certificated carriers, usually on a fixed route with a fixed schedule. Interface - The functional and physical characteristics required to exist at a common boundary
- in development, a relationship among two or more entities [such as software-software,
hardware-hardware, hardware-software, hardware-user, or software-user]. Interim Road Safety Audit - The application of Road Safety Audit to the whole or part of a
highway design project at any time during the design and construction stages. Interim Road
Safety Audit is not mandatory or a substitute for the formal Road Safety Audits at the end of
each design and construction stage. Interlocking - The effective load transfer from one side to the other of a joint or crack is
assisted by the projection of aggregate particles exposed when the crack forms. Intermodal Container - A reusable, transportable enclosure that is especially designed with
integral locking devices that secure it to a container chassis trailer to facilitate the efficient and
bulk shipping and transfer of goods by, or between various modes of transport, such as
highway, rail, sea and air. Internal Trip Capture - The portion of trips generated by a development that both begin and
end within the development. International Federation of Surveyors - The premier international organization representing
the interests of surveyors worldwide. International Friction Index - A pavement surface friction index developed to compare and
harmonize surface friction and macrotexture measurements. It consists of friction number FN
(F60) which indicates friction at a speed of 60 km/h and constant speed which indicates the
speed dependency which is calculated from the macrotexture of the pavement.
