chunks.text:
International Organization for Standardization (ISO) - An international nongovernmental
organization of national standards bodies, headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland that
develops and promotes worldwide technical, proprietary, industrial and commercial
standards. International Roughness Index - An index used as a pavement roughness statistic that is
computed from a longitudinal profile measurement using a quarter-car simulation at a
simulation speed of 50 mph (80 km/h). Internet of Everything - The internet of everything (IoE) is a broad term that refers to devices
and consumer products connected to the internet and outfitted with expanded digital features. It is a philosophy in which technology’s future is comprised of many different types of
appliances, devices and items connected to the global internet. Interrater Reliability - A test to assure that multiple independent observers reliably arrive at
the same result in a transportation study. Intersection - A point where two or more highways cross or meet at grade, where vehicular
travel between the highways is accomplished via turning movements, and where right-of-way
is typically regulated through the use of traffic control devices. 89
SHC 101
Interval - The duration of time during which traffic signal indications (e.g., red, yellow, green,
and flashing don’t walk) do not change state (i.e., red interval, yellow interval, green interval,
and flashing don’t walk interval). Inventory Level Rating (LRFR) - Generally corresponds to the rating at the design level of
reliability for new bridges in the AASHTO LRFD Bridge Design Specifications, but reflects the
existing bridge and material conditions with regard to deterioration and loss of section.

chunks.text:
Inventory Rating - Load ratings based on the inventory level allow comparisons with the
capacity for new structures and, therefore, results in a live load, which can safely utilize an
existing structure for an indefinite period of time. Invert - The invert is the inside bottom of the pipe. Irrigation - Irrigation is the application of water to the soil through a system of pipes pumps
and sprinklers. Island - A defined area between traffic lanes for control of vehicular movements, for toll
collection, or for pedestrian refuge. It includes all end protection and approach treatments. Within an intersection area, a median or an outer separation is considered to be an island. Isolated Interchange - A grade-separated roadway crossing with one or more ramp
connections between the crossing roadways, which is lighted and is not part of a continuous
roadway lighted system. Isolated Intersection - Single crossing point between two or more roadway facilities. Isolated Location - Limited study area, such as a single intersection or interchange. Isolated Operation - An intersection that is not currently being operated as part of a
coordinated system. Also known as free operation. See also Uncoordinated Operation. Iteration - A replication or run of a simulation model starring with a different random number
seed. Each iteration populates the simulation model with a different set of stochastic base
conditions that will affect the final estimated parameters. Simulation studies require multiple
runs to obtain an average estimate and a sense of the variability in the estimate.

chunks.text:
Jack & Boring - A method of boring where casing or carrier pipe can be jacked through bores
carved progressively ahead of the leading edge of the advancing pipe as soil is moved through
the pipe, normally with an auger that has been placed inside the pipe. Limited directional
change can be achieved with new advances in equipment. Jacking - Pushing a pipe horizontally under a roadway by mechanical means with or without
boring. Job Mix Formula - The end result of a successful mix design, which includes aggregate
gradation, asphalt binder type and their proportions. Joint - A designed vertical plane of separation or weakness. Joint Deterioration - The premature disintegration of concrete around the joint of the
pavement structure, described as a series of closely spaced transverse cracks or a large number
of interconnecting cracks near the joint and is generally caused by freeze-thaw damage and
mechanical damage. Joint Filling - Filling joints between slabs of concrete pavements with a relatively compressible
material to protect joint edges from damage caused by temperature changes and heavy loads. 90
SHC 101
Joint Seal Damage (Joint Seal Deterioration) - Any joint seal condition that enables
incompressible materials (soil or rocks) to accumulate in the joints, or allows significant
infiltration of water from the surface. Types of Joint Seal Damage are extrusion, hardening,
adhesive failure (bonding), cohesive failure (splitting), complete loss of sealant, intrusion of
foreign material in the joint, or weed growth in the joint. Joint Sealant - Compressible material used to exclude water and solid foreign materials from
joints. Joint Use - The use of pole-lines, trenches, or other facilities by two or more Utilities.

chunks.text:
Jointed Plain Concrete Pavement - A type of concrete pavement that uses transverse
contraction joints to control the location of all the expected natural cracks, does not use any
steel reinforcement, and may use load transfer devices (e.g., dowel bars) at transverse joints. The transverse joint spacing is selected such that temperature and moisture stresses do not
produce intermediate cracking between joints. Jointed Reinforced Concrete Pavement - Jointed reinforced concrete pavements contain
steel mesh reinforcement. In JRCP, designers intentionally increase the joint spacing and
include reinforcing steel to hold together mid-panel cracks. Junction - The place where two or more roads meet, either at the same level (intersection) or
at different levels (interchange). The term does not include grade-separated crossings that do
not allow turning vehicle movements. Junction Box - An appliance where multiple conductors or semiconductors make physical
contact. Electrical junction types include thermoelectricity junctions, metal-semiconductor
junctions and p-n junctions. Junctions are either rectifying or non-rectifying. Non-rectifying
junctions are called ohmic contacts. Electronic components employing rectifying junctions
include p-n diodes, Schottky diodes and bipolar junction transistors. K-Factor - The proportion of AADT that occurs during the peak hour. Kingdom - The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. KSA Highway Construction Authority - Any authority of the KSA that is competent or
tendering the construction, the reconstruction, rehabilitation, maintenance and operation of a
public highway (MoTLS, MOMRAH, Ammanah, Royal Commission). L10 - It identifies the sound level exceeded ten percent of a time period.

chunks.text:
LA10,18hr - The noise level, in dB, that is exceeded 10 % of the time between 00:60 - 24:00. Laboratory - The testing laboratory of the Department or any other testing laboratory which
shall be designated by the Engineer. LAeq,T - The A-weighted equivalent continuous pressure sound level. It is the constant sound
level that, in a given situation and time period (T), conveys the same sound energy as the actual
time-varying A-weighted sound. Lag - Time elapsed between the arrival of a minor-street vehicle ready to move into the major
street and the arrival of the front bumper of the next vehicle in the major traffic stream. The
distinction between a gap and a lag is critical, because drivers may react differently to each. LAmax - The maximum A-weighted level measured during a given time period, measured using
the “fast” time response on a sound level meter. 91
SHC 101
Land Degradation - Decreases in vegetation cover or loss of productivity and biological or
economic diversity of lands as a result of natural factors or human activities, including soil
erosion by wind and/or water, deterioration of soil properties, and loss of natural vegetation
cover. Landscape - An area, as perceived by people, whose character is the result of the action and
interaction of natural and/or human factors (The European Landscape Convention 2000). Landscape Receptor - Defined aspect of the landscape resource that potentially could be
affected by the project. Landscape Resource - Natural and physical attribute (i.e., soils, vegetation). Landscape Sensitivity - Applied to specific landscape receptors, combining judgements of
the susceptibility of the receptor to the specific type of change proposed and the value related
to the receptor (GLVIA 3 in UK- LA 107).

cover. Landscape - An area, as perceived by people, whose character is the result of the action and
interaction of natural and/or human factors (The European Landscape Convention 2000). Landscape Receptor - Defined aspect of the landscape resource that potentially could be
affected by the project. Landscape Resource - Natural and physical attribute (i.e., soils, vegetation). Landscape Sensitivity - Applied to specific landscape receptors, combining judgements of
the susceptibility of the receptor to the specific type of change proposed and the value related
to the receptor (GLVIA 3 in UK- LA 107).

Landscaping - Enhancement of land by means of planting and minor construction works. Land-Use Plan - A plan that describes the recommended location and intensity of
development for public and private land-uses, such as residential, commercial, industrial,
recreational and agricultural. Lane - The area of deck receiving one vehicle or one uniform load line. Lane Delay - The control delay for a given lane group. Lane Drop - See Dropped Lane. Lane Group - A lane or set of lanes designated for separate analysis. Lane Line Markings - White pavement marking lines that delineate the separation of traffic
lanes that have the same direction of travel on a roadway. Lane Live Load - The combination of tandem axle and uniformly distributed loads or the
combination of the design truck and design uniformly distributed load. Lane Marking - Also referred to as pavement marking, and marking or traffic marking, is set
into the surface of, applied upon, or attached to the pavement in order to define lanes of
traffic, show the location of the shoulder of the road, warn and guide traffic. Lane to Shoulder Dropoff - The difference in elevation between the edge of the travel lane
and the shoulder surface, typically caused by the settling of the shoulder. Lane Use Management System (LUMS) - A lane use management system is used to control
the movement of vehicles both in, and to and from, a lane. Functions can include lane
opening/closure, capability to create a merge zone in advance of lane closures, and the special
use of symbols such as bus, transit or truck lane symbols, and can incorporate dynamic control
of speed zones.

The system may be integrated with other road management systems such as
ramp metering, variable message signs, variable speed limit signs and traveler information
(e.g., diversions) to provide for overall corridor management. It also facilitates the
implementation of incident management plans. Lane-Changing - An algorithm used in microsimulation models to describe the behavior of
driver switching into an adjacent lane of traffic. 92
SHC 101
Large Deflection Theory - Any method of analysis in which the effects of deformation upon
force effects is taken into account. Laser - A technology used to measure vehicle speeds. See Doppler Effect. Laser Crack Measurement System - A measurement system for automatic assessment of
pavement conditions that uses laser line projectors, a high-speed camera, and advanced optics
to acquire high-resolution 3D profiles of the road. Laser Profiler - An instrument equipped with non-contact laser sensors that are used to
measure the distance to the road surface and accelerometers to compensate for the effects of
the vehicle’s movements. Laser Rut Measurement System - A measurement system used to detect and characterize
pavement rutting conditions by providing pavement transverse profiles with the use of two
laser profilers that digitize transverse sections of the pavement. Lateral Line - A lateral line, sometimes referred to as a connector, has inlets connected to it
but has no other storm drains connected. It is usually tributary to the trunk line. Lateral Separating Island - An island located at the highway’s edge line which is meant to
separate same or opposite directions of traffic. Lawn - Area of closely mown sward. LAx - The A-weighted sound level, in dB, that is exceeded X % of the measurement period.

chunks.text:
Layover Time - Time built into a schedule between arrivals and departures, used for the
recovery of delays and preparation for the return trip. The term may refer to transit units (also
known as vehicle layover) or operators. Note that layover time may include recovery time and
operator rest time as two specific components. Lday - The noise indicator for annoyance during the day period. It is the A-weighted long-term
average sound level as defined in ISO 1996-2, determined over all day periods of a year. Lden - Day-evening-night noise level. It is a descriptor of noise level based on equivalent noise
level (Leq) over a whole day, with a penalty of 10 dB(A) for night-time noise (23:00 - 07:00) and
an additional penalty of 5 dB(A) for evening noise (i.e., 19:00 - 23:00). Lead-Lag Left-Turn Phasing - A left-turn phase sequence where one left-turn movement
begins with the adjacent through movement and the opposing left-turn movement begins at
the end of the conflicting through movement. Least Squares Adjustment - A model for the solution of an overdetermined system of
equations based on the principle of least squares of observation residuals. It is used extensively
in the disciplines of surveying, geodesy, and photogrammetry. Leeward - The lee side; the point or quarter toward which the wind blows. Leq (8 hours) - It measures the equivalent continuous sound level in frequency-weighted
decibels over eight hours. Level - An optical instrument used to establish or verify points in the same horizontal plane in
a process known as leveling, and is used in conjunction with a leveling staff to establish the
relative height levels of objects or marks.

chunks.text:
93
SHC 101
Level of Service (LOS) - A quantitative stratification of a performance measure or measures
that represent quality of service, measured on an A-F scale, with LOS A representing the best
operating conditions from the traveler’s perspective and LOS F the worst. Level Staff (or Leveling Rod) - A graduated wooden or aluminum rod, used with a leveling
instrument to determine the difference in height between points or heights of points above a
vertical datum. Leveling Course - The layer of material placed on an existing surface to eliminate irregularities
prior to placing an overlaying course. Lever Rule - The statical summation of moments about one point to calculate the reaction at
a second point. License - A document granted by the competent authority to a person to authorize him to
engage in an environmental activity. Licensee - A Person who is granted a License pursuant to the provisions of an Executive
Regulation. Life-Cycle Cost Analysis - An economic analysis procedure used to estimate and compare
the overall costs of project alternatives for pavement structures taking into account life-cycle
costs that include agency costs (construction, maintenance, and rehabilitation costs) and user
costs (costs of operational delays or restrictions during construction). Lift-Off Load - Checking the load (lift-off load) in the tendon at any specified time with the
use of hydraulic jack, by lifting the anchor head off the bearing plate. Light Detection and Ranging - A remote sensing method that uses light pulses from a laser
to examine the surface of the earth and collect information about shape and surface
characteristics. LiDAR can be used to make digital 3-D representation models of objects or
areas on the earth.

chunks.text:
Light Loss Factor (LLF) - A depreciation factor that describes the drop in light output over
the life of the system. The total LLF is determined by a combination of factors, such as lumen
depreciation and luminaire dirt depreciation. Light Pole - A pole with a light source attached to it, used primarily for lighting streets, parking
areas, parks or pedestrian paths. Light Rail Line - Electric-powered railway system operating single cars or short trains on a
variety of alignment types on a partially controlled right-of-way. Light Rail Transit (LRT) - A form of urban rail public transportation characterized by a
combination of tram and rapid transit features that usually use electric rail cars operating
mostly in private rights-of-way separated from other traffic but sometimes, if necessary, mixed
with other traffic in city streets. Light Rail Transit Traffic (Light Rail Transit Equipment) - Every device in, upon, or by which
any person or property can be transported on light rail transit tracks, including single-unit light
rail transit cars (such at streetcars and trolleys) and assemblies of multiple light rail transit cars
coupled together. 94
SHC 101
Light Rail Vehicle (LRV) - A streetcar or rail vehicle similar to a streetcar, often articulated,
operating on light rail systems with substantial amounts of segregated track and higher speeds
than traditional on-street streetcar operation. Light Standard - A light pole, the supporting member for a luminaire
Light Truck (Pickup) - A motor vehicle designed for transportation of goods and materials
whose unladen weight does not exceed 3,500 kg.

chunks.text:
Lighting - The application of illumination systems along roadways, primarily for the purpose
of improving safety by increasing visibility of roadside hazards and by reducing the effects of
glare from other light sources in the visual environment, such as vehicle headlamps. Limit State - A condition beyond which the bridge or component ceases to satisfy the criteria
for which it was designed. Limit Value - A value of Lday or Lnight, and where appropriate Lden and Levening, the exceeding of
which causes Center to consider or enforce mitigation measures; limit values may be different
for different types of noise (road-, rail-, air-traffic noise, industrial noise, etc.), different
surroundings and different noise sensitiveness of the populations; they may also be different
for existing situations and for new situations (where there is a change in the situation regarding
the noise source or the use of the surrounding). Limited Access (Controlled Access) - In transportation, to have entry and exit limited to
predetermined points, as with rail rapid transit or freeways. Linear Response - Structural behavior in which deflections are directly proportional to loads. Line-Haul (Service) - 1. Transportation service along a single corridor, without branches, with
stops along the way. Usually service is intensive (high capacity) and may use exclusive right-
of-way. 2. May also be used to describe express service or even main-line service, as opposed
to feeder service. Link - A directional connection between two areas with an origin - destination relationship. Link (Model) - The spatial representation of a highway segment in a travel demand model. Liquefaction - The loss of shear strength in a saturated soil due to excess hydrostatic pressure.

chunks.text:
Load Factor - A statistically-based multiplier applied to force effects accounting primarily for
the variability of loads, the lack of accuracy in analysis, and the probability of simultaneous
occurrence of different loads, but also related to the statistics of the resistance through the
calibration process. Load Modifier - A factor accounting for ductility, redundancy, and the operational importance
of the bridge. Load Range - It is a range of load capacities in which the highest capacity is no more than 2.0
times as large as the lowest. Load Rating - The determination of the live-load carrying capacity of an existing bridge. Load Transfer Device - A mechanical means designed to carry loads across a joint. Local Street or Local Road - Roadways which provide high access to abutting property, low
average traffic volumes, and short average trip lengths. Local roads and streets may include
minor county roads, minor urban and suburban subdivision streets, and graded or unimproved
roads. Lock-Off Load - The prestressing force in an anchor immediately after transferring the load
from the jack to the stressing anchorage. Locomotive Horn - An air horn, steam whistle, or similar audible warning device mounted on
a locomotive or control cab car. The terms “locomotive horn,” “train whistle,” “locomotive
whistle,” and “train horn” are used interchangeably in the railroad industry. Logo - A distinctive emblem or trademark that identifies a commercial business and/or the
product or service offered by the business. Longitudinal Cracking - A type of distress in asphalt and concrete pavements, described as
cracks running parallel to the direction of traffic, usually caused by poorly constructed paving
joints, thermal shrinkage, inadequate support, and reflection from underlying layers.

Longitudinal Evenness - A surface property of relevance to users defined as the evenness of
the longitudinal profile of the road surface that is measured using four laser sensors that are
secured to a rigid beam positioned beneath the vehicle in the right-hand wheel path. Longitudinal Joint - A joint normally placed between traffic lanes to control longitudinal
cracking. Longitudinal Markings - Pavement markings that are generally placed parallel and adjacent
to the flow of traffic such as lane lines, center lines, edge lines, channelizing lines, and others. 96
SHC 101
Loop - See Sensor. Loose Surface Aggregate - Loose gravel or stone fragments, appeared on the road surface
when the aggregate breaks free which can reduce friction and loss of vehicle control. It may
be caused by the raveling of a chip seal or asphalt concrete pavement, gravel deposited on
the road by erosion of cut sections above the road, or unbound surplus aggregate not
removed from the surface when the road is resurfaced. Lot - A unit of production, or a group of other units or packages, taken for sampling or
statistical examination, having one or more common properties and being readily separable
from other similar units. Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level - Level above which adverse effects on health and
quality of life can be detected. LRFD Exclusion Limits - Weight and length limits of trucks operating under grandfather
exclusions to federal weight laws. Lumen (lm) - The luminous flux emitted within a unit solid angle by a point source (one
steradian) having a uniform luminous intensity of one candela (cd). See luminous flux.

chunks.text:
Luminaire - A complete lighting device consisting of the light source, lens, reflector, refractor,
7- pin receptacle, housing and such support as is integral with the housing. If the dimming
driver is located within the housing, it is considered integral and therefore part of the luminaire. The pole, posts, and bracket or mast arm are not considered to be part of the luminaire. Luminaire Efficacy - Efficacy is the lumen output of the luminaire divided by the total
luminaire power input expressed in lumens per watt. It is a measure of how energy efficient
luminaires are compared to alternative luminaires. Luminaire Lumens - The amount of light delivered by the luminaire. Luminance (candelas per square meter, cd/m2) - The luminous intensity of any surface in a
given direction per unit of projected area of the surface as viewed from that direction; i.e., the
apparent brightness of a surface. Luminous Flux (lumen, lm) - A unit of measure of the quantity of light. One lumen is the
amount of light that falls on an area of one square meter, every point of which is one meter
from a source of one candela. A light source of one candela emits a total of 12.57 lumens. Light sources are rated in terms of luminous flux. Luminous Intensity (candela, cd) - The candela is the basic unit of light quantity. The candela
is historically related to the light emitted by a candle flame and was previously known as
candlepower. The candela can be thought of as the number of photons per second emitted
by the light source. (A photon is a subatomic particle with zero mass that carries the energy of
light and all other forms of electromagnetic energy.)
Lux (lx) - The SI unit of illuminance. One lux is equal to one lumen per square meter (lm/m2),

apparent brightness of a surface. Luminous Flux (lumen, lm) - A unit of measure of the quantity of light. One lumen is the
amount of light that falls on an area of one square meter, every point of which is one meter
from a source of one candela. A light source of one candela emits a total of 12.57 lumens. Light sources are rated in terms of luminous flux. Luminous Intensity (candela, cd) - The candela is the basic unit of light quantity. The candela
is historically related to the light emitted by a candle flame and was previously known as
candlepower. The candela can be thought of as the number of photons per second emitted
by the light source. (A photon is a subatomic particle with zero mass that carries the energy of
light and all other forms of electromagnetic energy.)
Lux (lx) - The SI unit of illuminance. One lux is equal to one lumen per square meter (lm/m2),

The routes connect areas of
principal traffic generation and important roadways entering and leaving the city. These routes
are often classified as “arterials”, “thoroughfares”, or “preferentials”. Major Activity Center - A geographical area characterized by a large transient population
and heavy traffic volumes and densities; for example, central business district, major air
terminal, large university, large shopping center, industrial park, sports arena. Major Interchange - An interchange with another freeway or expressway, or an interchange
with a high-volume multi-lane highway, principal urban arterial, or major rural route where the
interchanging traffic is heavy or includes many road users unfamiliar with the area. Major Item - Any item having an original contract value in excess of 10 % of the total contract
bid price. Major Maintenance - Also referred to as pavement rehabilitation strengthening, is a
pavement maintenance procedure that includes full restoration of the qualitative state of the
pavement to extend its service life (e.g., using asphalt overlay). Major Road - Regional, national or international road, designated by the KSA, which has a
number of vehicle passages per year above a threshold determined by the KSA. Major Street - The street normally carrying the higher volume of vehicular traffic. The street
not controlled by STOP signs at a two-way STOP-controlled intersection (HCM). Managed Lane - A highway lane or set of lanes, or a highway facility, for which variable
operational strategies such as direction of travel, tolling, pricing, and/or vehicle type or
occupancy requirements are implemented and managed in real-time in response to changing
conditions.

A low pedestrian classification can occur in any street
classification but may be typified by suburban streets with single family dwellings, very low-
density residential developments, and rural or semi-rural areas. Minimum Gap - The volume-density parameter that specifies a minimum time between
detector actuations (that is applied with the gap reduction feature). See also Gap Reduction. Minimum Green - The minimum length of time that a phase must be green. It should be set
based on driver expectancy and the storage of vehicles between the setback detectors and the
stop bar (if stop bar presence detection is not used). Minimum Guaranteed Ultimate Tensile Strength (MUTS) - The minimum guaranteed
breaking load of the prestressing steel as defined by the specified standard. Minimum Track Clearance Distance - For standard two-quadrant warning devices, the
minimum track clearance distance is the length along a highway at one or more railroad or
light rail transit tracks, measured from the highway stop line, warning device, or 3.65 m
perpendicular to the track center line, to 1.82 m beyond the track(s) measured perpendicular
to the far rail, along the center line or edge line of the highway, as appropriate, to obtain the
longer distance. For Four-Quadrant Gate systems, the minimum track clearance distance is the
length along a highway at one or more railroad or light rail transit tracks, measured either from
the highway stop line or entrance warning device, to the point where the rear of the vehicle
would be clear of the exit gate arm. In cases where the exit gate arm is parallel to the track(s)
and is not perpendicular to the highway, the distance is measured either along the center line
or edge line of the highway, as appropriate, to obtain the longer distance.

weed growth and retain soil moisture. Mulching - Applying a layer of organic or other suitable material to the surface soil to prevent
drying out and to minimize weed growth. Multi-Lane - More than one lane moving in the same direction.

usually based on the dry weight. Monolithic Construction - Single-cell steel and/or concrete box bridges, solid or cellular cast-
in-place concrete deck systems, and decks consisting of precast, solid, or cellular longitudinal
elements effectively tied together by transverse post-tensioning. 102

A multi-lane street, highway,
or roadway has a basic cross-section comprised of two or more through lanes in one or both
directions. A multi-lane approach has two or more lanes moving toward the intersection,
including turning lanes. Multilane Highways - Higher-speed facilities, with two or more lanes in each direction,
without full access control (i.e., traffic can enter or exit via at-grade intersections, which may
or may not be signal controlled). Multilayer Elastic Theory - An extension of the linear theory of elasticity for homogenous
half space of Boussinesq that was developed by Burmister assuming that the layers are
isotropic and homogenous, circular loading is applied, and the material extends infinitely in
the horizontal direction. MLET is used to calculate the responses of layered structures due to
external loading. Multiple Layered Flexible Pavement - A multi-layered system with low flexural strength that
consists of various layers (i.e. wearing or surface course, base course, subbase course, and the
subgrade), each of which corresponds to a different service or integration. Multiple-Load-Path Structure - A structure capable of supporting the specified loads
following loss of a main Load-carrying component or connection. Municipality Amanah - A service system of a legal entity has financial and administrative
independence headed by an official called secretary who carries out its delegated duties
specified by assignment resolutions. Municipality of Province (Baladiah) - A legal entity of financial and administrative
independence has its own separate budget and serves the city and related villages established
by a Ministerial resolution issued by the Minister of Municipal and Rural Affairs.

Mutually Exclusive Projects - In BCA of transportation projects, the term mutually exclusive
refers to projects having benefits that are independent of other projects being constructed. This is often the case, but in other situations, candidate projects may overlap in the locus and
type of some of the benefits they provide. There may also be cost synergies (negative or
positive) among candidate projects. Nadir - Nadir is the lowest point on a sphere directly straight down. 103
SHC 101
National Cooperative Highway Research Program (NCHRP) - A national research program
conducted through the collaborative efforts of the Federal Highway Administration (FHWA),
the National Academy of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine (NASEM), and the American
Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO). National Environment Legislation - All KSA statutory provisions associated with the
protection or conservation of the environment. National Water Legislation - All KSA statutory provisions associated with the protection or
conservation of Waterbodies. Native Plant - A plant occurring naturally in a particular region, ecosystem, or habitat at the
time of European settlement. Natural Resources - All living and non-living materials found in nature and their products,
which are directly exploited or invested by man, such as air, water, land, soil, biodiversity, and
geological formations of environmental value. Network - A collection of streets, highways and intersections that define the physical
characteristics included within a study area or within study boundaries. This also refers to
boundaries in a simulation study.

Network-Level Management - Management approach that deals with the pavement network
as a whole and is generally concerned with high-level decisions relating to network-wide
planning, policy, and budget. Neural Network - A neural network is a form of machine learning that uses statistical
techniques patterned after the operation of neurons in the human brain. Neutral Area - The paved area between the channelizing lines separating an entrance or exit
ramp or a channelized turn lane or channelized entering lane from the adjacent through
lane(s). Night-Time - The time period between 8:00 pm and 7:00 am. Noise - The unpleasant and undesirable sound that adversely affects the health of humans
and living species, and environment quality, that arises from various human activities such as
manufacturing, transportation, construction work, and others. Noise Assessment - Any method used to calculate, predict, estimate or measure the value of
a noise indicator or the related harmful effects. Noise Barrier - An exterior structure designed to protect inhabitants of sensitive land use
areas from noise pollution. Noise Indicator - A physical scale for the description of environmental noise, which has a
relationship with a harmful effect. Noise Mapping - The production of computer software generated maps showing how the
predicted levels of outdoor noise vary with location. Noise Modeling - Software to predict noise levels. Noise Monitoring - Measurement of noise levels. Noise Sensitive Receptor - Receptors which are potentially sensitive to noise. Examples
include dwellings, hospitals, healthcare facilities, education facilities, community facilities,

international and national or statutorily designated sites, public rights of way, and cultural
heritage assets.

Nominal Load - An arbitrarily selected design load level. Nominal Maximum Aggregate Size (NMAS) - Is designated as one sieve size larger than the
first sieve to retain more than 10 percent in a standard series of sieves. Nominal Resistance - Resistance of a component or connection to force effects, as indicated
by the dimensions specified in the contract documents and by permissible stresses,
deformations, or specified strength of materials. Non-Continuous Lighting - When lighting is provided at key locations to improve visibility
and wayfinding. Non-Destructive Test - Also referred to as non-destructive examination (NDE), non-
destructive inspection (NDI) and non-destructive evaluation (NDE), is a testing and analysis
technique used to evaluate the properties of the material, components, or assemblies for
discontinuities, or differences in characteristics without causing damage to the original part. Nonlinear Response - Structural behavior in which the deflections are not directly
proportional to the loads due to stresses in the inelastic range, or deflections causing
significant changes in force effects, or a combination thereof. Non-Project Noise Change - Noise change based on the DMOY against DMFY scenario, with
no Project implementation. Non-Renewable Groundwater - Groundwater in an Aquifer that does not receive an
appropriate amount of water in a natural way over a year compared to the amount of water
withdrawn from the same Aquifer over the year concerned, or that receives a very small
amount of water compared to the amount of water stored therein.

No-Show - In demand-responsive transit (DRT), a no-show occurs when a passenger fails to
show up for a scheduled trip, resulting in a negative impact on other passengers' quality of
service on a shared-ride trip, and on DRT performance for the transit agency. When a
scheduled rider no-shows a trip, passengers on board the DRT vehicle spend extra time
traveling to the pick-up location and waiting for that rider who does not appear. Nozzle - Attachment at end of delivery hose from which shotcrete is projected at high velocity. Nozzle-Man - (in Dry-Mix Process) is an individual who has demonstrated the knowledge and
ability to properly place dry-mix shotcrete. Nuclear Gauge - A non-destructive field device used to determine the density of a compacted
material, such as in-place Hot Mix Asphalt, and then the level of compaction using radioactive
sources (gamma radiation). Object Marker - A device used to mark obstructions within or adjacent to the roadway. Objectives - Specific, measurable statements related to the attainment of goals. Obstacle - An item or hazard being protected from vehicular impact. Occupancy - The time a vehicle spends traveling over a roadway sensor expressed in seconds
per vehicle or percent. Occupancy can be used in combination with an assumed or known
vehicle length to infer the speed of the vehicle. Also, occupancy can refer to the number of
persons per vehicle as a measure of throughput. 105
SHC 101
Occupancy Rate - The number of occupants of a vehicle, expressed as persons per vehicle. Occupancy Requirement - Any restriction that regulates the use of a facility or one or more
lanes of a facility for any period of the day based on a specified number of persons in a vehicle.

At an intersection,
vehicles entering from an approach that is approximately straight ahead would be considered
to be opposing traffic, but vehicles entering from approaches on the left or right would not
be considered to be opposing traffic. Opposing Traffic Lane Divider - A traffic control device which helps drivers stay in designated
travel lanes through work zones. Optimization - The process of using a computer algorithm to obtain optimum configuration
of signal timing or another traffic control strategy. The optimum configuration is identified by
minimizing or maximizing an objective function such as total vehicle delay. Original Ground - The ground surface just prior to the initiation of the proposed work. Origin-Destination - Origin-Destination. Starting and ending points of all trips in a
transportation network are compiled into an O-D matrix that is used for planning and
operational analysis. Ortho-Rectification - The process of calibrating a video image for the purpose of applying
video image processing software for automated data collection from known (true) dimensions
such as pavement markings, lane widths, or the height of the camera. Outer Separation - The portion of an arterial highway between the traveled ways of a roadway
and a frontage street or road. Outfall - Point of discharge into a Waterbody. Output - The Voltage from a load switch part that powers a signal indication. Overall Stability - Stability of the entire retaining wall or abutment structure and is
determined by evaluating potential slip surfaces located outside of the whole structure. Over-consolidated Soil - A soil that has been under greater overburden pressure than
currently exists. Over-consolidation Ratio - Ratio of the maximum pre-consolidation pressure to the
overburden pressure.

installations include signs placed on cantilever arms that extend over the roadway or shoulder,
on sign support structures that span the entire width of the pavement, on mast arms or span
wires that also support traffic control signals, and on highway bridges that cross over the
roadway. 107

Asphalt (HMA) over an existing pavement structure in order to provide strength, to improve
ride and to extend service life. Overpass - A grade separation where the subject highway passes over an intersecting
highway. Oversaturated Flow - Traffic flow where (a) the arrival flow rate exceeds the capacity of a

dissipated, or (c) traffic flow is affected by downstream conditions. Owner - The individual, company, government agency, etc., having ownership and

costs, depreciation, insurance premia, and registration fees are examples of ownership costs.

Pace Speed - The highest speed within a specific range of speeds that represents more
vehicles than in any other like range of speed. The range of speeds typically used is 10
kilometers per hour (km/h). Paratransit - A public transportation system intended to serve persons with disabilities in a
community. Parent Phase - A phase (or phases) used to determine whether an overlap should be active. Parent phases may be in different rings and/or may be on different sides of a barrier. Park-and-Ride - An access mode to transit in which patrons drive private automobiles or ride
bicycles to a transit station, stop, or carpool-vanpool waiting area and park the vehicle in the
area provided for that purpose. They then ride the transit system or take a car or van pool to
their destinations. Parking Area - A parking lot or parking garage that is separated from a roadway. Parallel or
angle parking spaces along a roadway are not considered a parking area. Parking Lane - Area intended for parking along the street paved surface, which can also be
used for traffic, when necessary. Parking Lot - Area intended for parking vehicles outside the street edge line (may or may not
be raised). Parking Space - A cleared area intended for parking vehicles. Parks (National, Terrestrial, Geological) - Sites within vegetation cover lands with unique
environmental or geological characteristics, which have been allocated to conservation and to
promote ecotourism within their boundaries. Parkway - A wide road, usually divided, with an area of grass and trees on both sides and in
the middle leading to or passing through a park.

Patch Deterioration - The deterioration of a patch, i.e., a portion of the pavement surface,
greater than 1 ft2, that has been removed and replaced or where additional material has been
applied to the pavement after original construction, that typically progresses at a higher rate
than the original pavement and affects adversely the ride quality. Patching - A type of asphalt pavement treatment including marking and saw cutting outside
the distress area, removing the old asphalt and thorough cleaning, applying tack coat,
backfilling with hot asphalt, and compacting with a vibratory plate compactor or small
vibratory roller compactor. Path-Based Count - A type of volume study that requires the analyst to trace a vehicle through
an intersection such as a roundabout or superstreet. Pathway - A general term denoting a public way for purposes of travel by authorized users
outside the traveled way and physically separated from the roadway by an open space or
barrier and either within the highway right-of-way or within an independent alignment. Pathways include shared-use paths, but do not include sidewalks. Pathway Grade Crossing - The general area where a pathway and railroad or light rail transit
tracks cross at the same level, within which are included the tracks, pathway, and traffic control
devices for pathway traffic traversing that area. Paved - A bituminous surface treatment, mixed bituminous concrete, or Portland cement
concrete roadway surface that has both a structural (weight bearing) and a sealing purpose
for the roadway. 109
SHC 101
Pavement - A durable surfacing of a road. The primary function of a pavement is to transmit
loads to the sub-base and underlying soil.

Pavement Condition - A quantitative representation of pavement condition data including
description and quantity of distresses and the PCI at different points along with the feature. Pavement Condition Index - A numerical index between 0 and 100 (excellent condition) is
used to indicate the general condition of a pavement section, and to measure its performance
and level of service. Pavement Defect (Pavement Distress) - Any unsatisfactory performance of pavement or
signs of failure such as transverse cracking, rutting, map cracking, pothole, longitudinal
cracking, raveling, depression, pumping, bleeding, bump, poor skid resistance, and roughness. Pavement Delineator - Devices that are installed temporarily to direct vehicles away from
hazards that may exist in the roadway. One of the primary characteristics is their high visibility,
which is essential for catching the attention of motorists. They are generally equipped with
reflective strips which tend to make them visible even in poor lighting conditions. Pavement Design - The process followed for the construction of a pavement structure
including the selection of appropriate pavement and surfacing materials, types, layer
thicknesses, and configurations in order to ensure that the pavement performs adequately and
requires minimal maintenance under the anticipated traffic loading for the design life adopted. Pavement Distress Index - A composite index calculated by mathematically combining the
effects of distresses (i.e., cracking, rutting, and defects) on pavement conditions. Pavement Functional Condition - The condition of the pavement related to the level of
service provided to the pavement users through the initial life of the pavement and between
major rehabilitations that depends on its riding and safety quality.

chunks.text:
Pavement Management - A management approach that assists in finding optimum
strategies, especially related to cost-effectiveness for providing and maintaining pavements in
a serviceable condition over a period of time as identified in the Pavement Management
System. Pavement Management System - A system of tools or methods used to provide assistance
in pavement management decision-making to find optimal strategies for evaluating pavement
performance and allocation of resources given budget constraints, repairing, and maintaining
pavement in a serviceable condition over a period of time. Pavement Marking - Any kind of device or material that is used on a road surface in order to
convey official information. Pavement Monitoring - A part of the pavement treatment process that aims to achieve
roadway satisfaction for users during their movements and satisfy safety conditions on
roadways and avert accidents caused by pavement distresses. There are mainly two types of
pavement monitoring: dynamic and static pavement monitoring. Dynamic pavement
monitoring requires an action to collect the pavement data, while the pavement data are
automatically collected in the static pavement monitoring system which uses fixed sensors or
instruments. Pavement Performance - The ability of pavement to maintain its serviceability over time or
with increasing axle-load applications. Pavement Preservation - A strategy that focuses on preserving the pavement structure
including cost-effective pavement treatments to extend the service life or to improve the
serviceability of an existing pavement.

equivalent new structure by removing and replacing all asphalt and concrete layers, and often
the base and subbase layer, in combination with remediation of the subgrade and drainage,
and possible geometric changes. Pavement Rehabilitation - A strategy followed to restore pavement performance and extend
its service life using rehabilitation treatments and techniques including structural overlays and
replacement of failed Portland cement concrete slab. Pavement Rehabilitation Treatment - Treatment applied to restore pavement performance
and extend its service life including adding or replacing one or more pavement surface layers. Pavement Structural Condition - The condition of the pavement is related to the level of
service of the structure of pavement to cumulative traffic load and it is characterized by the
pavement modulus and the structural number of pavement. Pay and Display (Parking Ticket Machines) - Standalone machines which are commonly
used on-street or in large car parks where drivers park and walk to their closest machine select
and pay for the duration of stay they require. The machine produces a ticket recording the
time and date of purchase and the expiry time and date. 111
SHC 101

system does not issue a ticket. The machine requires an alphanumeric key pad which allows
drivers to type in their vehicle registration number then select and pay for the duration of stay
they require.

movement of the anchor at each increment, including alignment load. Period of Maintenance - Period of maintenance shall mean the period of Contractor
maintenance named in the contract, calculated from the date of completion of the Work as
certified by the Provisional Handover Committee. Permanent Anchor - Any prestressed anchor intended for permanent use, generally with
more than a 24- month service life. Permanent Lateral Displacement - Residual lateral deformation of leading and trailing
terminals as well as crash cushions during impact tests. Permanent Loads - Loads and forces that are, or are assumed to be, either constant upon
completion of construction or vary only over a long-time interval. Permeability - The rate of flow of a liquid under a differential pressure through a material. Permissible Number of Specimens – Number of specimens, expressed as a percentage, that
may have strength less than the specification value. Permit - Document issued by a Center pursuant to the provisions of an Executive Regulation
before start of an Activity permitting a Person to start the Activity. Permittee - A Person who is granted a Permit pursuant to the provisions of an Executive
Regulations. 113
SHC 101
Permittivity (Ψ) - The volumetric flow rate of water per unit cross-sectional area per unit head
under laminar flow conditions, in the normal direction through a geotextile. Person - Any natural or legal person.

seconds. Phasing Diagram - A graphical representation of a sequence of phases, typically in the form

interpreting photographic images and patterns of electromagnetic radiant imagery and other
phenomena. Photopic - Photopic vision is our day vision. It is color vision provided by cones in the eye,
generally associated with adaptation to a luminance of at least 3.4 cd/m2. Pickup - A two axle freight vehicle with a total of four tires. In general, this classification

exposure of coarse aggregate due to the wearing away of the asphalt binder and fine

surface aggregates. Pollutant - A Harmful Substance in certain quantities over a period of time that causes

chemical units called monomers that are used to modify asphalt enhancing its mechanical
properties. Polymer Modified Binder (Polymer Modified Asphalt) - A conventional asphalt cement
blended with a synthetic polymer or crumb rubber, used to enhance the performance of binder
(reducing temperature susceptibility, increasing elasticity, resilience, cohesion, etc.) on heavily
trafficked or distressed pavement surfaces, often in adverse climatic conditions. Popouts - A small fragment of concrete surface that broke away, normally ranging in diameter

steel bar. Pretimed Control - A mode of operation where every phase is on recall every cycle, regardless
of changes in traffic conditions. Typically, only used in closely spaced grid-like networks.

chunks.text:
Prevention - Measures taken before a substance, material or product has become waste, that
reduce: 1) the quantity of waste, including through the re-use of products or the extension of
the life span of products; 2) the adverse impacts of the generated waste on the environment
and human health; or 3) the content of harmful substances in materials and products. Preventive Maintenance - The number of cost-effective treatments (i.e. thin overlay) aiming
to preserve the quality of the pavement, retard future deterioration, and extend the service life
of the pavement. Price Breakdown - The Contractor's proposal that submitted to the Ministry which shows the
costs of all items indicated in the Bill of Quantity and describes the details contents of cost for
each work item. Primary Grout - Portland cement based grout that is injected into the anchor hole prior to or
after the installation of the anchor tendon to provide for the force transfer to the surrounding
ground along the bond length of the tendon. Primary Highway - A highway usually found in urban areas having a population of 100,000
or greater. Primary Member - A member designed to carry the loads applied to the structure as
determined from an analysis. Prime Coat - An application of a low-viscosity asphalt material to an absorptive surface,
designed to penetrate, bond, and stabilize this existing surface and to promote adhesion
between it and the construction course that follows. Principal Legend - Place names, street names, and route numbers placed on guide signs. 117
SHC 101
Prior Rights - An industry term and refers to the superiority (order) of property interests
determined between multiple parties.

engineering services directly to the public, who are experienced in the work for which they are

by a code of ethics to protect the public health. Profile - A longitudinal section of a highway. Profilograph - A device consisting of a mounted sensing wheel to provide free vertical
movement at the center of the frame that is used for determining the pavement surface
roughness by calculating data used to define the International Roughness Index (IRI). Program of Work - A work schedule prepared and submitted by the Contractor to the

the equipment, the order of procedure, and methods which the Contractor proposes to use to
carry out the Work. Progression (Signal) - Coordination of a set of traffic signals such that vehicles moving down
a street receive green signal indications at several traffic signals in a row. Project - Construction works, installations, schemes, or interventions (in the natural

chunks.text:
Project’s Time Horizon (or Reference Period) - The time period over which the stream of
benefits and costs is to be evaluated. The final year of construction is designated year 0 (zero),
and subsequent years are designated year 1, year 2, etc. Projects involving staged construction
that extends over more than four or five years should either be divided into separate projects
for the separable stages, or use the final year of construction for the first major stage as year
0, with prior capital outlays being discounted to their present value in year 0. Project-Level Management (Project Level Pavement Management) - A combination of
methods, procedures, data, software, policies, and decisions to produce network solutions
using specific pavement section data (e.g. traffic, ESALs, safety, inventory, pavement condition)
within the network that is generally concerned with lower-level decisions relating to condition,
maintenance, reconstruction, rehabilitation assignment, and unit costs. Proof Test - Incremental loading of an anchor, and recording the total movement of the
anchor at each increment. Proposal - The offer of a bidder, on the prescribed form, to perform the stated construction
work at the prices quoted. Proposal Form - The prescribed form on which the offer of a bidder is to be submitted. Protected Area - A terrestrial, marine or coastal area stated under Article (4) of the Executive
Regulation for Protected Areas. Protected Movement - A movement that has the right-of-way, and there are no conflicting
movements occurring. Protected Turn - Turning movements that are served by an exclusive phase indicated by a
green arrow signal indication. Turning vehicles do not need to yield to other traffic but may
have to watch for pedestrians.

work at the prices quoted. Proposal Form - The prescribed form on which the offer of a bidder is to be submitted. Protected Area - A terrestrial, marine or coastal area stated under Article (4) of the Executive
Regulation for Protected Areas. Protected Movement - A movement that has the right-of-way, and there are no conflicting
movements occurring. Protected Turn - Turning movements that are served by an exclusive phase indicated by a
green arrow signal indication. Turning vehicles do not need to yield to other traffic but may
have to watch for pedestrians.

chunks.text:
Protective Device - A vehicle restraint system type. This type may be one of the following:
safety barrier, terminal, transition, removable safety barrier, crush cushion. Provisional Handover - A conditional acceptance, by the Ministry, of a partial or total
completion of the Work as authorized in the General Conditions of the contract. Public (The) - One or more natural or legal persons and, in accordance with national
legislation or practice, their associations, organizations, or groups. Public Rights of Way - A right by which the public can pass along linear routes over land at
all times. Although the land may be owned by a private individual, the public have a legal right
across that land along a specific route. Public Transportation - Transportation service to the public on a regular basis using vehicles
that transport more than one person for compensation, usually but not exclusively, over a set
route or routes from one fixed point to another. Routes and schedules of this service may be
predetermined by the operator or may be determined through a cooperative arrangement. 119
SHC 101
Subcategories include paratransit and mass transit service that are available to the public. Also
referred to as “public transit”. Pumping Station - A pumping station is an area that consists of all the water intake structures
for suction pipes of pumps, the pumps, motor, gear from motors to the pumps, pressure
pipeline transporting water from the pumps to the water-discharge device, etc. Pushbutton - A button to activate a device or signal timing for pedestrians, bicyclists, or other
road users.

chunks.text:
Pushbutton Information Message - A recorded message that can be actuated by pressing a
pushbutton when the walk interval is not timing and that provides the name of the street that
the crosswalk associated with that particular pushbutton crosses and can also provide other
information about the intersection signalization or geometry. Pushbutton Locator Tone - A repeating sound that informs approaching pedestrians that a
pushbutton exists to actuate pedestrian timing or receive additional information and that
enables pedestrians who have visual disabilities to locate the pushbutton. Quality Assurance - A group of planned and systematic actions (e.g. planning, design,
development of plans and specifications, advertising and awarding of contracts, construction,
and maintenance, and the interactions of these activities) necessary to ensure that a product,
facility, or service will perform according to given requirements for quality or characteristics in
the most efficient, economical, and satisfactory manner possible. Quality Assurance Procedures - Specific sampling, testing, measuring, and evaluation
procedures for determining the degree of conformance to the quality and quantity
requirements of the Specifications. Quality Control - Also referred to as Process Control, is a system or process of measurements,
samplings, testing, and evaluation of tests and measurements results that determines the level
of conformance of a material, a process, or a product with specified quality requirements. Quality Index - A statistic computed when applying the variables acceptance procedures to
estimate the level of quality actually achieved. Quality of Service - An approach of quantifying the travel experience for users of the
transportation system.

chunks.text:
Questionnaire - The specified forms on which the Contractor shall furnish required
information as to his ability to perform and finance the work. Queue - A stacking of vehicles waiting to be serviced by a traffic signal or delayed by a roadway
incident. Queue Jump - A short bus lane section (often shared with a right-turn lane), in combination
with an advance green indication for the lane, that allows buses to move past a queue of cars
at a signal. Queue Length - The length of all vehicles stopped at a traffic control device or delayed at a
bottleneck on a freeway. Quicklime - A calcined limestone, the major part of which is calcium oxide or calcium oxide in
association with magnesium oxide, capable of slaking with water. 120
SHC 101
Quiet Area in an Agglomeration - An area, delimited by the Competent Authority, for
instance, which is not exposed to a value of an appropriate noise indicator greater than a
certain value set by the Center, from any noise source. Quiet Area in Open Country - An area, delimited by the Competent Authority, which is
undisturbed by noise from traffic, industry, or recreational activities. Radar - A technology used to measure vehicle speeds. Radar Drive Feedback Signs - A traffic calming device designed to slow speeders down by
alerting them of their speed. Rail - Transit system using trains operating in exclusive or shared rights-of-way (includes both
light and heavy rail systems). Rail Rapid Transit (System) (RRT) - Transit system using trains of high performance,
electrically powered rail cars operating in exclusive rights-of-way, usually without grade
crossings, with high platform stations. The tracks may be in underground tunnels, on elevated
structures, in open cuts, at surface level, or any combination thereof.

chunks.text:
Rail Traffic - Every device in, upon, or by which any person or property can be transported on
rails or tracks and to which all other traffic must yield the right-of-way by law at grade
crossings, including trains, one or more locomotives coupled (with or without cars), other
railroad equipment, and light rail transit operating in exclusive or semi-exclusive alignments. Light rail transit operating in a mixed-use alignment, to which other traffic is not required to
yield the right-of-way by law, is a vehicle and is not considered to be rail traffic. Railings - Non-vehicle restraint system installed alone or in combination with vehicle restraint
systems in bridges, retaining walls or similar structures for pedestrian or other user safety. Railroad Crossing - Also referred to as Level Crossing or Highway-Rail Grade Crossing or
Road-Rail Grade Crossing, is a place where a highway and a railroad cross at the same level
that is indicated by signs, lights, bells, and/or gates that barricade the road. Raised Pavement Marker - A device mounted on or in a road surface that has a height
generally not exceeding approximately 25 mm above the road surface for a permanent marker,
or not exceeding approximately 50 mm above the road surface for a temporary flexible marker,
and that is intended to be used as a positioning guide and/or to supplement or substitute for
pavement markings. Raking - Drawing a rake across a surface to remove unwanted material, to fine level the
surface; raking in - working in with a rake. Ramp - 1. A dedicated highway providing a connection between two other highways; at least
one of the highways a ramp connects is typically a high-speed facility such as a freeway,
multilane highway, or Collector-Distributor highway. 2.

chunks.text:
A sloped walkway connecting
pedestrian facilities at different elevations. Ramp Metering - A traffic management strategy on freeways that restricts parts or all of on-
ramp traffic to sustain stable flow conditions on the freeway mainline. Ramp-Freeway Junction - The point of connection between a ramp and a high-speed facility,
such as a freeway, multilane highway, or Collector-Distributor highway, designed for high-
speed merging or diverging without control. 121
SHC 101
Random - Relating to, having, proceeding, occurring or being elements or events with definite
probability of occurrence. Rangelands - Lands that are not exploited for agriculture, housing, and public utilities; wholly
or partially covered with local or non-local plants growing naturally and have varying suitability
for grazing and animal feeding, be it grasses, shrubs, or trees. This comprises degraded
rangelands that have been replanted with grazing plants. Ranking - An ordered list of projects or project alternatives based on specific factors or project
benefits and costs. Rapid Transit (System) (RTS) - Transit service that is operated completely separate from all
other modes of transportation. The term "rail rapid transit" frequently refers both to operation
of light rail transit vehicles over exclusive right-of-way and heavy rail transit vehicles; the term
"bus rapid transit" refers to operation of motor buses over exclusive bus roads or busways. Raveling - Loss of pavement surface material involving the dislodging of aggregate particles
and degradation of the asphalt binder. Reactivity - The rate at which a chemical substance undergoes a chemical reaction, either by
itself or with other materials, with an overall release of energy.

chunks.text:
Real-Time Kinematic - The application of surveying to correct common errors in current
satellite navigation (GNSS) systems and relies on a reference station to provide real-time
corrections, providing up to centimeter-level accuracy. Reasonable Alternatives - Different Project design, technology, location, size, and scale
solutions considered by the developer. Recall (Or Phase Recall) - A call is placed for a specified phase each time the controller is
serving a conflicting phase. This ensures that the specified phase will be served again. Reclaimed Asphalt Pavement (Recycled Asphalt Pavement) - A reuse of old asphalt
concrete, milled or gripped off an existing pavement, as the aggregate base for new asphalt
pavement. Reclaimed Asphalt Shingles - A common roofing material that can be recycled and used in
the asphalt pavement by repurposing shingles into an additive for hot-mix asphalt (HMA) or
cold patch to fill cracks and potholes. RAS provides an economic benefit through material cost
savings and improved material performance and significantly reduces material being
landfilled. Reclaimed Concrete Aggregate - A manufactured aggregate material that is derived from
the crushing, processing, and classification of Portland cement concrete construction debris
recovered from roadways, sidewalks, buildings, bridges, and other sources. Reconstruction - The replacement of an entire existing pavement structure that has reached
the end of its service life or is critically deteriorated by the placement of a new equivalent or
increased pavement structure.

chunks.text:
Recovery - Any operation, the principal result of which is waste serving a useful purpose by
replacing other materials which would otherwise have been used to fulfill a particular function,
or waste being prepared to fulfill that function, in the plant or in the wider economy. 122
SHC 101
Recreational Vehicle - A vehicle with light- or medium-duty chassis and suspension designed
for recreational living or hauling. Recycling - Reusing of old pavement materials, such as asphalt mixtures, concrete, or
base/subbase material in the process of the construction of a new pavement structure. Red Clearance Interval - An interval that follows a yellow change interval and precedes the
next conflicting green interval. Redirection - Capacity of a restraint system to return a vehicle to the highway in a controlled
manner after an impact against such restraint system. Refined Methods of Analysis - Methods of structural analysis that consider the entire
superstructure as an integral unit and provide the required deflections and actions. Reflection Cracking - A type of pavement distress defined as cracking that appeared in a
resurface or overlay, caused by horizontal and vertical movement of joints or cracks in the
underlying base or surface. Reflective Noise Barrier - A noise barrier that reflects noise. Region (Traffic Analysis) - Citywide or countywide study area involving all freeway corridors
and major arterials. Regional ITS Architecture - A specific regional framework for ensuring institutional
agreement and technical integration for the implementation of ITS projects in a particular
region.

chunks.text:
Regression-to-the-Mean - A concept in statistics that refers to the fact that if one sample of
a random variable is extreme, the next sampling of the same random variable is likely to be
closer to its mean, i.e., less extreme. Regulatory Sign - A sign that gives notice to road users of traffic laws or regulations. Rehabilitation - 1. A process that consists of a wide range of treatments for repair,
rehabilitation, restoration, and replacement of existing pavements to restore serviceability and
extend the service life. 2. A process in which the resistance of the bridge is either restored or
increased. Reinforced Concrete - Structural concrete in which steel reinforcement (steel bars)is
embedded to increase its tensile strength or ductility. Reinforcement - Steel embedded in a rigid pavement slab and in concrete structures to resist
tensile stresses and detrimental opening of cracks. Reinforcing Bars - Steel bars used in concrete to provide additional strength or as devices
that provide load transfer across cracks occurred on a PCC slab. Rejectable Quality Level (RQL) - The level of Lot percent defective at/or above which the
work is considered to be unacceptable. Rejected Gap - Gap that a minor-street vehicle at the stop bar does not accept. Rejected Lag - Lag that a minor-street vehicle at the stop bar does not accept. Rejuvenation - The process of restoring the original chemical properties of an aged oxidized
asphalt binder with an emulsion consisting of asphalt, polymer, latex, or other additives. 123
SHC 101
Relational Database - A relational database is a type of database organized as a collection of
data items that are interconnected by pre-defined relationships (data schema). These data
items are organized as a set of tables with columns and rows.

chunks.text:
Tables are used to hold
information about the objects to be represented in the database. Each column in a table holds
a certain kind of data, and a field stores the actual value of an attribute. In a relational database,
data can be accessed in many ways without having to reorganize the database tables
themselves. Reliability - 1. A User-Perception measure of traffic performance, indicating relatively stable
traffic conditions over multiple days that guide driver expectations. 2. The probability that a
pavement section will perform satisfactorily over the traffic and environmental conditions and
for the design period. Reliability Index - A quantitative assessment of safety expressed as the ratio of the difference
between the mean resistance and mean force effect to the combined standard deviation of
resistance and force effect. Relocation(s) - The adjustment, removing and reinstalling, or replacement of a Utility Facility
as necessary in order to accommodate an MoTLS KSA project for either or both of the
following reasons: (a) a physical conflict between the Utility Facility and the MoTLS KSA,
maintenance or use), and/or (b) an incompatibility between the MoTLS KSA project as
designed and the Utility Facility based on the requirements of the applicable Utility Standards
and/or applicable Governmental Requirements (even though there is no physical conflict). Remediation of Polluted Site - The restoration of a polluted site to the natural state of
environmental balance by implementing appropriate measures in accordance with the
requirements specified by the Competent Authority.

chunks.text:
Remote Weather Information System / Roadway Weather Information System (RWIS) -
A system of sensors, communications, and data collection technologies to measure specific
weather conditions including atmospheric, pavement and/or water level conditions. Renewable Groundwater - Groundwater in an Aquifer that receives an amount of water in a
natural way over one year that is appropriate compared to the amount of water withdrawn
from the same Aquifer over the year concerned. Repeatability - Also referred to as test-retest reliability, is the closeness of the agreement
between the results of successive measurements of the same measure, when carried out under
the same conditions of measurement and it is calculated by conducting the same experiment
multiple times and performing a statistical analysis on the results. Reprocessing - The renewal of an existing surface by scarifying, remixing with or without
additional material, and relaying. Residential Area - That portion of a municipality, or an area within the influence of a
municipality in which the dominant land uses is residential development, but where small
business area may be included. Residual Asphalt - The remaining asphalt after an emulsified asphalt has set, typically 57 - 70
percent of the undiluted emulsified asphalt. Resilient Modulus - A laboratory measurement of the behavior of pavement materials to
characterize their stiffness and resiliency and is defined as the ratio of the amplitude of the
repeated axial stress to the amplitude of the resultant recoverable axial strain. 124
SHC 101
Resistance Factor - A resistance multiplier accounting for the variability of material properties,
structural dimensions and workmanship, and the uncertainty in the prediction of resistance.

chunks.text:
Response Time - In demand-responsive operations, the time between a passenger's request
for service and the passenger pickup. Rest Area - Area next to the road specially designed to host facilities for road users’ needs
such as fuel stations, restaurants, restrooms and other amenities related to user safety and
comfort. Restoration - The reconstruction of the highway rights-of-way disrupted by the construction
of, maintenance, or repair of a utility facility with the resultant effects by which the highway is
returned to a condition as good as or better than its original condition. Restrainers - A system of high-strength cables or rods that transfers forces between
superstructure elements and/or superstructure and substructure elements under seismic or
other dynamic loads after an initial slack is taken up, while permitting thermal movements. Restricted Crossing U-Turn (RCUT) - An intersection design that restricts left turns at an
intersection but allows the same movement downstream via a U-turn in order to ease traffic
movements, reduce delays and enhance safety. It is used on high-speed, rural, four-lane
divided highways. It is also referred to as superstreet intersection, a J-turn intersection, or
synchronized street intersection. Resurfacing - A maintenance technique that involves the placing of one or more new layers
on an existing surface instead of replacing the entire pavement structure to extend its service
life and enhance safety. Retaining Wall - A structure that is designed and constructed to withstand lateral pressure of
soil or hold back soil materials. Retroreflectivity - A property of a surface that allows a large portion of the light coming from
a point source to be returned directly back to a point near its origin.

chunks.text:
Reuse - Any operation by which products or components that, before turning them into waste,
are used again for the same purpose for which they were conceived. Reversible Lanes - One or more lanes that can be operated to carry traffic in either direction
of travel found on freeway facilities, arterial streets and at sporting/event complexes. Traffic is
managed through ITS treatments, variable signing and movable barriers. Rhizome - A root-like, usually horizontal stem, growing under or along the ground that sends
out roots from its lower surface and leaves, or shoots from its upper surface. The primary
means by which some plants spread or reproduce. Ridership (Patronage) - The number of people making one way trips on a public
transportation system in a given time period. Ridesharing - A form of transportation, other than public transit, in which more than one
person shares in the use of the vehicle, such as a bus, van, or automobile, to make a trip. Right-Hand-Rule Intersection - An intersection, in which, the driver of a vehicle is required
to give way to vehicles approaching from the right. 125
SHC 101
Right-of-Way (Assignment) - The permitting of vehicles and/or pedestrians to proceed in a
lawful manner in preference to other vehicles or pedestrians by the display of a sign or signal
indications. Right-of-Way (ROW) - 1. The legal right, established by a grant from a landowner or long
usage (i.e., by prescription), to pass along a specific route through property belonging to
another. 2. The entire width between the boundaries of a strip of land occupied or intended
to be occupied by a road, sidewalk, crosswalk, railroad, electric transmission line, oil or gas
pipeline, water line, sanitary storm sewer, or other similar uses.

chunks.text:
Right-of-Way Transfer Time - The maximum amount of time needed for the worst-case
condition, prior to display of the track clearance green interval. This includes any railroad or
light rail transit or highway traffic signal control equipment time to react to a preemption call,
and any traffic control signal green, pedestrian walk and clearance, yellow change, and red
clearance intervals for conflicting traffic. Right-of-Way Width - Amount of land reserved or used for transport purposes. Right-Turn Pocket - Where right-turn volumes are high enough to interfere with transit
operations but cannot be prohibited, providing a right-turn pocket to the right of the through
transit lane reduces bus and streetcar delays. Rigid Pavement - A pavement structure consisting of a Portland cement concrete slab of
relatively high bending resistance, resting on a base course or directly on the subgrade. Rigidity - Force effect caused by a corresponding unit deformation per unit length of a
component. Ring - A sequence structure consisting of two or more sequentially timed and individually
selected conflicting movements, arranged to allow flexibility between compatible movements
in different rings. Rings along with barriers fundamentally define how a controller organizes
phases, so that compatible phases can time together and conflicts do not occur. Riprap - A protective covering of graded boulders, pieces of concrete or stone, with or without
mortar, to prevent erosion. Risk - Combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of the harm (ISO
IEC 51). Risk Analysis - Systematic use of available information to identify hazard and to estimate the
risk (ISO IEC 151). Risk Assessment - Overall process comprising a risk analysis and a risk evaluation (ISO IEC
51).
