chunks.text:
Risk Aversion - Empirical discernible phenomenon that some crashes are perceived to be
much worse than their inherent risk would indicate. Risk Evaluation - Procedure based on the risk analysis to determine whether the tolerable risk
has been achieved (ISO IEC 51). Risk Perception - The way in which a stakeholder views a risk, based on a set of values or
concerns. Risk Reduction - Actions taken to reduce the probability and/or negative consequences
associated with a risk. 126
SHC 101
Road - See Roadway. Road (Pavement) Marking - See Pavement Marking. Road Classification - The classification of roads according to the type of service they provide
and how they function within a transportation system in order to assist in establishing road
design features, land use planning policy, traffic density, mobility, safety, and access
requirements. Road Cleaning - The process of cleaning and clearing roads and highways of debris including
shoveling, brooming, sweeping, and/or vacuuming in order to ensure reliable and safe driving
conditions while improving road capacity and operational efficiency. Road Condition Monitoring - An application of traffic estimation systems that are used to
capture the longitudinal and transverse profiles, the condition of the edge of the road, and the
texture of its surface by collecting data using specialized vehicles equipped with several
sensors such as laser scanners, road profilers, accelerometers, image, and video cameras and
positioning systems, identifying and assessing defect and calculating Road Condition Index
(RCI). Road Construction and Maintenance - The activities undertaken to preserve and maintain
each type of road structure as nearly as possible in its original condition as constructed.

chunks.text:
Road Infrastructure Safety Management - See "Road Safety Management". Road Inventory - 1. A comprehensive survey which can be used to study the profile of the
roads in the area of study features like road/pavement widths, road pavement types, street
lighting, luminosity, drain types, encroachments, presence of vendors/street furniture, bus
stops etc. 2. The act of maintaining the highway inventory system by collecting descriptive and
statistical data on all highway systems. The inventory includes the functional classification of
roads, total mileage, owner, and the assets assigned to highway sections. Road Management System - A maintenance management system that provides a set of
decision support tools that can predict future road conditions based on the knowledge of the
main characteristics of a road network and transport system over time. Road Noise Detector - A detector used to measure the noise levels from flowing traffic. Road Painting - Paint made from thermoplastic resin mixed with titanium-dioxide pigment
and small reflective glass beads that is applied to the highway surface to create road markings. Road Safety Audit - A formal examination of an existing or future road or traffic project by
an independent and suitably qualified team, reporting on the project’s crash potential and
safety performance and how it can be improved, considering all road users and at all stages
of the project development. Road Safety Inspection - A formal on-site evaluation of an existing road by an independent,
qualified team, to identify features that could adversely affect the safety of road users and to
propose appropriate improvements.

chunks.text:
Road Safety Management (or Road Infrastructure Safety Management) - The total
procedures supporting the road infrastructure management authorities to prevent future road
crashes and/or to limit their consequences. 127
SHC 101
Road Users - Any person who uses any part of a road, including pedestrians, cyclists and
drivers of vehicles. Road-Rail Grade Crossing - The general area where a road and a railroad cross at the same
level, within which are included the railroad tracks, highway, and traffic control devices for road
traffic traversing that area. Roads General Authority (RGA) - A regulatory authority under Saudi Arabia's Ministry of
Transport and Logistic Services that is responsible for overseeing the country's construction,
operation and maintenance of roads. Roadside - Area beyond the traveled way (e.g., driving lanes) and the paved shoulder (if any)
of the roadway itself. Roadside Restoration - The use of planning, design, construction, and maintenance activities
to restore roadside plant communities according to designated roadside character. Roadside Unit (RSU) - A device on a roadway that communicates with on-board units in
connected vehicles and sends information back to a traffic management center. Roadway - That portion of a highway improved, designed, or ordinarily used for vehicular
travel and parking lanes, but exclusive of the sidewalk, berm, or shoulder even though such
sidewalk, berm, or shoulder is used by persons riding bicycles or other human-powered
vehicles. In the event a highway includes two or more separate roadways, the term roadway
as used in this Code shall refer to any such roadway separately, but not to all such roadways
collectively. Roadway Width - Clear space between barriers and/or curbs.

chunks.text:
Roller Compacted Concrete - Concrete compacted by roller compaction; concrete that, in its
unhardened state, will support a roller while being compacted. Rolling - Passing a roller over a surface. Rolling Weight Deflectometer - A deflectometer composed of a towed trailer, a networked
data-acquisition system, and a load platform that is designed to measure pavement
deflections using a method of laser triangulation at normal highway speeds without traffic
control and lane closure for pavement management purposes. Roughness - An element of surface texture defined as an expression of irregularities in the
pavement surface that adversely affect vehicle dynamics, ride quality, dynamic loads, drainage,
longitudinal profile, transverse profile, cross slope, vehicle delay costs, fuel consumption,
maintenance costs, etc. Roundabout - An intersection with a generally circular shape, characterized by yield on entry
and circulation around a central island. Rout-and-Seal - With rout-and-seal, a saw or router is used to grind a shallow trench over
the crack; the routed reservoir is then filled with sealant. Route - 1. The geographical path followed by a vehicle or traveler from start to finish of a
given trip. 2. A designated, specified path to which a transit unit (vehicle or train) is assigned. Several routes may traverse a single portion of highway or line. 3. In traffic assignments, a
continuous group of links that connects two centroids, normally the path that requires the
minimum time to traverse. 4. The specified course of a driver traveling through a simulated
128
SHC 101
transportation network. Routes can be permanently defined by the user (static) or can be
determined through a path-optimization algorithm by the model (dynamic).

chunks.text:
Routine Maintenance - Also referred to as operational maintenance, is defined as the number
of activities carried out repeatedly, on a daily, weekly, monthly, or annual basis on all parts of
the road/highway to maintain and preserve the condition of the highway system and ensure
serviceability under any weather conditions, including crack sealing, fog sealing, and repair of
localized failed areas of pavement. Routine Runoff - The normal runoff from roads including any contaminants washed off the
surface in rainfall events, which can result in either Acute or Chronic impacts. Routine runoff
excludes the effect of spillages and major leaks which usually result in Acute impacts. Rumble Strip - A series of intermittent, narrow, transverse areas of rough-textured, slightly
raised, or depressed road surface that extend across the travel lane to alert road users to
unusual traffic conditions or are located along the shoulder, along the roadway center line, or
within islands formed by pavement markings to alert road users that they are leaving the travel
lanes. Run - See Iteration. Running Speed - A measure of the average speed over a segment, which is calculated as the
distance traveled divided by the running time. Running Time - The time a vehicle is actually in motion (or moving faster than a predesignated
speed) while traversing a given segment of street or highway. Rural Council - A legal entity of financial and administrative independence and has its own
budget formed to serve a group of villages and its head office shall be located in one of those
villages. Rural Highway - A type of roadway normally characterized by lower volumes, higher speeds,
fewer turning conflicts, and less conflict with pedestrians.

chunks.text:
Rut Depth - The maximum vertical distance between the top of the heave and the bottom of
the depression of the pavement at a specific location that is used to report rutting caused by
traffic loading. Rutting - A contiguous longitudinal depression deviating from a surface plane defined by
transverse cross slope and longitudinal profile. Sabkha - An area of coastal flats, supratidal mudflat or sandflat in which evaporite-saline
minerals accumulate as the result of semiarid to arid climate, found in North Africa and Saudi
Arabia. Safe Load Capacity - A live load that can safely utilize a bridge repeatedly over the duration
of a specified inspection cycle. Safe System - The Safe System approach aims to eliminate fatalities and serious injuries of all
users of the transportation system through a holistic model of multiple elements working
together to safeguard against tragic crash outcomes. Safety Barrier - Continuous vehicle restraint system installed alongside, or on the median area
of a highway. 129
SHC 101
Safety Performance Function - A regression equation estimating the average crash
frequency for a specific site type (with specified base conditions) as a function of Annual
Average Daily Traffic (AADT) and other parameters (e.g., segment length in the case of roadway
segments). Safety Performance Indicator(s) - Any measurement(s) that is causally related to crashes or
injuries and is used in addition to the number of crashes or injuries, in order to indicate safety
performance or understand the process that leads to crashes. Sag Point / Major Sag Point - A low point in a vertical curve. A major sag point refers to a
low point that can overflow only if water can pond to a depth of 0.6 m or more.

chunks.text:
Salvage Value - The value of an investment or capital outlay remaining at the end of the
project’s time horizon. Sample - 1. A representative subset of the total population used for data analysis. Statistics
(mean, standard deviation) of the sample are used to approximate the true parameters of the
population. 2 .It is a piece of material or a complete bearing which is tested in order to infer
the properties of the batch of material or group of bearing elements from which it is taken. A
sample shall consist of at least one bearing chosen randomly from each lot and material batch
and shall comprise at least 10 % of the lot. Sand Dune Barrier - A barrier implemented to assist windblown sand to accumulate along an
appropriate boundary. Sand fences are employed to control erosion, assist in sand dune
stabilization and keep sand off highways. They often consist of a plastic mesh vertical
perforated on a wooden barrier with similar characteristics. Sand Seal - A type of treatment in asphalt pavements, defined as a spray application of asphalt
binder, normally an emulsion followed with a light covering of fine aggregate (e.g., clean sand,
screenings) to improve the skid resistance of slippery pavements and to seal against air and
water intrusion. Sand Stabilization - A practice designed to prevent the erosion of a sand dune, by either
mechanical or chemical methods. Satellite Remote Sensing - The process of detecting and monitoring the physical
characteristics of an object or area by measuring its reflected and emitted radiation at a
distance with the use of a satellite.

It is used in several fields, including geography, land
surveying, and most Earth science disciplines, and in applications such as monitoring of floods
and droughts, soil moisture, vegetation, deforestation, forest decline, forest fires, carbon
storage, or land cover, road monitoring, and urban planning. Saturation Flow Rate - Saturation Flow Rate is the equivalent hourly rate at which previously
queued vehicles can traverse an intersection approach, assuming the green signal is available
at all times and no lost times are experienced. Scale House - Office building for truck inspection station employees. Scaling - Local flaking or peeling away of the upper concrete slab surface, normally 3 mm (1/8
in) to 12.5 mm (1/8 in) in-depth, resulting in exposure to freezing and thawing and the loss of
mortar and aggregate. Scenic and Recreational Highways - A public road having special scenic, historic,
recreational, cultural, archeological, and/or natural qualities that have been recognized as such
130
SHC 101
through legislation or some other official declaration. The terms “byway,” “road,” and
“highway” are synonymous. They are not meant to define higher or lower functional
classifications or wider or narrower cross-sections. School - A public or private educational institution recognized by the Ministry of Education
(MOE) for one or more grades K through 12 or as otherwise defined. School Zone - A designated roadway segment approaching, adjacent to, and beyond school
buildings or grounds, or along which school related activities occur. Scoping - The process of considering the information required for reaching a (reasoned)
conclusion on the likely significant effects of a Project on the environment resulting in the
issuance of Project classification by the NCEC.

chunks.text:
Scotopic - Scotopic vision is our night vision when light is not present. It is black and white
vision provided by rods in the eye, and generally associated with adaptation to a luminance
equal to or less than 0.034 cd/m2. Scour Critical Bridge - A bridge whose foundation (or foundations) has been determined to
be unstable for the predicted scour conditions. Screen-Line Count - A count of traffic between two subareas performed by recording traffic
flows at crossing points between the two regions. A screen-line count is typically performed
along a natural or human-made barrier with limited crossing points, such as an urban freeway
or a river. Scrub Seal - A type of treatment in asphalt pavements, defined as the application of asphalt
emulsion and crushed rock on the surface through a series of brooms (scrub broom) placed
at different angles. Seal Coat - A category of treatments in asphalt pavements, defined as the application of a
bituminous coating with aggregate to improve and waterproof the surface texture. The types
of seal coats are fog seals, sand seals, slurry seals, micro-surfacing, cape seals, sandwich seals,
and chip seals. Secondary Highway - A highway of less national significance than a Primary Highway, but a
highway that is intended to move large volumes of traffic at high speed between population
centers of 50,000 or more. Second-Order Analysis - Analysis in which equilibrium conditions are formulated on the
deformed structure; that is, in which the deflected position of the structure is used in writing
the equations of equilibrium. Section - An immediate subheading of a Section of this Code consisting of Sub sections, Items,
Subitems and/or paragraphs which set forth details and requirements essential or necessary
to form the Specifications.

Specifications are divided into Sections, Sections into Sub-sections,
Items, Subitems and paragraphs. Segment - 1. For interrupted-flow facilities, a link and its boundary points. 2. For
uninterrupted-flow facilities, a portion of a facility between two points. Selvage - The woven edge portion of a fabric parallel to the warp. Semi-Actuated Control - Signal control in which some approaches (typically on the minor
street) have detectors and some approaches (typically on the major street) have no detectors. 131
SHC 101
Semitrailer - Any trailer designed to be coupled to a motor vehicle in such a way that part of
it rests on the motor vehicle and a substantial part of its weight and the weight of its load is
borne by a motor vehicle. Sensitive Areas - Environmentally Sensitive Areas. Sensitive Buildings - Residential dwellings, hospitals, healthcare facilities, education facilities,
religion facilities, or other buildings where noise or vibration can cause disturbance to people
using the buildings. Sensitive Receptors - Receptors that are likely to be severely affected by an Activity or Project
due to their geographical proximity or sensitive nature. They include environmental elements,
living species, archeological, cultural, and religious sites, and community groups (such as
endangered species, hospitals, elder care centers, schools, residential complexes, and others). Sensitivity Analysis - The process of varying input parameters in a stochastic model to gauge
the effect of these changes on the predicted performance measures. Sensor - A term describing various types of automated data collection equipment used to
obtain traffic operational data. Sensors include magnetic inductance loops, video-based
detection methods and sensors using microwave or infrared technology.

chunks.text:
Shadow Vehicle - A vehicle that provides close-up protection to the rear of workers on foot. Shafts - A vertical Adit generally used for access. Shared Parking - Shared Parking is a type of parking where spaces are shared by more than
one user, which allows parking facilities to be used more efficiently. Shared Transit / Right-Turn Lane - On streets with a right-side dedicated transit lane that
accommodates a moderate volume of right-turn movements, the transit lane can permit right
turns approaching an intersection. Shared-Use Path - A bikeway outside the traveled way and physically separated from
motorized vehicular traffic by an open space or barrier and either within the highway right-of-
way or within an independent alignment. Shared-use paths are also used by pedestrians
(including skaters, users of manual and motorized wheelchairs, and joggers) and other
authorized motorized and nonmotorized users. Shop Drawings - Fabrication plans for any part of the work including, but not limited to,
precast concrete items, structural steel items, or other metal items, and connections thereof,
which the Contractor is required to submit to the Engineer. Short-Term - Noise change based on parallel assessment year (for example DMOY against
Do-Something Opening Year Scenario (DSOY)). Shotcrete - Concrete or mortar conveyed through a hose and pneumatically projected at high
velocity onto a surface to achieve compaction. Shoulder - The portion of the roadway continuous with the traveled way for accommodation
of stopped vehicles, for emergency use, and for lateral support of base and surface courses. Shoving - The horizontal displacement of a localized area of the pavement surface which may
also include some vertical displacement.

chunks.text:
Shrinkage Crack - A type of pavement distress, defined as block-patterned cracks of almost
square shape formed over the whole area of the pavement that is caused by a reduction in the
volume of the pavement because of improper materials or proportions and aging of the
asphalt mix. Shrubs - Any natural or planted flora with a height ranging from (0.5) half a meter to less than
(1.5) one and a half meters. Shuttle (Service) - A service provided by vehicles that travel back and forth over a particular
route, especially a short one, or one that connects two transportation systems or centers, or
one that acts as a feeder to a longer route. Shuttle services usually offer frequent service, often
without a published timetable. Side Slope - The slope from the edge of the roadway to the toe of a ditch, expressed as the
ratio of horizontal distance to vertical distance. Sidefill - Backfill alongside a pipe, ductline, conduits, cables, etc. 133
SHC 101
Side-Flow Interception - Flow that is intercepted along the side of a grate inlet, as opposed
to frontal interception. Sidewalk - That portion of a street between the curb line, or the lateral line of a roadway, and
the adjacent property line or on easements of private property that is paved or improved and
intended for use by pedestrians. Sidewalks and Paths - A section associated with a roadway that is provided primarily for use
by pedestrians and excludes other forms of traffic such as motorized vehicles, etc. Sign - Any traffic control device that is intended to communicate specific information to road
users through a word, symbol, and/or arrow legend. Signs do not include highway traffic
signals, pavement markings, delineators, or channelization devices.

chunks.text:
Sign Illumination - Either internal or external lighting that shows similar color by day or night. Street or highway lighting shall not be considered as meeting this definition. Sign Legend - All word messages, logos, pictographs, and symbol and arrow designs that are
intended to convey specific meanings. The border, if any, on a sign is not considered to be a
part of the legend. Sign Panel - A separate panel or piece of material containing a word, symbol, and/or arrow
legend that is affixed to the face of a sign. Signal Backplate - A thin strip of material that extends outward from and parallel to a signal
face on all sides of a signal housing to provide a background for improved visibility of the
signal indications. Signal Face - An assembly of one or more signal sections that is provided for controlling one
or more traffic movements on a single approach. Signal Head - An assembly of one or more signal faces that is provided for controlling traffic
movements on one or more approaches. Signal Housing - That part of a signal section that protects the light source and other required
components. Signal Indication - The illumination of a signal lens or equivalent device. Signal Lens - That part of the signal section that redirects the light coming directly from the
light source and its reflector, if any. Signal Phase - A period of time that allows one or more nonconflicting movements to proceed
into a signalized intersection. It is also the term used to describe the associated signal timing
parameters. Signal Section - The assembly of a signal housing, signal lens, if any, and light source with
necessary components to be used for displaying one signal indication. Signal System - Two or more traffic control signals operating in signal coordination.

chunks.text:
Signal Timing - The amount of time allocated for the display of a signal indication. Signal-to-Noise Ratio - A measure used in science and engineering that compares the level
of the desired signal to the level of background noise. A ratio higher than 1:1 indicates more
signal than noise. 134
SHC 101
Significant Observed Adverse Effect Level - The level above which significant adverse effects
on health and quality of life occur. Signing - Individual signs or a group of signs, not necessarily on the same support(s), that
supplement one another in conveying information to road users. Siltation - The deposit of waterborne material that generally consists of fine earth, sand, or
mud that passes through the 0.075 mm (No. 200) sieve, is non-plastic or very slightly plastic
and exhibits little or no strength when air-dried. Simple Assessment - The collection and assessment of data and information that is readily
available to reach an understanding of the likely environmental effects of a Project. This
informs the final design or need for further 'detailed assessment'. Simulation - A software-based analysis approach that uses stochastic algorithms describing
behavior of motorized and nonmotorized users of the transportation system with the objective
of quantifying traffic performance measures. Simulation Run - See Iteration. Single Space Electronic Meters - Single space meters allow payment for a single space. New
electronic meters accept payment by cash and other means. Single-Occupancy Vehicle (SOV) - A passenger vehicle containing only a single occupant. Single-Point Urban Interchange (SPUI) - A diamond interchange that combines all the left-
turning ramp movements into a single signalized intersection.

chunks.text:
Single-Unit Trucks - A single frame vehicle, longer than a passenger car, including trucks,
camping and recreational vehicles, motor homes, etc., regardless of the number of tires, axles,
or axle configuration. Site - 1. A homogenous roadway segment or an individual junction, e.g., divided or undivided
roadway segments, signalized or unsignalized intersections. 2. An area of land or location
where construction is planned, people & equipment are working or includes any vegetation
activity (i.e., mowing, chemical control, pruning, and tree removal) is being performed, on or
adjacent to MoTLS’s right-of-way. Site Engineer - He on-site representative of the Contractor duly authorized to receive and
execute all instructions of the Engineer and to supervise and direct all of the Contractor's
construction operations in all phases of the Work. Site Walkover - A non-intrusive visual inspection of a site captured by notes, photographs
and video. Skew Angle - Angle between the centerline of a support and a line normal to the roadway
centerline. Skid Resistance (Skid Number - Friction Number) - The ability of a pavement surface to
offer resistance to slipping or skidding when braking forces are applied by the vehicle tires
that is achieved with the selection of proper asphalt content and aggregate with a rough
surface texture. Skid Resistance Value - A friction measurement used in pavement design that expresses the
force developed when a tire is no longer able to rotate on the pavement surface, and as a
result, loses traction and slides along the top of the surface. It may be measured by the
135
SHC 101
following test methods: pendulum tester, locked wheel tester, spin-up tester, or surface texture
measurement.

chunks.text:
Slaking - The chemical reaction that produces hydrated lime when quicklime and water are
mixed. Sleeve - A short casing through a wall, pier, abutment, or similar highway structure. It can also
refer to a casing underneath a roadway used for enclosing a carrier pipe, conduit, or duct. Slippage Cracking - A type of pavement distress, defined as crescent-shaped cracks formed
due to the slippage of the asphalt layer (i.e., lack of bond between the surface and lower
courses), usually in areas where horizontal forces induced by traffic develop. Slotted Drain Inlet - A drainage inlet composed of a continuous slot built into the top of a
pipe that serves to intercept, collect, and transport the flow. Slump - A measure of consistency of freshly mixed concrete, mortar equal to the subsidence
measured immediately after removal of the slump cone. Slurry Seal (Slurry Coat) - A type of pavement treatment, defined as a mixture of slow-setting
emulsified asphalt, well-graded fine aggregate, and mineral filler with water added to achieve
slurry consistency that is used to fill cracks and seal areas of old pavements, to seal the surface
preventing moisture and air intrusion into the pavement, and to improve skid resistance. Small Deflection Theory - A basis for methods of analysis where the effects of deformation
upon force effects in the structure is neglected. Small Form-Factor Pluggable (SFP) - A compact, hot-pluggable network interface module
used for both telecommunication and data communications applications. Small Network - See Corridor. Small Target Visibility (STV) - A weighted average of the values of target Visibility Level over
a grid of points on an area of roadway for one direction of traffic flow.

measured immediately after removal of the slump cone. Slurry Seal (Slurry Coat) - A type of pavement treatment, defined as a mixture of slow-setting
emulsified asphalt, well-graded fine aggregate, and mineral filler with water added to achieve
slurry consistency that is used to fill cracks and seal areas of old pavements, to seal the surface
preventing moisture and air intrusion into the pavement, and to improve skid resistance. Small Deflection Theory - A basis for methods of analysis where the effects of deformation
upon force effects in the structure is neglected. Small Form-Factor Pluggable (SFP) - A compact, hot-pluggable network interface module
used for both telecommunication and data communications applications. Small Network - See Corridor. Small Target Visibility (STV) - A weighted average of the values of target Visibility Level over
a grid of points on an area of roadway for one direction of traffic flow.

chunks.text:
Speed - The rate of travel of a motorized or nonmotorized unit, which is defined as the
distance traveled over a unit of time, typically expressed in km/h or meters per second (m/s). Speed Limit - The maximum (or minimum) speed allowable on a section of highway as
established by law. Speed limits must be posted, as well as established by law to be legal. Speed Limit Sign Beacon - A beacon used to supplement a SPEED LIMIT sign. Speed Measurement Markings - A white transverse pavement marking placed on the
roadway to assist the enforcement of speed regulations. Speed Trap - A way to indirectly measure the spot speed of vehicles by measuring the time it
takes a vehicle to travel a known (short) distance, such as the distance between two closely
spaced magnetic inductance loops or two transverse pavement markings. Speed Zone - A section of highway with a speed limit that is established by law or regulation,
but which might be different from a legislatively specified statutory speed limit. Splash-Over - That portion of frontal flow at a grate that skips or splashes over the grate and
is not intercepted. Split - The time assigned to a phase (green and the greater of the yellow change plus red
clearance or the pedestrian walk plus clearance times) during coordinated operations. May be
expressed in seconds or as a percentage. Splitter Island - A median island used to separate opposing directions of traffic entering and
exiting a roundabout. Spot Speed - The speed of a vehicle as measured at a point location or over a short segment,
as opposed to the travel speeds measured over a segment or longer distance of roadway. Spread - 1. The width of stormwater flow measured laterally from the roadway curb. 2. Average
diameter of the top growth.

Spread Beams - Beams not in physical contact with one another, carrying a cast-in-place
concrete deck. Sprig - A vegetative piece of viable hybrid Bermuda grass plant specifically, a rhizome

repetitive use. Start-Up Lost Time - The additional time consumed by the first few vehicles in a queue whose
headway exceeds the saturation headway because of the need to react to the initiation of the
green interval and accelerate. Measured in seconds. Stated Preference (SP) Surveys - Stated preference (SP) surveys help quantify how people

both desirable and undesirable characteristics. In doing so, they indirectly reveal which parts

chunks.text:
SP surveys are most widely used in transportation to understand
how people will trade off money to gain something less tangible, such as time savings or
reliability. These surveys can also reveal how changes to infrastructure or services will alter
travel behavior. Statutory Speed - A speed limit established by legislative action that typically is applicable
for highways with specified design, functional, jurisdictional and/or location characteristics and
is not necessarily shown on speed limit signs. Statutory Speed Limit - A speed limit established by legislative action that typically is
applicable for a particular class of highways with specified design, functional, jurisdictional
and/or location characteristics and that is not necessarily displayed on Speed Limit signs. Steady (Steady Mode) - The continuous display of a signal indication for the duration of an
interval, signal phase, or consecutive signal phases. Steady-State - The point in a simulated traffic system where conditions have reached a stable
condition and behavior or performance measures no longer change. Steel Slag - The nonmetallic product consisting essentially of calcium silicates and ferrites
combined with fused oxides of iron, aluminum, manganese, calcium and magnesium, that is
developed simultaneously with steel in basic oxygen, electric, or open-hearth furnaces. Stereoplotting - The plotting of a map from aerial photographs using a stereoscopic device. Stiffness - Force effect resulting from a unit deformation. Stochastic - See Model. Stone Mastic (or Matrix) Asphalt SMA - A gap-graded hot mix asphalt designed to increase
deformation (rutting) resistance and durability that can be used for high volume, heavy, slow,
or standing traffic pavement.

139
SHC 101
Stop Beacon - A beacon used to supplement a STOP sign, a DO NOT ENTER sign, or a WRONG
WAY sign. Stop Line/Bar - A solid line marked across all or part of a road, behind which vehicles should
stand when required to stop by traffic signals or regulatory signs. Stop, Far-Side - A transit stop located beyond an intersection. It requires that transit units
(vehicles or trains) cross the intersection before stopping to serve passengers. Stop, In-Lane - A transit stop where transit vehicles run adjacent to the curb, passengers
board and alight directly from the sidewalk. Stop, Near-Side - A transit stop located on the approach side of an intersection. The transit
units (vehicles or trains) stop to serve passengers before crossing the intersection. Stop, Pull-Out - A designated spot on the side of a road where buses or trams may pull out
of the flow of traffic to pick up and drop off passengers. Stop-Bar Detection - Detection located at the stop bar that is generally used to discharge the
queue. Generally, uses non-locking memory mode in combination with large area detection
design, so that vehicles making permitted turns do not extend or call the phase. Stopped Delay - The amount of time that a vehicle is stopped. When calculated from vehicle
trajectories, it is equal to the time step delay on any step in which the vehicle is in a stopped
state. Time step delays accumulated over all time steps in which the vehicle was in the stopped
state represent the stopped delay for that vehicle. Storage Length - The length of turn lane available for storing queued vehicles. Storm Drain - A storm drain is a closed or open conduit that conveys storm water that has
been collected by inlets to an outfall. It generally consists of laterals, connectors, and trunk
lines or mains.

preservation or rehabilitation. Throat Length - The distance from the edge of the traveled way along the main roadway to

machine that produces paper or electronic tickets, or recharges a stored-value card or smart

chunks.text:
Tidal Flow - A means of increasing capacity under conditions of high traffic volume and
marked directional split by means of reversible lanes or carriageways. Tie Down - A combination of securing devices which form an assembly that attaches cargo
to, or restrains cargo on, a vehicle or trailer, and is attached to anchor point(s). Tilling - Reducing soil to fine particles. Time before Reduction - This volume-density timing period begins when the phase is green
and there is a serviceable call on a conflicting phase. When this period is completed, the linear
reduction of the passage time begins until the minimum gap is reached or the phase
terminates due to gap out. See also Time to Reduce. Time Mean Speed - The arithmetic average of speeds of vehicles observed passing a point
on a highway; also referred to as the average spot speed. The individual speeds of vehicles
passing a point are recorded and averaged arithmetically. The time mean speed is always equal
to or higher than the space mean speed. The two are equal only when the speeds of all vehicles
in the traffic stream are equal. Time to Reduce - This volume-density timing period begins when time before reduction ends
and controls the linear rate of reduction until the minimum gap is achieved. See also Time
before Reduction. Timed Exit Gate Operating Mode - A mode of operation where the exit gate descent at a
grade crossing is based on a predetermined time interval. Time-in-Queue Delay - The difference between the time a vehicle joins the end of the queue
at a signalized intersection and the time it takes to cross the intersection. Time-Mean Speed - The basic arithmetic mean of speed collected at a spot location.

construction, that includes a theodolite to measure both vertical and horizontal angles,

instrument to a particular point, and an on-board computer to collect data and perform
calculations. Townscape - The character and composition of the built environment including the buildings
and the relationships between them, different types of urban open space, including green
spaces, and the relationship between buildings and open space (LI – TIN 05/2017). Traffic - Pedestrians, bicyclists, ridden or herded animals, vehicles, streetcars, and other

private road open to public travel. Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ) - A geographic unit of analysis used to aggregate socioeconomic
data (household and employment data). Traffic Barrier - A device used to prevent a vehicle from striking a more severe obstacle or
feature located on the roadside or in the median, or to prevent crossover median accidents. It
can also refer to a temporary device used to prevent vehicular access into construction or
maintenance work zones and to redirect an impacting vehicle so as to minimize damage to
the vehicle and injury to the occupants, while providing worker protection

into several distinct classes based on vehicle or axle configuration.

chunks.text:
Traffic Control - The method of controlling vehicular and pedestrian traffic around a
construction zone or other road disruption, thus ensuring the safety of emergency response
teams, construction workers, and the general public. It uses traffic control devices such as
signs, signals, markings, etc. placed on or adjacent to a street or highway. Traffic Control Device - A sign, signal, marking, or other device used to regulate, warn, or
guide traffic, placed on, over, or adjacent to a street, highway, private road open to public
travel, pedestrian facility, or shared-use path by authority of a public agency or official having
jurisdiction, or, in the case of a private road open to public travel, by authority of the private
owner or private official having jurisdiction. Traffic Control Plan - A plan for handling traffic through a specific highway or street work
zone or project. Traffic Control Signal (Traffic Signal) - Any highway traffic signal by which traffic is
alternately directed to stop and permitted to proceed. 146
SHC 101
Traffic Detector - A safety electro-mechanical device used to detect the presence of any
moving vehicle to send a signal to the traffic control system and prevent any incident. It is
usually used near train rails or at a gate, or door. Traffic Distribution - The distribution of vehicular traffic across the roadway. Traffic Generator - A designated land-use (residential, commercial, office, industrial, etc.) that
generates vehicular or pedestrian traffic to and from the site. Traffic Growth - The growth rate of traffic is defined as the percentage change of traffic
volume within a specific time period that depends on site and social-economic factors.

chunks.text:
Traffic Guidance Scheme - An arrangement of temporary signs and devices to warn traffic
and guide it through or past a works site or temporary hazard. Traffic Impact Study (TIS) - An engineering study that determines the potential impacts the
expected traffic of a proposed traffic generator will have on the surrounding highway network. The study includes a recommendation of highway improvements that may be necessary to
accommodate the additional traffic. A complete analysis includes an estimation of future traffic
with and without the proposed generator, analysis of traffic impacts, and recommended
highway improvements which may be necessary to accommodate the expected traffic. Traffic Index - A measure of the cumulative number of Equivalent Single Axle Loads (ESALs)
expected in the traffic lane over the design life of the pavement structure. Traffic Lane - The portion of a traveled way for movement of a single line of vehicles. Traffic Management Center (TMC) - A centralized data processing and observation unit that
monitors traffic flow and ITS technology deployed on a freeway or arterial network. Traffic Management System (TMS) - The development and application of network-wide
data collection and sharing of traffic information system. The system can integrate data and
control systems from freeways, arterials, and city streets to provide real-time proactive traffic
information and control. Implementation of the system would facilitate congestion
management over the entire network across multijurisdictional boundaries. The system could
provide incident detection, transit and emergency vehicle priority, and advance traveler
information.

chunks.text:
Traffic Sign - A traffic control device mounted on a support above the level of the roadway
that conveys a specific message by means of words or symbols. Traffic Signage - Signs erected at the side of or above roads to give instructions or provide
information to road users. Traffic Signals - A set of automatically operated colored lights, typically red, amber, and
green, for controlling traffic at road junctions, pedestrian crossings, and roundabouts. Traffic Space - Paved area in the highway that includes the traffic lanes, the inner and outer
paved shoulders, and the emergency lane. Traffic Speed Deflectometer - A deflectometer used to measure pavement response to
applied load to identify bearing capacity indices and pavement fatigue life and to proceed in
preventive maintenance at the correct time. 147
SHC 101
Traffic Violation Detectors - A traffic enforcement camera (e.g., a red light camera) which
may be mounted beside or over a road to detect motoring offenses (e.g., vehicles going
through a red traffic light). Traffic Volume - The number of vehicles crossing a section of roadway, or another travel way
per unit time at any selected period, usually an hour. Trailer - A vehicle designed for carrying persons or property and drawn by a motor vehicle
that carries no part of the weight and load of the trailer. Train - One or more locomotives coupled, with or without cars, that operates on rails or tracks
and to which all other traffic must yield the right-of-way by law at highway-rail grade crossings. Trajectory - The precise path of a vehicle through a transportation network, including
pertinent operational data (position, speed, acceleration rate) recorded in frequent time steps
and used in simulation analysis.

that conveys a specific message by means of words or symbols. Traffic Signage - Signs erected at the side of or above roads to give instructions or provide
information to road users. Traffic Signals - A set of automatically operated colored lights, typically red, amber, and
green, for controlling traffic at road junctions, pedestrian crossings, and roundabouts. Traffic Space - Paved area in the highway that includes the traffic lanes, the inner and outer
paved shoulders, and the emergency lane. Traffic Speed Deflectometer - A deflectometer used to measure pavement response to
applied load to identify bearing capacity indices and pavement fatigue life and to proceed in
preventive maintenance at the correct time. 147
SHC 101
Traffic Violation Detectors - A traffic enforcement camera (e.g., a red light camera) which
may be mounted beside or over a road to detect motoring offenses (e.g., vehicles going
through a red traffic light). Traffic Volume - The number of vehicles crossing a section of roadway, or another travel way
per unit time at any selected period, usually an hour. Trailer - A vehicle designed for carrying persons or property and drawn by a motor vehicle
that carries no part of the weight and load of the trailer. Train - One or more locomotives coupled, with or without cars, that operates on rails or tracks
and to which all other traffic must yield the right-of-way by law at highway-rail grade crossings. Trajectory - The precise path of a vehicle through a transportation network, including
pertinent operational data (position, speed, acceleration rate) recorded in frequent time steps
and used in simulation analysis.

chunks.text:
Tramway - A street transit system consisting of electrically powered rail vehicles operating in
single or multiple-unit, mostly on surface streets with mixed traffic. Transboundary Waters - Surface or Groundwater crossing international boundaries. Transient Loads - Loads and forces that can vary over a short time interval relative to the
lifetime of the structure. Transit (Public) - Passenger transportation service, usually local in scope, that is available to
any person who pays a prescribed fare. It operates on established schedules along designated
routes or lines with specific stops and is designed to move relatively large numbers of people
at one time. Examples include bus, light rail, rapid transit. Transit Lane, Curbside - The lane adjacent to the curb can be dedicated to transit vehicles,
especially on through corridors where parking is either not provided or not well utilized. Curbside transit lanes can be implemented with varying levels of separation, increasing service
capacity and allowing riders to board directly from the curb. Transit Lane, Offset - Also known as “floating” or “parking-adjacent” lanes, offset transit lane
place transit vehicles in the rightmost moving lane, but are offset from the curb by street
parking, curb extensions, or raised cycle tracks. Transit Lanes - They are a portion of the street designated for the preferential or exclusive
use of transit vehicles, sometimes permitting limited use by other vehicles. They are often
repurposed from a general traffic lane, designated by striping, signage, and pavement
markings. Transit lanes allow transit vehicles to easily enter and exit the lane to suit their route.

chunks.text:
Transit Signal Priority - A set of tools that modify traffic signal timing or phasing when transit
vehicles are present either conditionally for late runs or unconditionally for all arriving transit. Its purpose is to reduce travel times for transit vehicles by avoiding the need to stop and start
at signalized intersections. Transit Streets - Streets, often running along commercial corridors, that prioritize the street
for pedestrian and transit. Motorists are prohibited beyond limited deliveries and occasional
permitted access. Transition - Connection between vehicle restraint systems of different types, designs and/or
performances, or between vehicle restraint systems and unwavering structures. 148
SHC 101
Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) - In urban planning, transit-oriented development
(TOD) is a type of urban development that maximizes the amount of residential, business and
leisure space within walking distance of public transport. Transitways - On-street transitways are exclusive transit facilities physically s from mixed
traffic highway by medians or other vertical elements. They are often implemented to ensure
reliability of high-frequency/high-capacity transit services such as Bus Rapid Transit (BRT),
Light Rail Transit (LRT) or modern tramways. Transmission Lines - The part of a utility facility connecting its main energy or material
source(s) with its distribution system, to which individual customers usually are not connected. Transplanting - Lifting and replanting a plant. Transportation Demand Management (TDM) - A set of strategies that foster increased
efficiency of the transportation system by influencing travel demand by mode, time of day,
frequency, trip length, regulation, route or cost.

chunks.text:
Transportation Master Plan (TMP) - A comprehensive strategic planning document that
provides a framework and direction for transportation needs in a broad area. A TMP integrates
existing and future land-use planning and the planning of transportation infrastructure and
operations to meet established objectives. A TMP focuses on large area impacts, internal
network operations and integration of all transport modes. Transportation Research Board - Transportation Research Board is an organization
administering research, hosting conferences and publishing journal articles in the
transportation field. Transverse Cracking - A type of pavement distress, defined as predominantly perpendicular
to the direction of traffic cracks resulting primarily from thermal movements, fatigue, loss of
support, and environmental aging. Transverse Markings - Pavement markings that are generally placed perpendicular and
across the flow of traffic such as shoulder markings; word, symbol, and arrow markings; stop
lines; crosswalk lines; speed measurement markings; parking space markings; and others. Travel Time - The time spent by users traversing a highway or highway network. Conventionally, travel time is expressed in minutes. Travel Time (Total) - In transportation planning, the time duration of a linked trip, that is,
from the point of origin to the final destination, including waiting and walking time at transfer
points and trip ends. Travel Time Index - The time required to travel a certain distance. Travel Time Reliability - 1. The probability of “on-time” arrival (i.e., the probability that a trip
is completed below a certain threshold time). 2. The variability in travel time for a given trip
due to unforeseen causes such as variations in demand or an incident.

chunks.text:
Traveled Way - The portion of the roadway for the movement of vehicles, exclusive of the
shoulders, berms, sidewalks, and parking lanes. Travel-Time Delay - The difference between the actual travel time required to traverse a
section of street or highway and the travel time that will be obtained by assuming that a vehicle
traverses the study section at an average speed equal to that for an uncongested traffic flow
149
SHC 101
on the section being studied. It includes acceleration and deceleration delay in addition to
stopped delay. Traverse - A method in the field of surveying to establish control networks by placing survey
stations along a line or path of travel, and then using the previously surveyed points as a base
for observing the next point. Trees - Any tree with a wooden trunk or aerial roots that rises (1.5) one and a half meters or
more from the surface of the earth, whether naturally grown or planted. Trench - 1. An area where soil or rock has been excavated along a route to allow the road to
pass at a lower level than the original ground level. 2. (or Open Cut) - A method of installing
utilities in an open excavation
Trenchless Technology - The use of directional boring, horizontal drilling, tunneling, and
other techniques used in the construction or installation of underground portions of facilities
to minimize disruption and damage to right-of-way. Triangulation - The process of determining the location of a point by forming triangles to the
point from known points involving only angle measurements at known points. Trilateration - The process of determining the location of a point by forming triangles to the
point from known points involving distance measurements at known points.

chunks.text:
Trip - One-way movement of a person or vehicle between two points for a specific purpose;
sometimes called a one-way trip to distinguish it from a round trip. 2. The movement of a
transit unit (vehicle or train) in one direction from the beginning of a route to the end of it;
also known as a run. Trip Assignment - Determines the amount of the proposed development traffic plus off-site
traffic that will use each access point and route in the study area. Trip Distribution - The allocation of the trips generated by the proposed development
between all potential approach and departure routes. Trip Generation - The estimation of the number of trips generated to and from a site resulting
from the land-use activity on that site. Trip or Trip End - A single or one-direction person or vehicle movement with either the origin
or the destination (exiting or entering) inside a study site. In technical terms, a trip has an origin
and a destination at its respective ends (known as trip ends). Each trip end is a part of a trip. For site trip generation, the analyst is usually interested in trips entering and exiting a single
site. Trip, Unlinked - 1. A trip made in a single vehicle. 2. The boarding of one transit vehicle in
revenue service; also known as an unlinked passenger trip. 3. Any segment of a linked trip. Trolleybus - An electrically propelled bus that obtains power via two trolley poles from a dual
(positive and negative) overhead wire system along routes. It may be able to travel a limited
distance using battery power or an auxiliary internal combustion engine. The power-collecting
apparatus is designed to allow the bus to maneuver in mixed traffic over several lanes.

chunks.text:
Truck - Heavy vehicle engaging primarily in the transport of goods and materials or in the
delivery of services other than public transportation. 150
SHC 101
Truck Apron - The mountable portion of the central island adjacent to the circulatory roadway. It is required to accommodate the wheel tracking of long or oversized vehicles
Oversize/Overweight vehicles (OSOW). It is usually concrete with a contrasting color to
delineate the apron from the normal vehicle path. Truck Combination - A truck tractor and a semitrailer, either with or without a full trailer or a
truck with one or more full trailers. Truck Inspection Station - Roadside facility used for vehicle and/or operator assessment,
whereby vehicle weighing and inspection are carried out. Truck Lane - Designated lane for commercial vehicles, but not for public transit vehicles. Truck Only Toll - A facility that is intended exclusively to process heavy vehicles but that
charges a toll for its use. Truck Tractor - A motor vehicle designed for drawing other vehicles, but not for a load other
than a part of the weight of the vehicle and load drawn. Trunk - Main stem of a tree. Trunk Line - A trunk line is the main storm drain line. Lateral lines may be connected in at
inlet structures or access holes. A trunk line is sometimes referred to as a "main."
Tub Section - An open-topped section which is composed of a bottom flange, two inclined
or vertical webs, and top flanges. Tunnel - An enclosure roadway for motor vehicle traffic with vehicle access limited to portals,
regardless of the type of structure or method of construction, that requires, based on the
owner’s determination, special design considerations that may include lighting, ventilation, fire
protection systems, and emergency egress capacity.

chunks.text:
Turf - Grass maintained at a short relatively even length, used as a ground cover or lawn. Turfing - Laying turf on prepared ground. Turning Radius - Minimum radius (or "width") of available space required for that vehicle to
make a circular turn. Two-Lane Highways - Facilities with mostly one lane of travel per direction, with motorists
using passing lanes, turnouts, or the opposing lane (where allowed by regulation and opposing
traffic) to pass slower vehicles. Two-Way STOP-Controlled - The type of traffic control at an intersection where drivers on
the minor street or drivers turning left from the major street wait for a gap in the major-street
traffic to complete a maneuver. Typical Method - Typical layout that is used as a guide to produce a specific traffic
management drawing. Uncoordinated (Or Free Operation) - A traffic signal not operating as part of a coordinated
system of intersections. Free operation can be set by time of day. Uncracked Section - A section in which the concrete is assumed to be fully effective in tension
and compression. 151
SHC 101
Underdrain - A drainage feature such as a perforated or porous-walled pipe that is installed
underground to collect and carry away the underground water to prevent water from
accumulating under the pavement and causing premature subgrade failure. Underpass - 1. A grade separation where the subject highway passes under an intersecting
highway or railroad. 2. A structure whose length and physical configuration do not
substantially limit a driver’s ability to see objects ahead.

chunks.text:
Undersaturated Flow - Traffic flow where (a) the arrival flow rate is lower than the capacity of
a point or segment, (b) no residual queue remains from a prior breakdown of the facility, and
(c) traffic flow is unaffected by downstream conditions. Undiluted Emulsified Asphalt - An emulsion which consists primarily of a paving grade
binder, water, and an emulsifying agent. Undivided Tunnel (Bi-directional Bore) - A structure which consists of a common enclosure
to accommodate traffic flow in both directions. Uniformity Ratio - Maximum uniformity ratios are used to judge the evenness of the light on
the road or sidewalk surface. The most significant uniformity ratios are average to minimum
and maximum to minimum. Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) - A device that is used as emergency power when
there's an interruption to the standard power. Unipole Signs - They are large signs mounted on top of a single pole. Unitized Articles - A number of cargo articles grouped together with sufficient structural
integrity that they can be handled, transported and secured as a single article. Unpaved Road - A road that is not covered with a firm, level surface of asphalt, concrete, etc.,
does not meet the characteristics of a paved road and may consist of dirt, rock, gravel, or other
non-solidified material. Uplight - The percent lamp lumens or the luminaire lumens distributed above a luminaire
between 90 and 180 degrees vertical. Upstream - 1. The direction from which traffic is flowing. 2. A term that refers to a location
that is encountered by traffic prior to a downstream location as it flows in an “upstream to
downstream” direction.

For example, “the upstream end of a lane line separating the turn lane

built-up areas that can occur in cities and metropolitan areas. Urban Area - An area that contains or is zoned to contain built land use development. Urban Street - A street with a relatively high density of driveway and cross-street access,

SHC 101

commodity) line that extends from the main or primary utility facility into an adjacent property
and that is used to serve the property. Utility Standards - Includes all Governmental Requirements; all applicable utility industry
requirements; and all applicable MoTLS KSA and Utility standards, policies, and procedures.

chunks.text:
Utility System - An interdependent group of Facilities forming integral part of a network, or
any portion thereof, specifically for transmission and/or distribution of a utility product (e.g. water, sewer, electricity, gas, telecommunications, etc.), that is or are directly applicable to or
impacts the Utility and/or its customers located within Utility’s service area at any time,
presently or in the future. U-Turn - A turn made on the roadway usually without reversing the vehicle resulting in reversal
of direction of travel. Valet Parking - Service that operates at land uses such as hotels, restaurants, retail
establishments or other businesses, in which customer cars are parked by an attendant. Validation - The process by which the analyst checks the overall model-predicted traffic
performance for a street–road system against field measurements of traffic performance, on
the basis of field data not used in the calibration process. Value of Time (VOT) - The opportunity value attributed to one hour of a user’s time. This
value is different for different types of users and/or trip purposes. It is conventionally expressed
in SAR per hour. Variable Message Signs - Variable message signs can be changed to display information
messages to alert drivers of on-road incidences on the road ahead. They are also used to warn
road users about weather conditions and other important information. Variety - Group of plants that has arisen naturally, that differ in some heritable characteristics
such as form or color from what is regarded as typical of their species. Vegetation Cover - Natural plants, whether grasses, shrubs, or trees. Vegetation Cover Lands - Any state-owned land that contains wild plants and the like.

value is different for different types of users and/or trip purposes. It is conventionally expressed
in SAR per hour. Variable Message Signs - Variable message signs can be changed to display information
messages to alert drivers of on-road incidences on the road ahead. They are also used to warn
road users about weather conditions and other important information. Variety - Group of plants that has arisen naturally, that differ in some heritable characteristics
such as form or color from what is regarded as typical of their species. Vegetation Cover - Natural plants, whether grasses, shrubs, or trees. Vegetation Cover Lands - Any state-owned land that contains wild plants and the like.

chunks.text:
Vehicle Restraint System - Post crash, passive safety systems placed on highway areas aiming
to contain vehicles involved in run-off-road crashes and/or redirect them smoothly to the
highway. Vehicle Swept Path - The difference between a vehicle’s outer trace of the front overhang
and the path of the inner rear wheel. Used to define the boundaries of the turning paths per
design vehicle for its sharpest turns. Vehicle to Anything (Vehicle-to-Everything) - A vehicular communication system that
allows the wireless exchange of information between a vehicle and any entity that may affect,
or may be affected by, the vehicle. It incorporates other more specific types of communication
as V2I (vehicle-to-infrastructure), V2N (vehicle-to-network), V2V (vehicle-to-vehicle), V2P
(vehicle-to-pedestrian), V2D (vehicle-to-device). Its purpose is to improve road safety, energy
savings, and traffic efficiency on the roads. Vehicle to Everything (V2X) - A vehicular communication system that allows the wireless
exchange of information between a vehicle and any entity that may affect, or may be affected
by, the vehicle. It incorporates other more specific types of communication as V2I (vehicle-to-
infrastructure), V2N (vehicle-to-network), V2V (vehicle-to-vehicle), V2P (vehicle-to-
pedestrian), V2D (vehicle-to-device). Its purpose is to improve road safety, energy savings, and
traffic efficiency on the roads
Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) - A communication technology that allows the wireless
exchange of information about safety, mobility, or environment-related conditions between
vehicles and road infrastructure components such as overhead RFID readers and cameras,
traffic lights, lane markers, streetlights, road signs and parking meters.

chunks.text:
Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) - A communication technology that allows the wireless exchange
of information about the speed and position of surrounding vehicles. Its purpose is to improve
road safety, avoid crashes, ease traffic congestion and improve the environment. Veiling Luminance Ratio - Luminance that is superimposed on the retinal image. This
describes how bright the light source is compared to the roadway surface. It is important in
roadway lighting since light source brightness can inhibit one’s ability to see details or objects
on the pavement. Velocity Head - Velocity head is a quantity proportional to the kinetic energy of flowing water
expressed as a height or head of water, (V2/2g). 154
SHC 101
Vertical Clearance - Unobstructed distance from the top surface of a floor to the underside
of the ceiling or ramp or electrical/mechanical system. Viaduct - A specific type of bridge in a long elevated roadway, usually consisting of a series
of short spans supported on arches, piers, or columns. Vibration - A to-and-from motion which oscillates about a fixed equilibrium position. Vibration Sensitive Receptor - Receptors which are potentially sensitive to vibration. Examples include dwellings, hospitals, healthcare facilities, education facilities, community
facilities, buildings containing vibration-sensitive equipment, and cultural heritage assets. Video Analytics - A technology that uses artificial intelligence to complete tasks including
detecting and tracking objects such as vehicles, people, traffic lights, etc., in real-time, and
vehicles or people-counting in crowded areas by applying computer vision and deep learning
to video footage or live video streams.

chunks.text:
Video Camera - A camera that transmits video to a monitor screen that is used to provide
traffic monitoring by the facility operator along the length of the facility and particularly at
points of entry and tolling locations. Virtual Phone-based Systems (Applications) - Virtual phone-based systems are also known
as “ticketless systems”. This is a payment by mobile phone that can be added to many
electronic parking pricing systems. The mobile acts as a meter recording the commencement
time and counts down the duration of stay according to the payment made. Mobile phone-
based applications allow drivers to top-up payment to extend their duration of stay which is
beneficial for unexpected or unpredicted situations where the drivers require more time for
parking. This would only be allowed where the top-up does not exceed any time restrictions. The system can alert drivers of the expiry of their parking by phone call or SMS. Virtual Weigh Station - An enforcement facility that does not require continuous staffing and
is monitored from another location. Viscosity - A measure of the resistance to flow of a liquid, such as an asphalt, measured in
poises by direct tests using a viscometer or by indirect procedures and defined as the ratio of
applied shear stress to applied shear strain. Visibility - 1. The quality or state of being perceivable by the eye. Visibility may be defined in
terms of the distance at which an object can be just perceived by the eye or it may be defined
in terms of the contrast or size of a standard test object, observed under standardized view-
conditions, having the same threshold as the given object. 2.

Limited Signal Face or Visibility-
Limited Signal Section—a type of signal face or signal section designed (or shielded, hooded,
or louvered) to restrict the visibility of a signal indication from the side to a certain lane or
lanes, or to a certain distance from the stop line. Visual Amenity - The overall pleasantness of the views people enjoy of their surroundings,
which provide an attractive visual setting or backdrop for the enjoyment of activities of the
people living, working, recreating, visiting or travelling through an area (LI - TIN 05/2017). Visual Envelope - An area from which the scheme can be visible. Visual Inspection - Inspection, used in pavement maintenance, that aims to identify pavement
repair requirements (e.g., repairing of surface cracks and potholes) using either or all of the
155
SHC 101
raw human senses such as vision, hearing, touch, and smell, and/or any non-specialized
inspection equipment. Visual Receptor - Individuals and/or defined groups of people who potentially could be
affected by a project. Visual Sensitivity - Visual experience be it sensitivity to light or visual clutter. Visualization - The process of presenting data in image form that allows rapid understanding
of relationships and findings that are not readily evident from raw data. Voids Filled with Asphalt - The percentage portion of the volume of intergranular void space
between the aggregate particles (i.e. VMA) that is occupied by the effective asphalt binder
volume. It is expressed as the ratio: VFA = [(VMA-Va)/VMA] x 100, where Va is the volume of
air voids and VMA is the percent voids in mineral aggregate.

chunks.text:
Voids in Mineral Aggregate - The volume of intergranular void space between the aggregate
particles of a compacted paving mixture that includes the air voids and the effective asphalt
content, expressed as a percent of the total volume of the sample. Volts - Alternating Current (VAC) - A measure of circuit power pressure for alternating
current. Volts - Direct Current (VDC) - A measure of circuit power pressure for direct current. Volume - The number of vehicles, pedestrians, bicycles, heavy vehicles, or other user class of
interest that are observed passing a point. Volume is often expressed as volume per hour, in
which case a specified period of time would be used to calculate a rate. Volume-To-Capacity (V/C) Ratio - The ratio of flow rate to capacity for a system element. Vulnerable Road User - Road users who are most likely to suffer serious injuries in a collision,
including pedestrians, cyclists, motorcyclists and wheelchair users. Walk Interval - An interval during which the WALKING PERSON (symbolizing WALK) signal
indication is displayed. Walking Profiler - A high-precision measurement instrument used to collect pavement
surface condition information (pavement profile) at true walking speed and determine the
International Roughness Index (IRI), the Mean Profile Depth (MPD) texture (as an optional
parameter), and distance. Warning Beacon - A beacon used only to supplement an appropriate warning or regulatory
sign or marker. Warning Light - A portable, powered, yellow, lens-directed, enclosed light that is used in a
temporary traffic control zone in either a steady burn or a flashing mode. Warning Sign - A sign that gives notice to road users of a situation that might not be readily
apparent.

chunks.text:
Warrant - A warrant describes a threshold condition based upon average or normal conditions
that, if found to be satisfied as part of an engineering study, shall result in analysis of other
traffic conditions or factors to determine whether a traffic control device or other improvement
is justified. Warrants are not a substitute for engineering judgment. The fact that a warrant for
a particular traffic control device is met is not conclusive justification for the installation of the
device. 156
SHC 101
Waste - Any substance or object which the holder disposes or intends / is required to dispose. Waste Management - Regulating any activity or practice related to waste, such as collecting,
transporting, sorting, storing, treating, recycling, importing, exporting, and safely disposing of
it, including aftercare at waste disposal sites. Waterbodies - Accumulation of water on the earth’s surface or underground; including
oceans, seas, rivers, lakes, ponds, wetlands and other geographical components in which water
is transported from one place to another. Water-Cement Ratio - The ratio of mixing water to cement in a concrete mixture expressed
by volume or by weight, calculated in order to determine the strength and durability of the
concrete when it is cured properly. Wayside Horn System - A stationary horn (or series of horns) located at a grade crossing that
is used in conjunction with train-activated or light rail transit-activated warning systems to
provide audible warning of approaching rail traffic to road users on the highway or pathway
approaches to a grade crossing, either as a supplement or alternative to the sounding of a
locomotive horn.

chunks.text:
Weathering - Also referred to as raveling, is a type of distress in asphalt pavements that is
defined as the loss of pavement surface material due to displacement of the aggregate
particles and deterioration of the asphalt binder. Weaving Segment (Freeway) - Freeway segments in which two or more traffic streams
traveling in the same general direction cross paths along a significant length of freeway
without the aid of traffic control devices (except for guide signs). Weeds - An undesired, uncultivated plant growing in a manner so as to adversely compete
with desirable plants for water, light and nutrients, or destroy aesthetic qualities of a lawn. Weephole - A hole through an abutment or retaining wall to relieve hydrostatic pressure from
groundwater. Weigh House - See Scale house. Weigh in Motion System - A system of sensors and supporting instruments designed to
capture and record the axle weights and gross vehicle weights as vehicles drive over a
measurement site. WIM System can estimate tire loads, speed, axle spacing, and vehicle class
according to axle arrangement and other parameters concerning the vehicle. Weigh Station - See Truck Inspection Station. Weighted Mean Annual Pavement Temperature - A method used in flexible pavement
design for determining an asphalt design temperature that affects extensively the strength
and stiffness of asphalt, using Weighted Mean Annual Air Temperatures (WMAAT) and does
not account for contributing factors including asphalt thickness greater than 100 mm and
variations to mix composition. Wetlands - Lands which are naturally or artificially, permanently or temporarily covered with
stagnant or running water, freshwater, or brackish water. These areas have special biological
systems and attract migratory birds.

pathway right-of-way, and law enforcement personnel when directing traffic, investigating

restraint system during normal service, usually assigned by the manufacturer of the

restraint system. Works Area - The area of Right or Left of Way that is occupied by the actual construction or
maintenance operation. Yellow Change Interval (Y) - This interval follows the green interval. It is used to warn drivers
of the impending red indication. A yellow indication is displayed for this duration. Measured
in seconds. 158
SHC 101
Yellow Trap - A condition that leads the left-turning user into the intersection believing the
opposing user is seeing a yellow.

restraint system. Works Area - The area of Right or Left of Way that is occupied by the actual construction or
maintenance operation. Yellow Change Interval (Y) - This interval follows the green interval. It is used to warn drivers
of the impending red indication. A yellow indication is displayed for this duration. Measured
in seconds. 158
SHC 101
Yellow Trap - A condition that leads the left-turning user into the intersection believing the
opposing user is seeing a yellow.
qa_hints: What does hour. mean?
Explain hour. in simple words.
What are the rules about road signs?

chunks.text:
Yield Line - A row of solid white isosceles triangles pointing toward approaching vehicles
extending across approach lanes to indicate the point at which the yield is intended or required
to be made. Yield Line Method - A method of analysis in which a number of possible yield line patterns
are examined in order to determine load-carrying capacity. Yield Point - The earliest point in a coordinated signal operation that the controller can decide
to terminate the coordinated phase(s). Zebra Marking - A pedestrian crossing marked with white stripes. Zone of Theoretical Visibility - Map produced (usually digitally) to specific criteria to illustrate
the area(s) from which a project can theoretically be visual. 159
SHC 101

6. Referenced Standards and Codes
Standard and Codes referenced in SHC Volumes are specified in the following:
•
3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP). •
Aerospace Material Specification (AMS). •
American Concrete Institute Standards (ACI). •
American National Standards Institute (ANSI). •
American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM). •
American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME). •
American Welding Society (AWS). •
Asphalt Recycling & Reclaiming Association (ARRA). •
Australian Standards Information and Compliance Organization (SAI). •
British Standards Institution (BSI). •
Economic and Social Council Economic Commission for Europe (ECE). •
EN (European Norm). •
EU Directives. •
European Standards (CEN, ETSI). •
Federal Lands Highway (FLH). •
Federal Specification. •
GCC Standardization Organization (GSO). •
German Institute for Standardization (DIN). •
Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers Standard (IEEE). •
International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC). •
International Organization for Standardization (ISO). •
International Slurry Surfacing Association (ISSA). •
Italian Electrotechnical Committee (CEI). •
Materials and Research Department Test Method (MRDTM). •
National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA). •
National Transportation Communications for Intelligent Transportation System
Protocol (NTCIP). •
SASO Standards & Technical Regulations. •
Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE). •
Standard Specifications for Transportation Materials and Methods of Sampling and
Testing (AASHTO). •
Telecommunications Industry Association's (TIA). •
UN Regulations. •
Underwriters Laboratories (UL). All referenced standards are included and briefly described in SHC 309 (Material Specification
and Standardized Testing).
